name: Documentation Home
title: Documentation Home
description: Entry point to the AI evaluation platform documentation. Provides an overview of key sections, global search, and an API/docs version selector that controls which version of the documentation is displayed.

core_features: Load and display documentation overview sections using getDocsHomeOverview, including section titles, descriptions, and navigation targets for core areas such as getting started, API reference, concepts, guides, examples, playground, and SDKs.
Load available documentation/API versions using getAvailableDocsVersions and render a version selector that reflects the current version from siteSettings and DocsVersion state.
Allow users to switch the active documentation/API version via the version selector by calling switchDocsVersion, updating the currentDocsVersionId and synchronizing it with the URL parameters.
Provide a global search input in the header that constructs navigation to the search_results.html page with a q URL parameter based on the user’s query.

Expose navigation from the overview sections and header/footer links to other documentation pages (getting started, API reference, concepts, guides, examples, playground, SDKs, about, support) using standard link navigation with appropriate URL parameters where applicable.
user_workflows:
Discover documentation structure on first visit:
1. User navigates to the Documentation Home via the main site entry point or a link.
2. On page load, the docs-home-initializer component invokes getDocsHomeOverview to retrieve siteSettings (including currentDocsVersionId) and overviewSections.
3. In parallel or immediately after, docs-home-initializer invokes getAvailableDocsVersions to retrieve all selectable DocsVersion entries.
4. The docs-version-selector component uses the returned DocsVersion list and siteSettings.currentDocsVersionId to display the current version and available alternatives.
5. The docs-overview-section-list component uses overviewSections to render clickable items for each key documentation area.
6. The user scans the list of overview sections and clicks the relevant section item, triggering navigation to the corresponding documentation page.

Switch documentation/API version from the home page:
1. User opens the Documentation Home, optionally with a version URL parameter present (e.g., index.html?version=<id>).
2. On page load, docs-home-initializer reads the version parameter from the URL.
3. If the version URL parameter is present and differs from siteSettings.currentDocsVersionId returned by getDocsHomeOverview, docs-home-initializer calls switchDocsVersion with that parameter value and updates the local currentDocsVersionId state to the returned value.
4. After switchDocsVersion completes, docs-home-initializer may re-call getDocsHomeOverview so that overviewSections reflect the newly active docs version.
5. The docs-version-selector component updates its selected option to match the new currentDocsVersionId.
6. When the user manually selects a different version from the docs-version-selector, it calls switchDocsVersion with the chosen docsVersionId.
7. On success, docs-version-selector updates the URL’s version parameter (e.g., via history.pushState) to the new docsVersionId and triggers a refresh or notifies docs-home-initializer to re-run getDocsHomeOverview so that the overview content is aligned with the selected version.

Use global search from Documentation Home:
1. User focuses the global search input within the global-search-form component in the header.
2. As the user types, the component updates its internal query state but does not call any search APIs on this page.
3. When the user submits the form (e.g., presses Enter or clicks a submit button), global-search-form URL-encodes the current query string and navigates the browser to search_results.html with a q URL parameter set to the query (e.g., search_results.html?q=<encoded-query>).
4. The search_results.html page (not part of this design) then uses its own interfaces to resolve the query into results.

Navigate between core doc sections using overview cards and header/footer links:
1. From the Documentation Home, the docs-overview-section-list component renders a list of overviewSections, each including a title, description, and targetDocPageId.
2. When the user clicks a section item, the component maps that section to the appropriate page URL (e.g., getting_started.html, api_reference.html, etc.) based on routing configuration and initiates navigation.
3. Alternatively, the user can use header-navigation or footer-navigation links, which are bound to NavigationLink entries or static routes provided by the server, to navigate directly to getting started, API reference, concepts, guides, examples, playground, SDKs, about, or support pages.
4. The browser loads the target HTML page, which then uses its own interfaces and data models.
interactions: On page load, docs-home-initializer calls getDocsHomeOverview with no parameters, receives siteSettings and overviewSections, and populates shared state for other components.
On page load, docs-home-initializer calls getAvailableDocsVersions with no parameters and passes the resulting DocsVersion list to docs-version-selector.

When a user changes the selected documentation/API version in docs-version-selector, the component calls switchDocsVersion(docsVersionId), updates the local representation of currentDocsVersionId based on the response, updates the version URL parameter, and triggers a refresh of overviewSections via getDocsHomeOverview.
When a user types into the global-search-form input, the component updates an internal query state but performs no API calls on this page.
When a user submits the global-search-form, the component constructs a navigation to search_results.html with the q URL parameter set to the submitted query value.

When a user clicks an overview section item in docs-overview-section-list, the component navigates to the corresponding documentation page URL, using the section’s targetDocPageId and routing configuration to select the correct HTML target.

When a user clicks a link in header-navigation or footer-navigation, the browser navigates to the appropriate documentation page (e.g., getting started, API reference, concepts, guides, examples, playground, SDKs, about, support) using standard anchor navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Documentation Home page maintains its functional state using the currentDocsVersionId from SiteSettings, the list of DocsVersion entries, and optional URL parameters, without using localStorage. On initial load, the page reads the currentDocsVersionId from the siteSettings portion of the getDocsHomeOverview response. If the current URL includes a version parameter (index.html?version=<docsVersionId>), docs-home-initializer compares this parameter to siteSettings.currentDocsVersionId; if they differ, it calls switchDocsVersion with the version parameter to align the backend SiteSettings with the URL. After a successful switch, the initializer re-fetches overview data via getDocsHomeOverview so overviewSections correspond to the active version. When the user changes the version via docs-version-selector, the component calls switchDocsVersion with the selected docsVersionId and, upon success, updates the URL’s version parameter to match, ensuring that reloading or sharing the URL keeps the same active docs version. The global search form does not maintain long-lived state on this page; instead, on submit, it redirects to search_results.html with a q URL parameter (search_results.html?q=<encoded-query>) representing the user’s search term. All navigation to other documentation pages from the overview cards or header/footer links relies on URL paths and parameters, not on client-side storage, so that browser reloads or direct links remain consistent with the user’s navigational choices.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data fetching and state setup for the Documentation Home page. On page load, it invokes getDocsHomeOverview to retrieve siteSettings and overviewSections, and getAvailableDocsVersions to retrieve DocsVersion entries. It also reads the version URL parameter and, if necessary, calls switchDocsVersion to sync the active docs version between the URL and SiteSettings, then exposes the resulting state to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onVersionParamDetected
onVersionSwitchComplete

components.functionality: Renders the top navigation bar for the documentation site. Displays a link to the Documentation Home, primary section links (e.g., getting started, API reference, concepts, guides, examples, playground, SDKs), and hosts the global-search-form and docs-version-selector subcomponents. It relies on navigation configuration (e.g., NavigationLink data or static routing) provided by the server and does not call any client-side interfaces directly.
event_handlers: onNavLinkClick

components.functionality: Provides a global search input in the header. Maintains an internal searchQuery state as the user types. On form submission, it constructs a navigation to search_results.html with a q URL parameter set to the current searchQuery value, delegating actual search behavior to the Search Results page.
event_handlers: onInputChange
onSubmit

components.functionality: Displays a control (e.g., dropdown) for selecting the active documentation/API version. It receives the list of DocsVersion entries from docs-home-initializer and highlights the entry corresponding to SiteSettings.currentDocsVersionId. When the user selects a different version, it calls switchDocsVersion with the selected docsVersionId, updates the currentDocsVersionId state from the response, updates the version URL parameter, and triggers a refresh of overview data via docs-home-initializer.
event_handlers: onVersionChange
onVersionSwitchSuccess
onVersionSwitchError

components.functionality: Displays the currently active docs/API version in a non-editable text area near the top of the Documentation Home content. It reads currentDocsVersionId from SiteSettings provided by docs-home-initializer and updates whenever the active version changes, helping users confirm which version of the documentation they are viewing.
event_handlers: onSiteSettingsUpdate

components.functionality: Renders the main list/grid of documentation overview sections on the home page. It consumes overviewSections from getDocsHomeOverview, displaying each section’s title and description. When a user clicks an item, it navigates to the target documentation page using the section’s targetDocPageId and the site’s routing configuration to determine the appropriate HTML file (e.g., getting_started.html, api_reference.html, concepts.html, guides.html, examples.html, playground.html, sdk_javascript.html).
event_handlers: onSectionClick

components.functionality: Renders footer-level navigation links for the documentation site. Includes links back to the Documentation Home and to informational/support pages such as about and support. Clicking these links triggers standard browser navigation to the corresponding HTML pages (e.g., about.html, support.html).
event_handlers: onFooterLinkClick

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the documentation layout pattern from the mockup: a fixed global header at the very top, followed by a three-column content area (left sidebar for navigation, central column for the Documentation Home content, and right sidebar for contextual meta like the version indicator). Within the central column, the vertical flow is: page initializer/wrapper (defining the docs grid and H1 context), then the prominent global search form as the main entry interaction, followed closely by the docs version selector control, and finally the overview section grid presenting key documentation areas. The footer navigation spans the full width at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the documentation layout pattern from the mockup: a fixed global header at the very top, followed by a three-column content area (left sidebar for navigation, central column for the Documentation Home content, and right sidebar for contextual meta like the version indicator). Within the central column, the vertical flow is: page initializer/wrapper (defining the docs grid and H1 context), then the prominent global search form as the main entry interaction, followed closely by the docs version selector control, and finally the overview section grid presenting key documentation areas. The footer navigation spans the full width at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Adopt a 12-column grid where the left sidebar occupies roughly 20–25% of the viewport width, the central content column 50–55%, and the right sidebar 20–25%. The header-navigation and footer-navigation span 100% width with centered content constrained to the same max-width as the central column. Within the central column, the global search and version selector occupy the full useful width of that column (up to a comfortable max-width), while the overview section list uses a multi-column grid inside that same central band.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: generous line heights and 24–40px vertical gaps between major elements in the central column (search, version selector, overview grid), while navigation/meta elements (header nav items, sidebar items, footer links) are more compact but still padded enough to be easily scannable. Cards in the overview grid have internal padding around 16–24px and clear separation between them. No elements touch or crowd; every section has breathing room consistent with the 8px-based spacing scale.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the Z-pattern from the mockup: the eye starts at the header-navigation (logo left, header search center, CTAs right), then drops diagonally into the left sidebar (global docs navigation, assumed framework), then across into the central H1 context and global-search-form as the primary action. From there, attention moves down through the version selector and into the overview cards. The right sidebar, with the docs-version-indicator, provides a secondary reference point on the right, supporting a subtle left-to-right flow between the central content and contextual version status as users scan and scroll.

layout.overall_description: The Documentation Home page sits within a modern three-column documentation shell. A fixed, full-width header bars the top of the viewport with a light background and subtle shadow, containing the product logo on the left, a compact header search centered, and key CTAs on the right, establishing the classic Z-pattern entry. Below it, the main content area is organized into a 12-column grid: a left sidebar reserved for documentation navigation, a wider central column for the home content, and a narrower right sidebar for contextual metadata. Within the central column, the page opens with a clear documentation title context managed by the initializer, immediately followed by a large, inviting global search form that visually anchors the page. Just beneath the search, a neatly aligned version selector control lets users choose the docs/API version, while the right sidebar surfaces the current version indicator in a small, sticky meta panel near the top. Below this hero area, the central column unfolds into a spacious overview grid of cards, each representing a major documentation section, arranged in two or three columns depending on width but always within the central band. Generous vertical spacing separates these blocks, maintaining a calm, readable rhythm. The layout finishes with a full-width footer navigation band that mirrors the header’s width and alignment, providing secondary links and closing the page with a balanced, minimal structure that feels like a professional SaaS/DevTools documentation experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The docs-home-initializer functions as the structural wrapper for the Documentation Home content, occupying the entire central content region between the fixed header-navigation at the top and the footer-navigation at the bottom. It establishes the three-column documentation grid within the viewport: a left sidebar column (about 3 of 12 grid columns, 20–25% width), a central content column (about 6 of 12 columns, 50–55% width), and a right sidebar column (about 3 of 12 columns, 20–25% width). Within this grid, it anchors the central page context (H1, intro copy) at the top of the central column, with approximately 32–48px of vertical offset below the bottom edge of the fixed header. The global-search-form, docs-version-selector, and docs-overview-section-list are all placed linearly below this context in the central column, while the docs-version-indicator is positioned in the right column. The initializer itself does not render visible chrome; instead, it provides the layout container, ensures consistent max-width for the central content (e.g., around 960–1120px), and applies the base spacing system that creates generous gaps between the stacked components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, full-width layout container for the content area beneath the fixed header; defines the responsive three-column grid and central column max-width, but does not scroll independently from the page (it scrolls with the document).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation sits fixed at the very top of the viewport, spanning the full browser width. It has a modest vertical height (roughly 64–72px) with 12–16px of vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding inside. Within it, content is centered in a max-width container that aligns with the main central column below. Using an implicit three-part horizontal division, the left third accommodates the logo and product name; the center third holds a compact header-level search input that spans about 50–60% of the header’s central area; and the right third contains utility links and a primary CTA button, aligned to the right edge. The background is a solid light color with a subtle bottom border or shadow to visually separate it from the content below. All subsequent page content starts below this bar, with enough top margin (equal to the header height plus about 16px) so that the central content does not sit underneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport, remaining visible during scroll; the rest of the layout scrolls beneath it. The header’s content is horizontally responsive, maintaining alignment with the central column’s max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-search-form is positioned near the top of the central column, immediately visible once users look past the header and left navigation. It sits below the central H1/intro text defined by the initializer, with about 24–32px of vertical space separating the intro copy and the search. The form is horizontally centered within the central column and spans roughly 80–100% of that column’s width, up to a max width around 640–720px so it feels substantial but not edge-to-edge. The search input itself is a prominent horizontal bar with comfortable 12–16px internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, emphasizing its role as the main interaction on the page. There is about 16–24px of breathing room above and below the search bar before adjacent elements (version selector row below, intro content above), consistent with the spacious documentation style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, scrolling naturally with the page. It maintains its relative position directly beneath the introductory content and above the version selector, without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The docs-version-selector is placed directly beneath the global-search-form within the central column, forming a compact control row that feels connected to the search but visually separate. There is a vertical gap of about 16–24px between the bottom of the search form and the top of the selector. Horizontally, the selector aligns to the left edge of the search input, occupying a fixed-width area (roughly 220–260px, or about 25–30% of the central column) so it reads as a self-contained dropdown or segmented control. It may sit on a single-line row with optional explanatory text to its right if needed; otherwise, it stands alone as a neatly padded pill or field with 8–12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal internal padding. Below the selector, there is another 24–32px of vertical space before the docs-overview-section-list begins, keeping the controls distinctly separated from the grid of section cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, scrolling with the content. It maintains a tight coupling to the global-search-form above and serves as a control that affects what content appears in the rest of the page, but it does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The docs-version-indicator occupies the upper part of the right sidebar column, aligning vertically with the global-search-form and version selector in the central column. It is rendered as a small, lightweight card or label block that spans most of the right column’s width (about 70–90% of that column, leaving a slim gutter on either side). Positioned roughly 32–40px below the bottom edge of the fixed header, it presents the current docs/API version as concise text and possibly a colored status pill (e.g., “Latest” or “Stable”). The block has internal padding around 12–16px and sits flush with the central content’s vertical rhythm but visually separated from it by the column gutter. This makes the current version highly glanceable without competing with the main search and overview content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right sidebar: as the page scrolls, the version indicator remains pinned near the top of the right column, typically offset by the height of the fixed header plus about 16px, so it stays visible while the central content scrolls alongside it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The docs-overview-section-list occupies the main bulk of the central column beneath the version selector. It begins after a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px below the docs-version-selector, reinforcing a clear transition from controls to content. Within the central column, it spans the full usable width (minus standard side padding) and arranges overview items in a responsive grid, typically two cards per row at this viewport. Each card is roughly 48–50% of the central column width, with 24px horizontal gutters between them and around 24–32px vertical spacing between rows. Individual cards have internal padding of 16–24px and a light border or subtle shadow, containing a section title, a short description, and possibly a small list of representative links. The grid extends downward as needed, forming a vertically scrollable area that defines the primary reading and exploration experience of the home page. Generous whitespace at the bottom (around 40–48px) separates the last row of cards from the footer-navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, forming the main scrollable body of the page. It reflows responsively as the viewport changes width but always stays confined to the central column defined by the initializer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-navigation forms a full-width horizontal band at the bottom of the page, appearing after the docs-overview-section-list once the user scrolls down. There is a generous vertical margin (around 40–56px) between the last row of overview cards and the top of the footer, creating a clear end to the main content. Inside the footer, content is centered in the same max-width container used by the header and central column, with 24–32px of vertical padding and 24px of horizontal padding. The layout typically includes a left-aligned area for a small logo or product name and one or two columns of navigation links (e.g., Product, Company, Legal) arranged in a low-density vertical or multi-column list. Typography is slightly smaller and more subdued than the main content, signaling its secondary, supportive role. The footer spans 100% width but feels visually anchored to the core layout via its aligned internal container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the document flow, revealed as the user scrolls past the central content. It is not fixed; it remains at the natural end of the page content.

name: Getting Started
title: Getting Started
description: Documentation page that initializes getting-started content, renders a dynamic sidebar and topic list, and routes users to specific quickstart pages using data from getGettingStartedPageContent.

core_features: Fetch the Getting Started page metadata, sidebar items, and getting-started topics using getGettingStartedPageContent on initial page load.
Render the page title and summary from the returned DocPage so users understand the purpose and scope of the Getting Started section.
Display a Getting Started sidebar navigation built from SidebarItem data, including entries that link to quickstart pages such as the text classification quickstart.
Display a main list of getting-started topics using gettingStartedTopics, each with a title, summary, and navigation to the corresponding documentation page via targetDocPageId.
Support navigation back to the documentation home (index.html) via a breadcrumb or logo click, without using additional APIs.
Support deep-linking to a specific Getting Started topic by reading a URL parameter and highlighting or focusing that topic in the list.
user_workflows: Landing on Getting Started from header navigation: 1) User clicks the global 'Getting started' navigation link in the header. 2) Browser navigates to getting_started.html. 3) On load, the page calls getGettingStartedPageContent. 4) Once data is returned, the page displays the Getting Started title and description, populates the sidebar with sidebarItems, and shows the list of gettingStartedTopics in the main content area.

Opening a topic (e.g., Text Classification Quickstart) from the sidebar: 1) User scans the Getting Started sidebar rendered from sidebarItems. 2) User clicks a sidebar item. 3) The click handler reads the SidebarItem.targetPageId and asks the router to navigate to the corresponding documentation page (for the item whose targetPageId corresponds to the text classification quickstart, this results in navigation to quickstart_text_classification.html). 4) The browser loads the quickstart page with any required URL parameters.

Opening a topic from the main topic list: 1) User scrolls through the gettingStartedTopics list in the main content. 2) User clicks a topic entry. 3) The click handler reads that topic's targetDocPageId. 4) The handler first updates the current page URL with ?topicId=<targetDocPageId> (for deep-linking purposes) using the History API, then triggers navigation to the documentation page for that targetDocPageId (e.g., the text classification quickstart).

Deep-linking to a specific Getting Started topic: 1) User opens a shared link to getting_started.html that includes a topicId query parameter (e.g., getting_started.html?topicId=<some-doc-page-id>). 2) On load, the URL state controller parses the topicId from the URL. 3) After getGettingStartedPageContent resolves, the topic list component compares the topicId from the URL to the targetDocPageId of each topic and marks the matching topic as selected/highlighted. 4) The page optionally scrolls that topic into view so the user immediately sees the relevant entry.

Returning to documentation home: 1) User clicks the breadcrumb link for the docs home or the site logo in the header. 2) The click handler prevents any additional API calls and directly navigates to index.html. 3) The browser loads the documentation home page.
interactions: On page load, the page loader component calls getGettingStartedPageContent with no parameters, sets a loading state, and handles success or error states.
Clicking the documentation home breadcrumb or logo triggers a navigation event to index.html without modifying URL parameters.

Clicking any sidebar item triggers a navigation event based on that SidebarItem's targetPageId, relying on the router to map the ID to the correct documentation URL (including quickstart_text_classification.html for the corresponding quickstart item).

Clicking a topic in the main getting-started topic list triggers two actions: updating the current URL with ?topicId=<topic.targetDocPageId> (without reloading the page) and then navigating to the doc page resolved from that targetDocPageId.

Clicking a secondary action (e.g., 'copy link' control) for a topic updates the current URL to include topicId=<topic.targetDocPageId> without leaving the page, and copies the full URL to the clipboard so it can be shared as a deep link.

Changing the browser URL manually (e.g., pasting a link with a different topicId) and reloading the page causes the URL state controller to pick up the new topicId, which the topic list uses to adjust which topic is highlighted when data is loaded.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Getting Started page uses only URL parameters (no localStorage) to manage client-side state that needs to be restorable or shareable.

On initial load, the page reads window.location.search and parses a single supported parameter: topicId. This parameter is expected to hold a value corresponding to a gettingStartedTopics[n].targetDocPageId. If topicId is present, it is stored in an in-memory state variable such as selectedTopicId.

The page then calls getGettingStartedPageContent. Once the data is available, the topic list component compares selectedTopicId to each topic's targetDocPageId:
- If a match is found, that topic is marked as selected/highlighted and optionally scrolled into view.
- If no match is found or topicId is absent, no topic is pre-selected.

When a user clicks a topic in the list without immediately navigating away (for example, to copy a shareable link), the topic list component calls history.replaceState (or pushState) to update the browser URL to getting_started.html?topicId=<topic.targetDocPageId>. This ensures that the current topic selection can be restored on reload or shared with others.

Sidebar selection state relies on the current page URL rather than extra storage. When a sidebar item is clicked and navigation occurs to another document (such as quickstart_text_classification.html), the browser clears the current page state because a full navigation occurs.

The page does not maintain any client state in localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies; all restorable state is derived solely from URL parameters and the dynamic data returned by getGettingStartedPageContent.

components.functionality: Initializes the Getting Started page by calling getGettingStartedPageContent on mount, managing loading and error states, and passing the resulting docPage, sidebarItems, and gettingStartedTopics to child components.
event_handlers: onLoad
onLoadSuccess
onLoadError

components.functionality: Parses and manages URL-based state for the page, specifically the topicId query parameter, exposes the currently selectedTopicId to other components, and updates the URL when topic selection changes.
event_handlers: onUrlInit
onUrlChange
onSelectedTopicChange

components.functionality: Renders the global documentation header for this page, ensures that the Getting Started navigation link is marked as active based on the current DocPage, and handles navigation when the logo or header links are clicked.
event_handlers: onLogoClick
onNavLinkClick

components.functionality: Displays a breadcrumb trail for the Getting Started page using docPage data, including a clickable Documentation Home crumb that navigates to index.html and a non-clickable current-page crumb.
event_handlers: onHomeCrumbClick

components.functionality: Renders the Getting Started section sidebar using sidebarItems, orders items by their order field, indicates which item corresponds to the current DocPage when applicable, and handles navigation when items are clicked.
event_handlers: onSidebarItemClick
onSidebarToggle

components.functionality: Displays the main Getting Started content including the page title and description from docPage and embeds the topic list component that shows getting-started topics and routes users to more detailed quickstart or guide pages.
event_handlers: onContentReady

components.functionality: Renders the list of getting-started topics using gettingStartedTopics from getGettingStartedPageContent, highlights the topic whose targetDocPageId matches the selectedTopicId provided by the URL state controller, and provides actions to navigate to the topic's documentation page or update/share a deep link to that topic.
event_handlers: onTopicClick
onTopicLinkCopy
onTopicHighlightChange

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a three-region documentation layout: a fixed global header spanning the full width at the very top; beneath it, a horizontal split into a left navigation sidebar, a central reading column, and a right auxiliary column. In the central column the vertical order is: breadcrumb at the very top, then the page category label (e.g., “Guides”), then the large H1 page title (“Getting Started”), a short one-line subtitle, a multi-line introductory paragraph, and optionally a full-width illustrative media block (screenshot or diagram). The left sidebar stacks documentation sections and nested links vertically, while the right column is dedicated to the getting-started topic list, laid out as a TOC-like vertical list of quickstart entries.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a three-region documentation layout: a fixed global header spanning the full width at the very top; beneath it, a horizontal split into a left navigation sidebar, a central reading column, and a right auxiliary column. In the central column the vertical order is: breadcrumb at the very top, then the page category label (e.g., “Guides”), then the large H1 page title (“Getting Started”), a short one-line subtitle, a multi-line introductory paragraph, and optionally a full-width illustrative media block (screenshot or diagram). The left sidebar stacks documentation sections and nested links vertically, while the right column is dedicated to the getting-started topic list, laid out as a TOC-like vertical list of quickstart entries.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the fixed header, the content area uses an implied 12‑column grid. Approximately 22–24% of the width is reserved for the left sidebar (about 3 columns), 52–56% for the central content (about 6–7 columns), and 20–24% for the right column (about 2–3 columns) at typical desktop widths. The header takes a modest vertical height (~64–72px including padding). Within the central column, generous top and inter-block spacing (24–40px) separates the breadcrumb, label, H1, subtitle, introductory text, and any media. The left sidebar is fixed width and vertically fills the viewport below the header; the right topic list column is narrower and aligned to the top of the main content block.
layout_strategies.content_density: The central reading column is low-density with generous line-height and 24–32px vertical gaps between major typographic elements, mirroring modern developer documentation. The left navigation and right topic list are more compact, with denser vertical stacking of items but still using 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding to keep items legible and scannable. No elements feel cramped; there is consistent whitespace around groups and between the three columns to maintain a spacious, breathable layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow begins with the global header: the eye moves from the top-left logo, across to the centered search, then to right-aligned actions. From there, attention drops diagonally into the left sidebar to confirm the current documentation section, then moves into the central column to the page category label and prominent H1 title. After parsing the introductory text and any hero media, attention naturally shifts to the right-hand topic list to choose a specific quickstart, creating a secondary left-to-right sweep across the three columns. Active states in the left sidebar and right topic list, plus the colored page label, reinforce the current context.

layout.overall_description: The Getting Started page presents a modern, three-column documentation layout beneath a fixed global header. The header spans the full width with a discrete height: logo and product name on the left, a wide search input centered, and compact account/actions on the right, all set against a light background with subtle brand accents. Below this, the viewport is divided horizontally into a sturdy left navigation sidebar, a generous central reading column, and a slimmer right column. The left sidebar, starting directly under the header, runs from the top to the bottom of the viewport and lists documentation sections in grouped blocks with nested links, clearly indicating the current Getting Started area. In the center, a breadcrumb row sits at the top, leading into a small colored label, an oversized H1 title, a short explanatory subtitle, and a comfortably spaced introductory paragraph explaining what the getting-started area offers. This content sits in a wide, readable column with ample margins and line-height, optionally followed by a full-width screenshot or diagram aligned to that same column width. On the right, vertically aligned with the H1 area, the topic list appears as a clean, TOC-like vertical stack of quickstart entries that mirrors the right-side in-page navigation from the mockup. It occupies a narrower column, feels secondary to the main text, and is kept visually light through smaller type and subtle section labels. Together, these pieces create a clear Z-shaped reading pattern: top header, down to left nav, into the central title and paragraph, and finally across to the right topic list where the user chooses a specific quickstart path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page loader is non-visual during normal operation and sits conceptually at the root of the page’s layout tree, above the three-column content area but beneath the fixed header so that header navigation remains visible. When active, it overlays the entire content region below the header with a light, full-width and full-height panel aligned to the same 12‑column grid. A centered spinner or skeleton pattern appears in the middle of the viewport, dimming or hiding the left sidebar, central content, and right topic list uniformly to communicate loading without shifting their underlying positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally non-rendering with no spatial footprint. In loading states, behaves as a fixed overlay covering the whole scrollable area beneath the global header, centered both vertically and horizontally in the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state controller does not render any visible UI and therefore does not occupy physical space in the layout. It operates behind the scenes, controlling which sidebar items are marked active, which topic in the right list is highlighted, and which content appears in the central column, but it does so without displacing or resizing any visual components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical/stateful with no visual or spatial presence; it does not affect flow, sizing, or positioning of any elements directly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header is fixed to the very top of the viewport, spanning 100% of the page width. It has a modest height (roughly 64–72px including padding) with 12–16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding, following the base spacing scale. Inside, the logo and product name are left-aligned, occupying about the left quarter of the width; a wide search input is centered and spans roughly 40–50% of the header width; primary CTAs and user/account controls align to the right, occupying the remaining space. The background is a solid light color with a subtle bottom border or shadow to separate it from the content below. The three-column content area begins immediately under the header, leaving a consistent 24–32px gap before the breadcrumb in the central column, making the header feel compact yet distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport; remains visible on scroll while the underlying three-column content scrolls beneath it. Establishes the horizontal grid alignment for the columns below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb is positioned in the central content column, directly below the fixed header’s lower edge and aligned flush with the left edge of that column. It sits at the top of the scrollable content area, spanning the full width of the central column but using only a single line (or two on wrap) of small, subdued text. There is approximately 24–32px vertical space from the bottom of the header to the breadcrumb, and about 8–12px of vertical space between the breadcrumb and the page category label that begins the main content. This keeps the breadcrumb visually present but lightweight, forming a subtle lead-in above the more prominent H1.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column’s normal document flow; it scrolls away with the content while respecting the fixed header above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sidebar occupies the leftmost column of the main content area, starting directly below the fixed header and extending down to the bottom of the viewport. It uses a fixed width corresponding to roughly 22–24% of the viewport on desktop (around 3 of 12 grid columns), with 24px internal horizontal padding and 16–24px vertical padding at the top. Navigation content is arranged in a vertical stack: at the top, a small group of top-level tabs (e.g., Documentation, API Reference, Blog) laid out as full-width pills or buttons; below that, grouped sections like “Welcome” and “Guides” are presented as section labels with nested links indented under each label. Each nav item uses 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, with an active state (e.g., a colored bar or pill) highlighting the current Getting Started section. There is a subtle dividing line or extra vertical spacing between section groups to avoid visual clutter while maintaining a compact vertical list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned along the left edge in a column that remains in place as the main content scrolls. The sidebar column itself is fixed relative to the page layout, and its internal content is scrollable if it exceeds the viewport height, ensuring the top of the sidebar stays aligned under the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main content component occupies the central column of the page beneath the header and to the right of the left sidebar. It spans roughly 52–56% of the viewport width (about 6–7 grid columns) and is horizontally centered between the sidebar and the right topic list. The left edge aligns vertically with both the breadcrumb above and any media below, creating a clean text column. Within this area, content flows vertically: directly below the breadcrumb, a small, colored page category label (e.g., “Guides” or “Getting Started”) appears with about 8–12px top margin and 4–8px bottom margin. Beneath that, the H1 page title is large and bold, occupying one or two lines with 12–16px space above and 8–12px below. Next, a shorter subtitle in lighter weight sits with about 8–12px spacing, followed by a multi-line introductory paragraph with comfortable line-height and approximately 16–24px spacing above and below. If a screenshot or diagram is used, it appears directly under the introductory paragraph, spanning the full width of this central column (not the entire page), with 24–32px top margin and 32–40px bottom margin before any further sections. The entirety of this column favors generous whitespace, consistent left alignment, and a clear reading rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central grid column; it scrolls as a continuous reading surface. It does not overlap the header or sidebars and adapts fluidly within its allocated column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic list occupies the rightmost column of the layout, visually mirroring a right-hand TOC from the mockup. It is positioned to the right of the main content, starting at approximately the same vertical level as the page category label and H1 in the central column. This column takes about 20–24% of the viewport width (roughly 2–3 grid columns) and has 16–24px horizontal padding inside. Within it, topics are arranged in a vertical list: a small, muted header label such as “Quickstarts” or “On this page” appears at the top, followed by individual topic entries stacked with 8–12px vertical spacing. Each entry is a single-line or two-line text link with subtle hover and active states; the active or currently viewed quickstart is indicated by a slightly bolder type, colored accent, or a small left border, paralleling the left nav’s active treatment but in a more subtle way. If topics are grouped (e.g., by language or use case), group headers use slightly stronger typography with nested items indented beneath. The overall density is moderate: items are close enough to scan quickly but separated enough not to blur together.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a right sidebar column. Typically uses sticky positioning so that the top of the topic list remains within view as the user scrolls the main content, with a small offset (e.g., 16–24px) beneath the fixed header. Within this sticky container, the list itself scrolls only if it exceeds viewport height.

name: Quickstart: Text Classification
title: Text Classification Quickstart
description: Interactive quickstart for configuring and copying a text classification evaluation script, with language tabs and an editable Python evaluation configuration.

core_features: Load quickstart metadata, language tabs, and associated code snippets for text classification using getQuickstartTextClassificationPage.
Display language-specific code samples in tabs, with the initially selected tab controlled by the optional `lang` URL parameter.
Provide a "Run your first evaluation" section with an editable Python code snippet that includes metrics, max_samples, and model configuration, backed by an EvaluationConfigSnippet.
Allow users to adjust evaluation settings by editing metrics, max_samples, and model_id within the Python code block and synchronizing these changes through configureQuickstartEvaluation.
Enable copying the currently displayed code snippet (including the fully configured evaluation script) via a copy button that calls copyCodeSnippet.
Offer navigation to the API reference (e.g., via a "Next: API Reference" link) after completing the quickstart.
user_workflows:
1. Initial load:
   - User lands on quickstart_text_classification.html, optionally with a `lang` URL parameter.
   - The page invokes getQuickstartTextClassificationPage.
   - The response provides a DocPage, available languageTabs, and related CodeSnippet and EvaluationConfigSnippet records.
   - The page determines the initial selected language tab by first checking the `lang` URL parameter; if it matches one of the languageTabs entries, that tab is selected, otherwise the tab where isDefault is true is selected.

2. Switching language tabs:
   - User clicks on a language tab in the tab bar.
   - The selected tab is updated in the page state and the associated CodeSnippet is displayed in the main code area.
   - The `lang` URL parameter is updated (e.g. via history.replaceState) to the selected language code so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the selected language.
   - If the Python tab is selected and there is a linked EvaluationConfigSnippet, the page shows the config summary (metrics, maxSamples, modelId) derived from that snippet.

3. Editing the Python evaluation configuration in code:
   - With the Python tab selected and the Python CodeSnippet marked as editable (isEditable true), the user modifies the metrics, max_samples, and model identifier directly in the code editor.
   - The editor updates its local representation of the CodeSnippet.code as the user types.
   - When the user chooses to synchronize and validate these changes (for example by clicking a dedicated "Apply changes" button), the page logic extracts the metrics array, maxSamples value, and modelId string from the current code content.
   - The page calls configureQuickstartEvaluation with the Python code snippet’s id and the extracted metrics, maxSamples, and modelId.
   - The API returns an updatedCodeSnippet and updatedEvaluationConfigSnippet; the page replaces the previous CodeSnippet and EvaluationConfigSnippet instances in its state with these updated objects, ensuring that the code editor shows the canonical, validated script and that the config summary reflects the new metric and model choices.

4. Reviewing configuration summary:
   - When the Python snippet is selected and its EvaluationConfigSnippet is available, the page shows a concise summary of the current configuration (e.g., selected metrics list, maxSamples, and modelId) sourced from EvaluationConfigSnippet.
   - This summary updates immediately after configureQuickstartEvaluation returns, so users can quickly confirm that their edited code corresponds to the intended metrics and model configuration.

5. Copying the evaluation script:
   - At any time, the user can click the copy button attached to the currently visible CodeSnippet.
   - The page calls copyCodeSnippet with the visible snippet’s id.
   - On success, the response contains the full code string; the UI copies this string to the clipboard and may show a transient success state (driven purely by in-memory UI state, not stored persistently).
   - The copied script always reflects the latest state of the snippet, including any configuration changes previously applied through configureQuickstartEvaluation.

6. Navigating to the API reference:
   - After configuring and optionally copying the script, the user can click a navigation control (e.g., "Next: API Reference").
   - The page performs a standard navigation to api_reference.html.
   - Optionally, the current `lang` URL parameter value can be appended to the target URL so that the API reference can show matching language examples, but this is handled purely via URL query construction without additional interfaces.
interactions: Click language tab: Updates the selected language in page state, updates the `lang` URL parameter, and renders the corresponding CodeSnippet in the main code area.
Code editor typing (Python): Updates the in-memory representation of the Python CodeSnippet.code while preserving the CodeSnippet.id and language metadata from getQuickstartTextClassificationPage.

Click "Apply changes" (or equivalent sync control) for Python configuration: Triggers parsing of metrics, maxSamples, and modelId from the Python CodeSnippet.code, then calls configureQuickstartEvaluation with these values and updates CodeSnippet and EvaluationConfigSnippet data in state based on the API response.

View configuration summary: Reads EvaluationConfigSnippet for the active Python CodeSnippet and displays metrics, maxSamples, and modelId; updates automatically after configureQuickstartEvaluation completes.

Click copy button on current code block: Calls copyCodeSnippet with the currently displayed CodeSnippet.id; on success, copies the returned code string to the clipboard and updates a transient success indicator in the UI.
Click "Next: API Reference" link: Navigates to api_reference.html, optionally preserving the current `lang` value in the query string.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary persistent state for this page is managed via URL parameters and API responses, not localStorage. On initial load, the `lang` query parameter (if present) is read from the page URL. After getQuickstartTextClassificationPage returns, the page inspects the languageTabs array and attempts to match the `lang` parameter to one of the available language values. If a match is found, that tab is set as the selected language; otherwise, the tab where isDefault is true is selected. Whenever the user changes the language by clicking a tab, the page updates the in-memory selectedLanguage state and synchronizes the URL by setting the `lang` parameter to the newly selected language code (e.g., by using the History API to avoid a full reload). All code content and configuration details used on the page come from the getQuickstartTextClassificationPage response and any subsequent configureQuickstartEvaluation response. The editable Python code’s authoritative version is always the latest CodeSnippet.code value returned from these interfaces. When the user edits the Python code and triggers synchronization, configureQuickstartEvaluation returns updatedCodeSnippet and updatedEvaluationConfigSnippet objects, which replace their previous counterparts in the page state. This ensures that future interactions (including copying the code via copyCodeSnippet) always operate on the current configuration. No configuration values are hardcoded; all evaluation-related fields (metrics, maxSamples, modelId) are derived from and written back to EvaluationConfigSnippet and CodeSnippet via the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Fetches all data required to render the Text Classification Quickstart page by calling getQuickstartTextClassificationPage on initial load and exposes the resulting DocPage, languageTabs, CodeSnippet array, and EvaluationConfigSnippet array to child components.
event_handlers: onLoad:getQuickstartTextClassificationPage
onLoadSuccess:distributeDocPageAndSnippetsToChildren
onLoadError:displayLoadErrorState

components.functionality: Renders a tab for each entry in languageTabs and manages selection of the active language. On page initialization, it reads the `lang` URL parameter and selects the matching language tab if available, otherwise it selects the tab marked as default. When a tab is clicked, it updates the selected language in state, informs the code display which CodeSnippetId to show, and updates the `lang` URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit:readLangFromUrlAndSelectTab
onTabClick:updateSelectedLanguageState
onTabClick:updateLangUrlParameter
onTabClick:notifyCodeDisplayOfSelectedCodeSnippet

components.functionality: Displays the CodeSnippet corresponding to the currently selected language tab. For the Python language, it uses the CodeSnippet.isEditable flag to enable in-place editing of the code and associates the snippet with its EvaluationConfigSnippet (if available). For non-Python languages, it shows the code in a read-only view while still allowing copying via the copy component.
event_handlers: onSelectedLanguageChange:loadCorrespondingCodeSnippetIntoView
onCodeChange:updateInMemoryCodeSnippetContent
onCodeBlur:markSnippetAsEditedForPotentialSync

components.functionality: Provides the logic to synchronize user edits in the Python code snippet with the underlying evaluation configuration. When the user requests to apply changes, it extracts the metrics, maxSamples, and modelId values from the current Python CodeSnippet.code, calls configureQuickstartEvaluation with these values and the CodeSnippet.id, and then updates the relevant CodeSnippet and EvaluationConfigSnippet records in the page state based on the response.
event_handlers: onApplyChangesClick:parsePythonCodeForConfigValues
onApplyChangesClick:configureQuickstartEvaluation
onConfigureQuickstartEvaluationSuccess:updateCodeSnippetState
onConfigureQuickstartEvaluationSuccess:updateEvaluationConfigSnippetState
onConfigureQuickstartEvaluationError:displayConfigErrorState

components.functionality: Shows a concise summary of the current evaluation configuration for the Python quickstart, including metrics, maxSamples, and modelId, derived from the EvaluationConfigSnippet linked to the active Python CodeSnippet. This component updates whenever the underlying EvaluationConfigSnippet changes, helping users confirm their configured metrics and model without scanning the full code.
event_handlers: onSelectedLanguageChange:showOrHideBasedOnPythonSelection
onEvaluationConfigSnippetChange:refreshSummaryDisplay

components.functionality: Attaches to the currently visible CodeSnippet and triggers the copy operation. When clicked, it calls copyCodeSnippet with the active CodeSnippet.id, receives the full code string, copies it to the clipboard, and updates a transient success or error indicator in the UI.
event_handlers: onClick:copyCodeSnippet
onCopyCodeSnippetSuccess:copyReturnedCodeToClipboard
onCopyCodeSnippetSuccess:showCopySuccessIndicator
onCopyCodeSnippetError:showCopyErrorIndicator

components.functionality: Provides a functional navigation control (e.g., a button or link) that directs the user to api_reference.html after they have reviewed or copied the quickstart code. It can optionally propagate the current `lang` URL parameter to the target URL so that subsequent pages can respect the user’s chosen language.
event_handlers: onClick:navigateToApiReferenceWithOptionalLangParam

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page adopts a three-column documentation layout: a fixed global header across the top, a fixed-width left sidebar for global and section navigation, a fluid central column for the Quickstart content, and a narrower right sidebar for in-page anchors and configuration summary. Within the central column, the vertical flow is: small page category label (e.g., "Guides") at the top, then the H1 page title "Quickstart: Text Classification", a one-line subtitle, a short introductory paragraph, followed by the interactive quickstart block composed of the language tab selector, sync controller, code viewer with copy button, and supporting text. The navigation to the next page appears as the last element in the central column, well-separated from the quickstart block.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page adopts a three-column documentation layout: a fixed global header across the top, a fixed-width left sidebar for global and section navigation, a fluid central column for the Quickstart content, and a narrower right sidebar for in-page anchors and configuration summary. Within the central column, the vertical flow is: small page category label (e.g., "Guides") at the top, then the H1 page title "Quickstart: Text Classification", a one-line subtitle, a short introductory paragraph, followed by the interactive quickstart block composed of the language tab selector, sync controller, code viewer with copy button, and supporting text. The navigation to the next page appears as the last element in the central column, well-separated from the quickstart block.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, horizontal space is split approximately 22% left sidebar, 56% central content, and 22% right sidebar. The central column houses all primary interactive Quickstart components and stretches generously to emphasize readability. The right sidebar contains the TOC at the top and the config summary panel beneath it, both within the 22% width. Vertical spacing follows an 8px base scale, using 24–40px gaps between major text blocks (label, H1, subtitle, intro) and 32–48px around the main quickstart code area to make it feel like a single, prominent content unit.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text and controls are moderately spacious: headings and body copy use comfortable line-height with clear separation, while functional UI elements (tabs, buttons, toggles) are compact but padded (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) for scannability. The quickstart code section is visually dense in terms of information but framed with generous padding and margins so it doesn’t feel cramped. Sidebar lists use a denser vertical rhythm than the main article but still include enough spacing to avoid clutter and maintain quick scanability.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins in the global header (logo at top-left, central search, right-aligned CTAs), then drops into the left sidebar where the active documentation section is highlighted. From there, attention moves into the central column, starting with the category label and H1, then down through subtitle and intro. The eye is then drawn to the interactive quickstart block, where the language tabs on the left and the sync control on the right form the entry point into the code viewer. The copy button in the top-right of the code block becomes the primary action once the snippet is inspected. The right sidebar TOC provides a secondary scan path for the page structure and draws attention laterally to the config summary card, reinforcing what the code snippet represents. Finally, after scrolling through the main content, the next-page navigation link at the bottom provides a clear directional cue for continued exploration.

layout.overall_description: The page appears as a clean, three-column developer documentation layout under a slim fixed global header. The left sidebar occupies roughly the left fifth of the screen, with a vertical stack of section tabs and nested navigation links, one of which is highlighted to indicate the current Quickstart. The central content column, slightly more than half the page width, opens with a small colored label like "Guides" above a large, bold H1 "Quickstart: Text Classification", followed by a softer subtitle and a short explanatory paragraph set with generous line spacing. Below this intro, the main interactive quickstart module spans the full width of the central column: a horizontal row at the top contains language tabs aligned to the left and a compact sync-with-config controller aligned to the right. Directly beneath, a full-width code viewer panel appears as a card-like block with ample internal padding, a subtle header bar, and a copy button anchored to the top-right of the code area. This block visually replaces the "screenshot" role from the mockup, acting as the hero media element. On the right side of the page, a narrower sidebar runs from just under the header down the viewport, starting with an "On this page" TOC list and, beneath it, a contained config summary card that echoes the central code configuration. As the user scrolls, the header and sidebars maintain orientation (with the right sidebar content partially sticky), while the central column flows vertically through the quickstart content and ends with a well-separated "Next: API Reference" navigation link aligned with the main text. The overall effect is a modern, minimally styled SaaS documentation experience that guides the user from understanding to copying an evaluation script with minimal friction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page loader overlays only the central content column where the Quickstart content lives. It appears after the static header and sidebars load, covering the area from just below the H1/intro text down to the bottom edge of the currently visible central column. Visually, it presents as a semi-transparent light scrim spanning nearly 100% of the central column width, with a centered loading indicator (spinner and short status text) aligned both horizontally and vertically within that column. Margins at the left and right match the central column’s internal padding, so the loader aligns crisply with the edges of the code viewer and language tabs. The left navigation and right sidebar remain visible and interactive around it, reinforcing that loading pertains specifically to the quickstart configuration and code, not the entire app.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolutely or relatively positioned overlay within the central column, anchored below the introductory text; it sits on top of the language tabs, sync controller, and code viewer while active, then disappears completely once loading finishes. It scrolls with the page but, under typical use, appears only at the initial load and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The language tab selector sits in the central column as the first interactive element of the quickstart module, directly below the introductory paragraph and separated from it by approximately 32px of vertical space. It forms the left side of a horizontal control bar that spans the full width of the central content area. The tabs are aligned to the left edge of the central column’s content, starting about 24px from the column’s left boundary, and occupy the remaining space horizontally until roughly halfway across this bar. Each tab appears as a pill or underlined label with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, following the mockup’s button sizing. Below the tabs, there is about 16px of vertical space before the top edge of the code viewer panel, making the tabs feel tightly coupled to the code they control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The language tabs are statically positioned within the central column, as part of a horizontal row that also contains the sync controller aligned to the right. They scroll away with the rest of the content; no sticky behavior is applied in order to keep the layout calm and predictable on long pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quickstart code display occupies the central role in the main column, appearing directly beneath the language tabs and sync controller row, separated by about 16px of vertical space. It spans nearly the full width of the central content column (approximately 90–96% of that column’s width), with equal left and right padding to align with the H1 and paragraph text. The code viewer is presented as a card-like block with a subtle border or shadow and 16–24px internal padding on all sides. At the top of the block, a compact header strip runs horizontally: on the left, a small label such as the file name or "Sample script"; on the right, the copy-snippet button is anchored. The code area itself fills most of the card vertically, using a monospace font with generous line-height to match the reading-friendly style of the page. The bottom of the code block has 32–48px of space before any following explanatory text or the next-page navigation, ensuring the code feels like a distinct hero element rather than compressed into surrounding content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The code display is a standard scrolling block within the central column; if the snippet is long, the code area itself can scroll internally while the card remains fixed in height, but the entire component scrolls with the page. It aligns to the center column grid and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Python config sync controller shares a single horizontal row with the language-tab selector and is aligned to the far right of the central content column, creating a balanced bar above the code viewer. The row runs full-width across the central column; the language tabs hug the left side while the sync controller sits on the right, with flexible space in between. Visually, the sync controller appears as a compact label and toggle switch or dropdown, with about 8px vertical and 16px horizontal padding around its interactive region. Its baseline aligns with the labels of the language tabs so that the entire bar forms a single cohesive control strip. There is consistent 24px padding between the controller and the right edge of the central column to match the left padding used for the tabs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sync controller is statically positioned in the control row above the code viewer, aligned to the right and horizontally opposed to the language tabs. On narrower viewports, it can wrap under the tabs while maintaining a left alignment, but on desktop it remains on the same line. It scrolls with the central column and does not have sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The config summary panel resides in the right sidebar as a structured card beneath the in-page TOC. Starting roughly 24–32px below the bottom of the "On this page" heading and its list of anchors, the config summary spans nearly the full width of the right column (about 90–95% of that column’s width), with consistent internal padding of 16–24px. It is visually grouped as a standalone block with a light border or subtle background tint to differentiate it from the TOC list above. The title of the summary sits at the top of the card, followed by bullet points or small key-value rows summarizing the selected text classification configuration; spacing between these rows is moderately tight but readable. The top of the card aligns vertically with the mid-height of the main code block when the page is first loaded, reinforcing their conceptual connection without crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the right sidebar, the config summary panel participates in the sidebar’s sticky behavior: once the user scrolls past the header, the TOC and the config summary panel can remain within view, pinned relative to the top of the viewport with a margin below the global header. The panel itself does not scroll independently; it simply sits within the right-sidebar flow and moves with the page while respecting the sticky constraints of the sidebar container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The copy snippet button is embedded in the top-right region of the quickstart code display, occupying the right end of the code block’s header strip. Horizontally, it is flush-aligned with the right internal padding of the code card, maintaining about 16–24px spacing from the card’s edge. Vertically, it is aligned to the center of the code header row, to the right of any file-name or label text on the left. The button itself is compact, with 8–10px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding, and uses the brand accent color to stand out against the neutral code block background. There is approximately 8–12px of space between the button and any adjacent text or icons, ensuring it is easy to hit and visually distinct without overwhelming the code.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The copy button is anchored within the code viewer component’s header and scrolls with it. It does not float outside the card or become sticky; its position is always relative to the top-right corner of the code display. On narrower screens, if the header content wraps, the button remains right-aligned on its own line to preserve its visibility and primary-action status.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The next-page navigation link is placed at the bottom of the central content column, following the quickstart code display and any short explanatory text by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px. It is left-aligned with the main text column, starting at the same horizontal position as the H1 and body copy. The link may include a small label like "Next" above or beside the main target text (e.g., "API Reference"), and an arrow icon to the right indicating forward navigation. The link is not boxed in a heavy card; instead, it appears as a clean textual call-to-action with subtle styling and 8–12px vertical padding, sitting on a neutral background with ample whitespace around it so it feels like a natural continuation step after completing the quickstart.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This navigation link is part of the normal vertical document flow inside the central column and scrolls with the page. It appears once the user has scrolled past the main quickstart interaction, acting as the concluding element of the page’s main content area without any sticky or floating behavior.

name: API Reference Overview
title: API Reference Overview
description: Central documentation hub that shows API-wide concepts and a version-aware sidebar of resource groups and endpoints, with navigation into detailed endpoint pages.

core_features: Load and display API Reference overview metadata (title, description, docs category) using getApiReferenceOverview.
Show the currently selected API/docs version and ensure all concepts and navigation reflect that version.
Render a sidebar listing API groups and their endpoints using getApiReferenceSidebarData, filtered by the current docs version.
Allow pre-expanding a specific API group in the sidebar using the URL group parameter.
Display high-level conceptual sections for authentication, base URLs, and common concerns (pagination, errors) using the overview content.
Enable navigation from sidebar endpoint entries to their dedicated endpoint documentation pages.
Provide breadcrumb navigation back to the documentation home page.
user_workflows:
Landing on API Reference without a group parameter:
1. User navigates to the API Reference page via a header navigation link or a direct link.
2. On page load, the page shell triggers getApiReferenceOverview and getApiReferenceSidebarData in parallel.
3. The page reads the current URL and detects that no group parameter is present.
4. Once getApiReferenceOverview resolves, the main content displays the API Reference title, description, currentDocsVersionId, authSummary, and paginationSummary.
5. Once getApiReferenceSidebarData resolves, the sidebar renders all API groups and their endpoints, with no group forcibly expanded by the URL (local default behavior may expand one group, but this is not persisted outside the page instance).
6. The user can browse conceptual sections in the main content and use the sidebar to inspect available endpoints.

Landing on API Reference with a pre-selected group:
1. User clicks a link from another page (e.g., Documentation Home or Support) that points to api_reference.html with a group query parameter (e.g., api_reference.html?group=<some-group-id>).
2. The page shell parses the group parameter before rendering.
3. The page fetches overview data with getApiReferenceOverview and sidebar data with getApiReferenceSidebarData.
4. After sidebar data is loaded, the sidebar component matches the group parameter to one of the ApiGroup.id values returned from getApiReferenceSidebarData.
5. If a matching group is found, that group is expanded and scrolled into view; other groups follow the default collapsed/expanded behavior.
6. The user sees the selected group expanded and can immediately inspect the endpoints in that group.

Navigating to an endpoint’s detailed documentation from the sidebar:
1. User sees an expanded API group in the sidebar containing a list of endpoints derived from getApiReferenceSidebarData.
2. The user clicks an endpoint entry.
3. The sidebar component’s click handler uses properties from the selected ApiEndpoint (including docPageId and any routing rules configured by the docs system) to construct a destination URL for the endpoint’s documentation page.
4. The browser navigates to the endpoint’s documentation page (e.g., a specific API endpoint doc page) using standard navigation.
5. The user reads detailed parameters, examples, and response schemas on the endpoint page (outside the scope of this page).

Understanding authentication and pagination behaviors:
1. After the overview data from getApiReferenceOverview is loaded, the main content displays authSummary and paginationSummary.
2. The user scrolls to the authentication section to view how to authenticate, where to find base URLs, and which headers are required.
3. The user scrolls to the pagination section to understand common pagination patterns and parameters such as limit and cursor, based on paginationSummary.
4. Using this information, the user can return to the sidebar, select an endpoint, and apply the authentication and pagination concepts when reading its detailed documentation.

Changing the active group using the sidebar (and updating the URL):
1. The user clicks a different group header in the sidebar.
2. The sidebar toggles that group’s expanded/collapsed state.
3. When a group is expanded, the sidebar component updates the page URL’s group query parameter (using history.pushState or a similar mechanism) to reflect the newly active group id.
4. The user can share or bookmark this URL; reloading it or opening it in a new tab will re-open the same group because the page will again read the group parameter on load.

Returning to Documentation Home via breadcrumb or logo:
1. The breadcrumb component renders based on the DocPage information from getApiReferenceOverview, including a parent item representing Documentation Home.
2. The user clicks the documentation home breadcrumb or logo.
3. The breadcrumb/branding click handler navigates the browser to the Documentation Home page URL.
4. The user leaves the API Reference page and arrives at the main docs home.
interactions:
Page load:
- Trigger: Browser loads api_reference.html.
- Behavior: The page shell calls getApiReferenceOverview and getApiReferenceSidebarData with no parameters. It also parses the current URL’s query string to read the group parameter if present.
- Result: Overview content and sidebar navigation data are loaded and rendered, with group expansion state initialized from the URL.

Sidebar group toggle:
- Trigger: User clicks on a group header in the sidebar.
- Behavior: If the group is collapsed, it is expanded and its endpoints list becomes visible; if the group is expanded, it may be collapsed depending on the UX rules. When a group becomes the active expanded group, the component updates the URL group parameter to that group’s id. If no group should be treated as active, the component may remove the group parameter from the URL.
- Result: User sees updated sidebar state and the URL reflects the current expanded group for shareability.

Endpoint link click in sidebar:
- Trigger: User clicks an endpoint item under a group in the sidebar.
- Behavior: The component reads the clicked ApiEndpoint’s docPageId and uses the docs routing configuration to generate the correct endpoint documentation URL. The browser navigates to that URL.
- Result: The user is taken to the detailed endpoint documentation page.

Breadcrumb home click:
- Trigger: User clicks the Documentation Home breadcrumb or logo within the page header.
- Behavior: The breadcrumb handler navigates the browser to the documentation home URL (derived from site routing, not hardcoded in this page’s logic).
- Result: The user returns to the main documentation landing page.

In-page navigation to conceptual sections:
- Trigger: User clicks anchor links (e.g., internal links within the page) that jump to sections for authentication or pagination.
- Behavior: The browser scrolls to the corresponding section within the Same page using hash fragments or in-page anchors; no additional API calls are made.
- Result: User can quickly access important conceptual content without losing context in the sidebar.
