Browser back/forward navigation affecting group selection:
- Trigger: User uses the browser’s Back or Forward buttons after previously switching groups (which updated the group URL parameter).
- Behavior: The page listens for URL changes (e.g., popstate) and re-parses the group parameter. If it differs from the current active group, the sidebar updates its expanded group accordingly.
- Result: Sidebar state stays in sync with browser history and URLs.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state for this page is managed via the URL query parameter group and the data returned from getApiReferenceOverview and getApiReferenceSidebarData.

URL parameter: group
- Purpose: Controls which API group is pre-expanded in the sidebar when the page loads, and keeps the active group shareable/bookmarkable as the user interacts.
- Source: Included in links from other pages (such as Documentation Home or Support) and updated by interactions on this page.

Initialization:
1. On initial load, the page parses window.location.search and extracts the group parameter value, if present.
2. The page concurrently calls getApiReferenceOverview and getApiReferenceSidebarData.
3. After getApiReferenceSidebarData resolves, the sidebar receives the array of { group, endpoints } objects. It compares the group parameter value to the id of each group.
4. If a match is found, that group is marked as initially expanded. If no match is found, default expansion behavior is applied (implementation-defined, but not persisted outside the page). No additional API calls depend on the group parameter.

Ongoing updates:
- When a group header is clicked and that group becomes the active group, the sidebar updates the URL via history.pushState or an equivalent method so that the group query parameter equals the active group’s id.
- If a group is collapsed and no group should be considered active, the sidebar may remove the group parameter from the URL by updating the query string accordingly.
- The page listens for browser navigation events (e.g., popstate). When such an event occurs, it re-reads the group parameter and updates the sidebar’s active/expanded group state to match the URL, without refetching data.

Docs version state:
- The currentDocsVersionId returned by getApiReferenceOverview represents the active documentation/API version. Both getApiReferenceOverview and getApiReferenceSidebarData are assumed to already be filtered based on this version, so the page does not need to control version via URL.
- The version indicator component uses currentDocsVersionId for display only; it does not modify the URL or trigger additional API calls.

Other state:
- The open/closed state of each sidebar group (beyond the active one controlled by group) is maintained in in-memory component state only. It is not persisted via localStorage and does not trigger further API calls.
- Scrolling positions, selected in-page anchor sections, and any UI focus states are handled by the browser and components locally without affecting or depending on URL parameters beyond standard anchors.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and layout composition for the API Reference Overview page. It calls getApiReferenceOverview and getApiReferenceSidebarData on load, passes results into child components, and ensures the sidebar and main content use consistent data and docs version.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onOverviewDataLoaded
onSidebarDataLoaded
onUrlParamChange

components.functionality: Displays the page title, optional subtitle/description, breadcrumb navigation, and the current documentation/API version indicator based on the overview data.
event_handlers: onClickBreadcrumbHome
onClickBreadcrumbCurrent

components.functionality: Shows the current documentation/API version using currentDocsVersionId from getApiReferenceOverview. It is a read-only indicator on this page to reassure users that endpoints and examples correspond to a specific version.

Renders the list of API groups and their endpoints using data from getApiReferenceSidebarData. It manages expanded/collapsed state for each group, pre-expands the group specified in the URL group parameter, updates that parameter when the active group changes, and handles navigation to endpoint documentation pages when endpoints are clicked.
event_handlers: onGroupHeaderClick
onEndpointLinkClick
onGroupExpansionStateChange
onSidebarInitializedWithUrlGroup

components.functionality: Displays the high-level overview content returned by getApiReferenceOverview, including description, authSummary, paginationSummary, and any other conceptual sections about common API behavior. It may expose anchor links for in-page navigation (e.g., jump-to-authentication, jump-to-pagination).
event_handlers: onClickInPageAnchor

components.functionality: Renders the authentication and base URL information using the authSummary field from getApiReferenceOverview. Content is treated as markup (Markdown or HTML) and is inserted into the page without modification. This section may include examples or notes defined entirely by the backend content.

Renders the paginationSummary field from getApiReferenceOverview, explaining common pagination parameters and behavior. The content is provided by the backend and displayed as-is to guide users when they read individual endpoint docs.

Optionally shows a high-level summary list of API groups within the main content area using the apiGroups array from getApiReferenceOverview. Each group entry can provide a link that focuses the corresponding group in the sidebar by updating the group URL parameter, keeping navigation between conceptual summaries and sidebar navigation synchronized.
event_handlers: onClickGroupSummaryItem

components.functionality: Centralizes logic for reading and writing URL parameters related to this page, specifically the group parameter. It exposes methods for reading the current group from the URL on load, updating the URL when the active group changes, and reacting to browser history events to keep the sidebar state in sync.
event_handlers: onInitReadUrl
onUpdateGroupParam
onBrowserPopState

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a three-column documentation layout: a fixed-width left sidebar for API navigation, a fluid central column for the API Reference Overview content (header + conceptual sections), and a narrower right sidebar for the in-page table of contents. Within the central column, keep a clear top-to-bottom flow: small page category label, large H1, supporting subtitle, brief intro paragraph, then conceptual sections (Authentication, Pagination, Resource Groups overview). The API version indicator sits visually attached to the page header, reinforcing that all content is version-scoped.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a three-column documentation layout: a fixed-width left sidebar for API navigation, a fluid central column for the API Reference Overview content (header + conceptual sections), and a narrower right sidebar for the in-page table of contents. Within the central column, keep a clear top-to-bottom flow: small page category label, large H1, supporting subtitle, brief intro paragraph, then conceptual sections (Authentication, Pagination, Resource Groups overview). The API version indicator sits visually attached to the page header, reinforcing that all content is version-scoped.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly 22% of the viewport width for the left sidebar, 54% for the central content column, and 24% for the right sidebar at this desktop viewport. The global header spans 100% width with modest height. Provide 24–32px gutters between columns. Within blocks, apply 16–24px internal padding and 24–40px vertical gaps between related elements, with 48–64px between major sections like the page header and the first conceptual block.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious reading experience: generous line-height, 70–80 character line length, and clear separation between sections. Navigation in both sidebars is denser but still padded enough (8–12px vertical per item, 16–24px horizontal padding) to be easily scannable. Avoid cramming: each subsection (Authentication, Pagination, Resource Groups) reads as its own block with breathing room above and below.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: At the top, follow a Z-pattern: brand/logo on the top-left of the global header, main search centered, and primary CTA or profile actions on the top-right. Below, attention drops to the left sidebar to select the API area, then moves into the central H1 area. The page category label, H1, and version indicator clearly signal context. After understanding the overview, the eye shifts right to the in-page TOC for quick scanning of subsections, creating a secondary left-to-right sweep across the three columns. Sticky behavior on the header and sidebars supports constant orientation while scrolling the central content.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a modern three-column documentation view beneath a fixed global header. The header runs full-width across the top with comfortable vertical padding, placing the logo on the left, a prominent centered search input, and right-aligned actions, establishing a clear Z-shaped scan path. Directly under this, the layout splits into three vertical regions: a fixed-width left sidebar for API navigation, a wide central column for the API Reference Overview content, and a narrower right sidebar for the in-page table of contents. The left sidebar is vertically stacked with top-level documentation tabs and a version-aware list of resource groups and endpoints, scrolling independently if needed. The central column begins with a spacious page header: a subtle colored label indicating "API Reference", a large H1 "API Reference Overview", a short descriptive subtitle, and a concise introductory paragraph that together set the context. A compact version pill near the header reinforces which API version is being viewed. Below, the main content flows downward through clearly separated sections such as Authentication, Pagination, and a visual summary of resource groups. Each section uses generous spacing, readable typography, and consistent alignment, so the content feels airy yet information-rich. On the right, a slim in-page navigation column mirrors the structure of the central content with a vertically stacked list of section links, slightly indented for subsections, and remains sticky under the global header as the user scrolls. Invisible URL state management keeps the current version, selected nav item, and anchored section in sync, making the layout feel cohesive and responsive to user navigation without adding visual clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell occupies the full browser viewport and establishes the primary structure: a fixed global header at the very top and a three-column body below. The global header spans 100% width with a height of roughly 64–72px, padded 16–24px vertically and 24–32px horizontally. Within the header, the logo sits on the far left, a search bar is centered horizontally (spanning about 40–50% of the header width), and key actions (e.g., sign in, primary CTA) are right-aligned, forming a clear Z-pattern visual flow. Directly beneath the header, the body is split into three vertical columns aligned to an implied 12-column grid: the left navigation sidebar occupies about 22% of the page width, the main content column occupies about 54%, and the right in-page TOC sidebar takes the remaining ~24%. There are consistent horizontal gutters (approximately 24px) between each column. The left and right sidebars extend from just below the header down to the bottom of the viewport, with sticky behavior so that their contents remain visible as the central column scrolls. The central column is the main scrolling region, framed with 24–32px padding on the left and right to keep text line lengths comfortable. Major vertical sections within the central column (page header, conceptual sections, lists) are separated by 32–48px, with larger 48–64px gaps between conceptually distinct areas. Background remains a clean, light neutral color, with subtle dividers or shadows only where necessary (e.g., below the header) to reinforce separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The global header is fixed to the top of the viewport. The left navigation and right TOC sidebars are sticky within the viewport, starting just below the header, while the central content column scrolls vertically. The page shell controls the main three-column layout and adapts proportionally to viewport width while preserving relative column percentages and gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API Reference header sits at the top of the central content column, directly below the global header and slightly inset from the column edges. It begins about 32–40px below the bottom edge of the global header, maintaining a clear separation between global and page-level navigation. Inside this header block, elements are arranged in a vertical stack with generous spacing following a top-to-bottom reading flow. At the very top of the block, a small, left-aligned label (e.g., "API Reference") in a subtle brand accent color appears, with about 4–8px of top padding relative to the central column’s upper padding. Below it, the main page title (H1) is left-aligned, spanning roughly 70–80% of the central column’s width and set in large, bold typography. A short subtitle follows beneath the H1 with about 8–12px of vertical spacing, rendered in a slightly smaller, lighter-weight font, still left-aligned and constrained to the same text width. Below the subtitle, a multi-line introductory paragraph explains the role of the API Reference Overview; it is left-aligned, uses comfortable line-height, and occupies roughly the full text width of the column (up to about 70–80 characters per line). There is a 16–24px vertical gap between each of these text elements to preserve readability. At the bottom of the header block, a slightly larger vertical gap (24–32px) separates the introduction from the first conceptual section (e.g., Authentication), clearly delineating the header as its own zone. The entire header block feels anchored to the left, with ample whitespace above and to the right to maintain the minimalist, documentation-oriented style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; scrolls off the top as the user moves down the page. It does not stick, but its above-the-fold placement and spacing make it the initial focal point when the page loads.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API version indicator appears as a compact pill or badge visually attached to the top of the main content, aligning with the API Reference header. Horizontally, it is right-aligned within the central content column, occupying a small width (roughly 80–120px), and positioned on the same vertical band as either the page label or slightly above the H1’s baseline. There is approximately 8–16px of horizontal space between the indicator and the right edge of the central column padding, so it doesn’t touch the column boundary. Vertically, it is aligned such that when you look across the header row, you see the category label and H1 on the left and the version pill on the right, forming a subtle horizontal tension that frames the header area. The pill has internal padding of about 4–8px vertically and 12–16px horizontally, using understated brand or neutral colors to keep it informative but not dominant. There is 8–12px of vertical spacing between the version indicator and whichever header text element it visually pairs with, ensuring it feels attached but not cramped. On narrow widths where space is constrained, it can drop to a new line just under the category label but remain near the top of the header block to preserve its association with page-level context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central content column and vertically scrolls with the header. It maintains its right-aligned position relative to the column, without sticky behavior, but stays visually tied to the page’s top context area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API reference sidebar occupies the left column of the page body, starting directly beneath the global header and extending down toward the bottom of the viewport. It has a fixed visual width corresponding to roughly 22% of the page, with 24px of internal horizontal padding from the page shell gutter. At the top of the sidebar, there is a horizontal row of top-level documentation tabs (e.g., "Documentation", "API Reference", "Blog") that span nearly the full width of the sidebar, with each tab rendered as a pill or simple text button. These tabs are aligned left, spaced evenly with 8–12px horizontal gaps between them and 12–16px vertical padding inside each pill. The active tab ("API Reference") is visually highlighted with a background tint or underline. Below the tabs, after a 24px vertical gap, the main navigation tree is stacked vertically. Section headings (e.g., "Overview", "Authentication", "Resource Groups") appear as slightly bolder labels with 16–20px of vertical margin above each group label. Under each heading, individual links for resource groups and endpoints are listed with moderate density: each link uses 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–20px left padding relative to its group label, creating an indented hierarchy. Nested endpoints under a specific resource group are indented an additional 8–12px. Active or hovered items are indicated by a subtle left border or background highlight, not overpowering the rest of the layout. The sidebar content scrolls within its own area if it exceeds viewport height, but its top edge remains pinned just under the global header, ensuring continuous visibility of navigation regardless of how far down the main content is scrolled.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the left column: the sidebar container remains fixed in place relative to the viewport top (offset by the global header height), while its internal list content can scroll independently if it becomes taller than the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API overview content fills the central column beneath the page header, forming the main reading surface of the layout. It begins with a comfortable vertical gap (24–32px) below the API Reference header’s introductory paragraph, clearly signaling the transition from high-level context to detailed documentation. The content is arranged as a linear stack of sections: typically starting with an overview paragraph or short bullets, then the Authentication section, followed by Pagination, and concluding with a Resource Groups overview list and any other high-level concepts. Each section is left-aligned and constrained to a comfortable text width within the column, with 24–32px padding on both sides to prevent overly long line lengths. The vertical spacing between sections is generous: 32–40px between closely related sections and up to 48px between more distinct conceptual blocks. Each section starts with a heading (H2 for top-level concepts, H3 for subtopics), followed by explanatory text and optionally code samples or diagrams. When code or examples are present, they appear as full-width blocks within the central column, with 16–24px internal padding and 16px of extra vertical space separating them from adjacent paragraphs. The overall rhythm is consistent: heading, short descriptive text, examples, then a clear gap before the next heading. As the user scrolls, the right-hand TOC mirrors their position through subtle highlighting, reinforcing that this central column is the primary reading area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrollable content within the central column. It scrolls vertically under the fixed header and sticky sidebars. Anchors are embedded into section headings to support in-page linking and scroll-sync with the right-hand TOC and URL state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API Authentication section is presented as one of the first major blocks within the API overview content, directly below any brief general overview text. It starts with a clear section heading (e.g., "Authentication") aligned to the left edge of the central column’s text area. Above the heading, there is a 32–40px vertical gap from the preceding block, giving this section a strong visual entry point. Immediately under the heading, a short descriptive paragraph or two explains how authentication works for this API (tokens, keys, headers), using the same comfortable line length and line-height as the rest of the body text. Below this description and separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing, one or more code snippets or example requests may appear, each in a full-width code block styled as a simple card with a subtle border or background and 16–24px internal padding. If multiple language tabs are used for examples, the tab strip sits at the top of the code block, aligned with the text’s left edge, with small horizontal tab labels. The total width of the section matches the central column width, and its bottom edge is separated from the next section (e.g., Pagination) by 32–40px, ensuring the sections do not visually bleed into each other. This section is also represented as a top-level item in the right-hand TOC, so its heading aligns with an anchor that the user can jump to.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow. It scrolls as part of the central column and acts as an anchor target for the in-page TOC and URL hash, but has no independent sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API Pagination section appears in the central column directly below the Authentication section, following the same structural pattern. A top-level heading (e.g., "Pagination") is left-aligned with the other section headings and is positioned after a clear vertical gap of 32–40px from the end of the Authentication content. Below the heading, there is a short explanatory block of text describing pagination strategy (cursor-based, page-based, limit/offset) typically in one to three paragraphs. Line height and width align with the rest of the documentation, and there is at least 16–20px of vertical space between each paragraph for readability. If examples are included, they sit below the descriptive text with 16–24px vertical spacing, rendered as code or JSON blocks that stretch the full width available within the central column’s padding. Any table or parameter list that explains pagination-related query parameters is displayed as a simple table or definition list, aligned with the same left margin as the paragraph text, and separated from adjacent content by 16–24px of vertical padding. The bottom of the Pagination section is separated from the next conceptual block (such as the resource groups overview) by 32–40px, keeping each section visually distinct and easy to locate via scanning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable part of the main content. It is an anchorable section connected to the right sidebar TOC and URL state, without any sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API groups overview list forms a visually distinct summary section toward the lower portion of the central column, after core concepts like Authentication and Pagination. It begins with a heading such as "Resource Groups" or "API Surface" aligned to the main text left margin, positioned 32–48px below the preceding section. Beneath the heading, a short introductory line explains that the API is organized into groups (e.g., Evaluations, Datasets, Runs). Below this introduction and separated by 16–24px, the resource groups themselves are presented in a structured list that fits within the column’s width. On this documentation-style layout, this list is most naturally a vertical stack of items, each representing a group. Each group row behaves like a compact card: it spans nearly the full text width, with 16–20px internal padding, a subtle dividing line between items or a faint boundary around each card, and 12–16px vertical spacing between cards. Within each item, the group name is left-aligned, bolded, and may include a small icon or tag on the far right indicating the number of endpoints in that group. A single-line or brief multi-line description sits immediately below the group name with 4–8px of vertical spacing. The overall block reads as a quick scan of available API areas, using consistent alignment and moderate density so users can quickly jump from this overview to specific endpoints via the left sidebar or links embedded in each item. The section’s bottom edge leaves at least 40–48px of vertical space before any following content or page footers, ensuring it feels like a clear end to the main API overview narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central content. It scrolls with the main column and may include inline links that navigate to specific endpoint pages, leveraging URL state but without independent sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API Reference URL state controller is an invisible, non-visual layer that ties together navigation, scrolling, and version awareness across the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind the entire page shell, affecting how the left sidebar, central content, and right-hand TOC respond to user interactions. Spatially, it occupies no visible space: instead, it binds URL parameters (such as the selected API version, active resource group, specific endpoint, and in-page hash anchors) to the UI. When a user selects a version via the version indicator or a control in the sidebar, the URL state controller updates the URL (e.g., via a query param or path segment) and triggers the sidebar to reflect version-scoped groups and endpoints while keeping the same layout proportions. When a user clicks on a section in the right-hand TOC or a deep link in the central content, it scrolls the central column to the appropriate anchored section and synchronizes the active state across the TOC, sidebar, and possibly breadcrumbs in the header. It also ensures that reloading the page or sharing a link preserves the current scroll target and version, so the layout reconstitutes itself with the same column widths and sticky behaviors already in place.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial in the rendered layout. It does not occupy layout space or affect physical positioning but orchestrates scroll-into-view behavior, active states in sidebars and TOC, and URL updates in response to user interactions and scrolling.

name: API: Create Batch Evaluation
title: API: Create Batch Evaluation
description: Endpoint reference page that shows method, path, parameters, and editable request examples (including a configurable curl example) for creating batch evaluations.

core_features: Fetch and display API endpoint metadata (HTTP method, version, path template, path/query parameters, and request body requirements) for the current documentation page using getApiEndpointPageDetails.
Show an API Reference sidebar grouped by API groups and endpoints for the current docs version, using getApiReferenceSidebarData, with navigation into this and other endpoint pages.
Render per-language example requests and responses for the endpoint (including curl), with language tab switching, based on the examples returned from getApiEndpointPageDetails.

Provide a dedicated curl batch evaluation configurator that lets the user specify a concrete project identifier and optional pagination parameters (limit, cursor) and applies them to the curl example via configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample.
Allow inline editing of the URL path/query portion of the curl example through bound inputs that update projectId, limit, and cursor before calling configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample.
Support copying the full, currently configured API example code (especially the curl command) to the clipboard by calling copyApiExample on the active example.
user_workflows: View endpoint details and parameters: 1) User navigates to api_evaluations_batch.html with a docPageId URL parameter. 2) The page calls getApiEndpointPageDetails with docPageId. 3) The endpoint header, method, path template, and description are shown. 4) Path and query parameter tables are rendered from apiEndpoint.pathParams and apiEndpoint.queryParams.

Explore related endpoints via sidebar: 1) On page load, the page calls getApiReferenceSidebarData. 2) The sidebar lists API groups and their endpoints. 3) User clicks an endpoint in the Evaluations group. 4) The browser navigates to the corresponding endpoint’s documentation page (e.g., another api_endpoint page) with its own docPageId query parameter. 5) This triggers a new getApiEndpointPageDetails call for that page.

Switch between example languages: 1) After getApiEndpointPageDetails returns, all examples for the endpoint are available. 2) The active language tab is determined from the lang URL parameter if present, otherwise from a default derived from the first example. 3) When the user clicks a language tab, the lang URL parameter is updated, and the example panel switches to examples for that language without re-calling the API.

Configure the curl batch evaluation example: 1) User selects the curl request tab (or is already on it if lang=curl). 2) The configurator reads URL parameters projectId, limit, and cursor (if provided) and seeds the form fields. 3) User edits the project identifier and optional limit and cursor fields, or directly edits the URL input that represents the path and query portion. 4) When the user submits or applies changes, the configurator calls configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample with the curl ApiExample’s id and the current projectId, limit, and cursor values. 5) The updatedApiExample.code returned from configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample replaces the displayed curl snippet and the URL parameters (projectId, limit, cursor) are updated in the browser location.

Copy the configured curl command: 1) With the configured curl example visible, the user clicks the copy button on that example. 2) The page calls copyApiExample with the id of the currently displayed curl ApiExample (which is the id returned in updatedApiExample from the latest configurator call). 3) On success, the returned code string is placed into the clipboard using client-side clipboard APIs. 4) A transient confirmation UI is displayed based on the success flag and message fields of the copyApiExample response.

Navigate back to the API reference listing: 1) The breadcrumb includes an "API Reference" item and the sidebar includes an "All endpoints" link. 2) When the user clicks either, the browser navigates to api_reference.html, without calling additional interfaces from this page. 3) Navigation may include URL parameters such as docsVersion if the global navigation scheme requires it.
interactions: Page initialization: On load, read URL parameters (docPageId, lang, projectId, limit, cursor). If docPageId is present, call getApiEndpointPageDetails with it. Independently, call getApiReferenceSidebarData to render the sidebar. Set initial active language tab from lang or from the examples returned.

Language tab click: When a user clicks a language tab in the examples panel, update the active language in local component state and in the URL (lang parameter). No interface calls are made; the correct example is selected from the already-fetched examples array.

Sidebar endpoint click: When an endpoint item in the sidebar is clicked, navigate to that endpoint’s documentation page by updating window.location to the relevant html filename with the endpoint’s docPageId as a URL parameter. No interfaces from this page are called as a direct result of this click; the new page instance will perform its own getApiEndpointPageDetails call.
Breadcrumb click: When the user clicks the "API Reference" breadcrumb, navigate to api_reference.html (optionally preserving version-related URL parameters if present).

Curl configurator field change: When the user types into the projectId input, limit input, cursor input, or the URL input, the configurator updates its internal state. On blur or on explicit "Apply" action, it parses the URL input (if used) to update projectId, limit, and cursor fields in its state, keeping them consistent.

Apply configurator changes: When the user triggers the "Apply" action in the curl configurator, the component identifies the ApiExample for curl request (from the examples array) and calls configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample with parameters (apiExampleId, projectId, limit, cursor) based on current state. On success, it updates the in-memory representation of that ApiExample (id and code from updatedApiExample) and updates the browser URL parameters projectId, limit, and cursor to match the new configuration.

Copy example code: When the user clicks the copy button on the currently displayed example, the component calls copyApiExample with the example’s id. On success, it uses the returned code to populate the clipboard and shows a non-blocking confirmation. On failure, it shows a non-blocking error indication using the returned message field.

Path/query parameters inspection: The user can expand or interact with the path/query parameter tables to see descriptions and required flags derived from apiEndpoint.pathParams and apiEndpoint.queryParams. These interactions are purely client-side and do not trigger any interface calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from URL parameters and the results of getApiEndpointPageDetails and getApiReferenceSidebarData. The URL must include docPageId, which is passed directly to getApiEndpointPageDetails. The selected language tab is controlled by a lang URL parameter (e.g., lang=curl), and the component updates this parameter when the user changes tabs. The curl configurator reads initial values for projectId, limit, and cursor from URL parameters of the same names, uses them to seed its form, and passes them into configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample. When the configurator applies changes, it updates these URL parameters so that a page reload reproduces the same configured curl example. The currently active curl ApiExample’s id is taken from the examples array returned by getApiEndpointPageDetails and may be updated using the id returned in updatedApiExample from configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample; this id is then used for copyApiExample calls. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; all restorable state is encoded in the URL parameters, and all endpoint-specific data is re-fetched on load using the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Orchestrates data fetching for the endpoint page, including loading endpoint details and sidebar data, and distributing them to child components.
event_handlers: onLoad: Read URL parameters (docPageId, lang, projectId, limit, cursor). If docPageId exists, call getApiEndpointPageDetails({ docPageId }) and store the returned docPage, apiEndpoint, and examples in local state. Independently call getApiReferenceSidebarData() and store the group and endpoints data.
onDocsVersionChange (if provided by a global wrapper): Trigger a fresh getApiReferenceSidebarData call so the sidebar matches the current docs version.

onChildStateChange: When child components modify state that must be reflected in the URL (e.g., lang, projectId, limit, cursor), update the browser query string. This does not trigger new interface calls unless a new docPageId is introduced via navigation.

components.functionality: Displays hierarchical navigation for the API reference, including a link back to the API Reference list and the current endpoint name.
event_handlers: onClickApiReference: Navigate to api_reference.html. Optionally preserve relevant global parameters (e.g., docsVersion) in the URL if they exist.
onClickCurrentPage: No navigation; may trigger scroll to top.

components.functionality: Shows grouped API endpoints for navigation, filtered by the current docs version, and includes an 'All endpoints' link.
event_handlers: onLoadSidebarData: When api-endpoint-page-container provides the result of getApiReferenceSidebarData, render groups and their endpoints.

onClickEndpointItem: Navigate to the selected endpoint’s documentation page by updating window.location to that endpoint page filename and setting docPageId in the query string. The new page load will then call getApiEndpointPageDetails with that docPageId.
onClickAllEndpoints: Navigate to api_reference.html (may include version-related URL parameters if needed).

components.functionality: Displays the endpoint name, HTTP method, API version, path template, and short description.
event_handlers: onClickCopyPath: Optional client-side action that copies apiEndpoint.pathTemplate to the clipboard without calling copyApiExample (this does not use any assigned interface).

components.functionality: Shows structured tables for path parameters and query parameters with their descriptions and required flags.
event_handlers: onTogglePathParams: Client-side expand/collapse of the path parameters table using apiEndpoint.pathParams.
onToggleQueryParams: Client-side expand/collapse of the query parameters table using apiEndpoint.queryParams.

components.functionality: Renders language tabs and example blocks (requests/responses) for each language, and coordinates which example is currently displayed based on the active language and example type.
event_handlers: onInitializeExamples: After getApiEndpointPageDetails returns, initialize the examples panel with the full examples array and determine the initial active language: if a lang URL parameter is present and matches at least one ApiExample.language, use that; otherwise, pick the language from the first example.
onClickLanguageTab: Update internal state to set the active language and update the lang parameter in the URL. Filter the examples array to the active language and render its request/response blocks.

onSelectExample: When a specific example within a language is selected (e.g., request vs response), update the internally tracked active example id (this id will be used by copy buttons and by the curl configurator when the active language is curl).

components.functionality: Specialized control that appears when the active language is curl and the exampleType is request. It allows the user to configure projectId and optional limit and cursor values, optionally through an inline URL editor, and uses configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample to update the curl example code.
event_handlers: onInitConfigurator: When api-examples-panel reports that the active language is curl and a curl request ApiExample is available, read URL parameters (projectId, limit, cursor). Initialize internal state with: activeCurlExampleId (from the selected ApiExample.id), projectId, limit, cursor, and a derived URL string that combines apiEndpoint.pathTemplate with query parameters based on current limit and cursor. Do not call any interfaces at this point.
onChangeProjectId: When the user edits the projectId text input, update the configurator’s projectId state and, if there is a bound URL input, update the URL string accordingly.
onChangeLimit: When the user edits the limit input, update the limit state, validate it as a number in the client, and update the URL string to include or remove the corresponding query parameter.
onChangeCursor: When the user edits the cursor input, update the cursor state and update the URL string to include or remove the corresponding query parameter.

onChangeUrlInput: When the user directly edits the URL input representing the path and query string, parse the URL string client-side to extract a projectId portion and any limit and cursor query parameters. Update the projectId, limit, and cursor state accordingly, keeping all fields in sync.

onApplyConfiguration: When the user clicks an Apply button or triggers an equivalent action, call configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample with parameters: apiExampleId = activeCurlExampleId; projectId = current projectId state; limit = current limit state; cursor = current cursor state. On success, use updatedApiExample.id and updatedApiExample.code to replace the corresponding ApiExample in the examples panel’s state (so all child components display the latest code). Also update the browser URL parameters projectId, limit, and cursor to match the newly applied values.

onConfigurationError: If configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample returns an error (e.g., via network failure or response handling in the runtime), display a non-blocking error message in the configurator; no additional interfaces are called.

onLanguageChange: When api-examples-panel switches away from curl, hide the configurator. When switching back to curl, re-run onInitConfigurator using current URL parameters and the latest curl ApiExample.

components.functionality: Displays a single ApiExample code block (for the active language and example type), including syntax highlighting, scroll, and a copy button that uses copyApiExample.
event_handlers: onRenderExample: Receive the currently active ApiExample (id, language, exampleType, code) from the api-examples-panel or curl configurator and render its code content.

onClickCopy: When the user clicks the copy button, call copyApiExample with the current ApiExample.id. On success, use the returned code string for clipboard operations and show a confirmation using the message field. On failure, show an error message using the message field. This component does not modify the URL or call any other interfaces.
onExampleUpdated: When curl-batch-evaluation-configurator updates an ApiExample (through configureBatchEvaluationCurlExample), receive the new ApiExample object and re-render with the updated code.

components.functionality: Indicates whether the endpoint requires a request body and, if so, points users to the relevant example sections. This is purely descriptive and based on apiEndpoint.requiresRequestBody.
event_handlers: onToggleBodyDetails: Client-side expand/collapse to show or hide explanatory text about the request body using the information inferred from requiresRequestBody and the structure of existing examples.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a three-column documentation layout: a fixed-width left sidebar for API reference navigation, a fluid central column for the endpoint content, and a narrower right column reserved for in-page navigation (TOC) or contextual help. The central column content flows linearly: breadcrumbs at the very top, then the endpoint header (method + path + title + brief description), followed by request body requirements, then the parameters section, and finally the examples panel containing the curl configurator and code block. Within the examples panel, configurator and code sit side-by-side in a split view on wide screens, stacking vertically on smaller widths.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a three-column documentation layout: a fixed-width left sidebar for API reference navigation, a fluid central column for the endpoint content, and a narrower right column reserved for in-page navigation (TOC) or contextual help. The central column content flows linearly: breadcrumbs at the very top, then the endpoint header (method + path + title + brief description), followed by request body requirements, then the parameters section, and finally the examples panel containing the curl configurator and code block. Within the examples panel, configurator and code sit side-by-side in a split view on wide screens, stacking vertically on smaller widths.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Apply an implied 12-column grid where the left sidebar uses roughly 2.5–3 columns (~20–25% width), the central content about 6–7 columns (~50–55%), and the right column about 2.5–3 columns (~20–25%). Vertical spacing follows the mockup: 24–32px gaps between related blocks (e.g., breadcrumbs and header, header and body info) and 40–56px between major sections (parameters vs examples). Interactive or card-like blocks (parameters table, examples panel) have 16–24px inner padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the central content moderately spacious with comfortable line-height and generous margins around headings and sections, optimized for reading API docs. Tables (parameters) and side-by-side panels (examples) are denser but still have 8–12px row and control spacing to avoid visual clutter. Navigation in the left sidebar is moderately dense but clearly grouped with section labels and indentation.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Global flow follows a Z-pattern: users first see the global header (logo on left, search in the center, primary CTA on the right), then drop down to the left sidebar where the active 'API Reference' section is highlighted. From there, the eye moves into the central column, starting with breadcrumbs and the prominent endpoint header. After understanding the endpoint (title + description + method/path), attention naturally continues downward through request body and parameters. The strong visual block of the examples panel near the lower portion of the central column then draws the eye, with a secondary left-to-right movement inside it between the curl configurator (controls) and the resulting code snippet.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The page sits within a full-width light-themed documentation shell with a fixed global header at the very top. Below this header, the main content area is divided into three vertical regions: a fixed-width left sidebar for the API reference tree, a wide, centered reading column for the current endpoint, and a narrower right rail reserved for an 'On this page' TOC or related content. The left sidebar hugs the left edge, running almost the full height of the viewport and remaining visible as you scroll, while the central column is visually anchored beneath the header with generous top padding. At the top of the central column, subtle breadcrumbs lead into a bold, well-spaced endpoint header that clearly shows method and path. Underneath, the request body requirements, parameter table, and examples panel are stacked with consistent vertical spacing, each spanning the width of the central column. The examples panel forms a visually distinct, card-like block where configuration controls on the left and a live-updating code example on the right create a balanced, tool-like experience. The overall feel is modern, calm, and highly readable, with a clear hierarchy guiding the user from understanding the endpoint to configuring and copying a working request.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component acts as the main canvas beneath the fixed global header. It spans the full browser width and height, with a top padding of roughly 64–80px to clear the header. Inside, it establishes a three-column grid: a fixed-width left column for the API reference sidebar (about 260–280px, ~20–25% of the viewport), a fluid central column (max-width around 960–1040px, occupying roughly 50–55% of the viewport and centered horizontally between sidebars), and a narrower right column (~220–260px, ~20–25% width) aligned to the right edge. The central column is aligned such that its left edge sits just to the right of the sidebar’s inner edge, creating a clear vertical reading lane. Horizontal padding of 24px is applied at the page edges on smaller viewports, tightening the layout while preserving the relative proportions. Vertically, the container uses 24–32px gaps between logically related components (breadcrumbs → header → body info) and 40–56px gaps between major sections (parameters → examples). On smaller screens, the grid gracefully collapses: the left sidebar moves above or becomes collapsible, and the central content stretches toward full-width while preserving comfortable side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow, but defines the grid system and contains sticky children (e.g., the sidebar). It stretches to at least the full viewport height and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs sit at the very top of the central content column, directly under the global header’s bottom edge and aligned flush with the central column’s left margin. They occupy the full width of the central column but visually read as a subtle horizontal strip of text, set in smaller, muted typography with light spacing between items. There is about 8–12px of space above them (from the top padding of the page container) and 16–24px below them before the endpoint header begins. The breadcrumb trail is left-aligned; the right side of the central column remains empty at this vertical level, reinforcing the breadcrumbs’ tertiary, contextual nature. On narrower screens, the breadcrumbs maintain their position at the top of the content stack, wrapping onto multiple lines if needed but always staying visually light and unobtrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls away with the main content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sidebar occupies the fixed-width left column, starting directly beneath the global header and running down toward the bottom of the viewport. It is inset slightly from the very left edge of the browser with about 16–24px of horizontal padding between its content and the page edge, and 16–24px internal padding on the right to align cleanly with the central column. Inside, items are arranged in a vertical stack: at the top, a small group of top-level tabs (e.g., Documentation, API Reference, Blog) in a horizontal row; below that, vertically stacked navigation sections (Welcome, Guides, API, etc.) with section labels and indentations for nested links. The currently active section and endpoint (e.g., 'API: Create Batch Evaluation') are highlighted using a subtle background pill and brand-accent text. Vertical spacing between groups is about 16–24px, with 8–12px between individual links, giving a moderately dense yet readable hierarchy. The sidebar’s width remains constant as the viewport expands, while the central column grows; on smaller viewports, this sidebar may collapse to an off-canvas or top accordion, but in the main desktop layout it is a stable left rail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within its column: it is positioned so that its top edge sits just below the global header and remains visible while the central content scrolls, until content height is exhausted. It does not scroll independently from the page but maintains its relative position in the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The endpoint header sits immediately below the breadcrumbs within the central column, forming the primary visual anchor near the top of the page. It spans the full width of the central column. At the top of this block, a small category label such as 'API Reference' or 'POST' appears in a subtle brand-accent color, left-aligned and separated from the H1 by about 8–12px of vertical space. Below this, a large H1-style title like 'Create Batch Evaluation' stretches across roughly 70–80% of the column width, with generous line-height and 8–16px of space below. Directly beneath the H1, a short subtitle or one-sentence description runs almost full width in a slightly smaller, neutral text style. Under the subtitle, there may be a horizontally arranged line showing the HTTP method (e.g., a colored pill reading 'POST') next to the endpoint path (e.g., '/v1/evaluations/batch'), with about 8–12px of spacing between items and 16–24px of padding above the next section. Overall, this block uses 24–32px of vertical space, with a clear hierarchy that draws attention before the user scrolls further.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page, without stickiness.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component appears directly below the endpoint header, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space. It aligns with the central column’s left edge and spans its full width, presenting one or more paragraphs explaining the request body semantics, required fields, and payload shape. Text is laid out in a single column with comfortable line-length and line-height, matching the reading-friendly style of documentation pages. If there is a section label like 'Request Body', it is set as an H2-style heading with 16–24px of space above and 8–12px below before the descriptive paragraphs. Bullet lists or inline code tokens are used as needed, with 8–12px spacing between list items to maintain readability. The bottom of this block leaves a 24–32px gap before the parameters section, clearly separating conceptual explanation from tabular details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it flows naturally between the endpoint header and the parameters section without any special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parameters section is positioned beneath the endpoint-body-requirements, with 24–32px of vertical spacing separating them. It spans the full width of the central content column and starts with a clear section heading like 'Parameters' or 'Query Parameters', aligned to the left, with 8–12px of space before the table. The parameters themselves are displayed in a structured table or table-like layout with columns for name, location, type, required/optional, and description. The table has 16–24px padding around its edges, with each row having around 8–12px of vertical padding for legibility without feeling airy. Column headers may be slightly bolded with a light bottom border, and the table uses subtle horizontal lines or background banding to separate rows. The section as a whole appears as a moderately dense block in the center column, visually distinct from the more narrative body text above and the card-like examples panel below. There is a 32–40px gap at the bottom before the examples panel begins to indicate a major functional transition from parameter specs to interactive examples.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; scrolls as part of the main content flow. The table header may optionally be sticky within the table’s scroll area if it overflows vertically, but generally the entire section scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The examples panel is placed directly below the parameters section, with a generous 32–40px vertical gap to signal a new, hands-on portion of the page. It spans the full width of the central column and appears as a card-like container with a subtle border or elevated background, and 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. At the top of the panel, a horizontal header row contains a section title such as 'Examples' on the left and, on the right, language tabs (e.g., cURL, Python, JS) or variant tabs (e.g., 'Basic', 'Advanced'). This header row is aligned with the central column edges and separated from the internal content by 12–16px of vertical space. Below the header, the panel’s content area is divided into two vertical subcolumns: the curl configurator on the left and the example code block on the right. On a typical desktop viewport, the configurator occupies about 40–45% of the panel’s width and the code block about 55–60%, with a 16–24px gutter between them. The panel height adjusts to its content, but can allow internal scrolling for code or configurations if they exceed a comfortable height (e.g., 400–520px), maintaining a compact, tool-like appearance. On narrower screens, the panel content stacks vertically: the configurator appears above the code block, both full-width, with 24px spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static as a block within the central column, but internally may use a flex layout with responsive behavior (side-by-side on wide screens, stacked on narrow). The header of the panel remains at the top of this block and scrolls out of view with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This configurator lives inside the left half of the api-examples-panel’s content area. On desktop, it is a vertical column occupying roughly 40–45% of the panel width, starting just below the panel’s header and inset by the panel’s padding. It is aligned to the left edge of the panel’s inner content, ending at the internal vertical gutter before the code block. Within this column, form controls (dropdowns for model, text inputs for dataset ID or evaluation name, toggles for options like 'dry_run', etc.) are stacked vertically with 8–12px of spacing between related controls and 16–24px between logical groups. Group labels or subheadings are left-aligned with small, subtle text styles. The overall configurator height is designed to roughly align with the visible height of the code block for visual balance; if there are many controls, the configurator area itself might scroll internally while the code remains visible alongside. On smaller viewports where the panel stacks, the configurator becomes a full-width block at the top of the examples panel, with the same internal spacing but now spanning the full width of the central column, followed by the code block below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the examples panel but part of a responsive split layout. It may have an internal scroll if content height exceeds a comfortable threshold, while the overall panel remains in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The code block sits to the right of the configurator within the api-examples-panel on desktop-sized viewports, occupying about 55–60% of the panel’s width. Its left edge aligns with the right side of the configurator plus the 16–24px gutter. The code area is a monospaced, syntax-highlighted block showing a fully composed curl request (and potentially other languages via tabs). At the top of the code block, a slim header bar (inside the panel’s padding) can include a language label and a 'Copy' button aligned to the right. Below that, the main code area has 12–16px of inner padding, a slightly different background tone than the panel itself, and supports horizontal scrolling for long lines and vertical scrolling if the snippet is long. The code block’s height is tuned to match or slightly exceed the visible height of the configurator, creating a visually balanced side-by-side presentation. On narrow screens, this block appears directly below the configurator, full-width, separated by about 24px of vertical space, still respecting the same internal padding and scroll behaviors.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the examples panel, with its own scrollable area for overflow content. It participates in the responsive split layout (right side on wide screens, stacked below on narrow screens).

name: API: Create Dataset
title: Create Dataset API
description: Endpoint documentation page that dynamically loads the dataset creation API details, parameters, and language-specific examples, with copyable requests that stay in sync with the selected docs version.

core_features: Load endpoint metadata (HTTP method, API version, path template) and associated documentation for the current docPageId using getApiEndpointPageDetails.
Display a left API Reference sidebar with API groups and their endpoints, filtered to the current docs version using getApiReferenceSidebarData.
Render path and query parameter sections based on apiEndpoint.pathParams and apiEndpoint.queryParams returned by getApiEndpointPageDetails.

Render request body documentation for the dataset creation endpoint using the docPage content returned by getApiEndpointPageDetails, including which fields are required or optional as provided by the backend.
Render per-language API examples (request/response) for the endpoint using the examples array from getApiEndpointPageDetails, with language tabs and example-type (request/response) toggles.

Provide copy buttons on API examples that invoke copyApiExample with the corresponding apiExampleId so users can copy a version-appropriate example (e.g., v2 dataset creation) after changing the global docs version.

Provide breadcrumbs and a "Back to Datasets" control that navigate to the API Reference page (api_reference.html) while passing URL parameters that allow that page to preselect the Datasets API group.
Keep the selected language tab, example selection, and section focus in sync with URL parameters so the page state is deep-linkable and restorable.
user_workflows:
View the Create Dataset endpoint details:
  1. User opens api_datasets_create.html with a docPageId query parameter (e.g., api_datasets_create.html?docPageId={currentDocPageId}).
  2. The page shell reads docPageId from the URL.
  3. The page calls getApiEndpointPageDetails with that docPageId.
  4. The page calls getApiReferenceSidebarData to populate the API Reference sidebar for the current docs version.
  5. Once getApiEndpointPageDetails resolves, the endpoint header displays the HTTP method, apiVersion, and pathTemplate for the dataset creation endpoint.
  6. The parameters section displays pathParams and queryParams returned in apiEndpoint.pathParams and apiEndpoint.queryParams.
  7. The request body section renders field descriptions for the dataset creation payload from the docPage content supplied via getApiEndpointPageDetails.
  8. The examples section displays per-language example requests/responses from the examples array, defaulting to a language based on URL parameters or the first available language.

Switch example language and deep-link that state:
  1. User clicks a language tab (for example, a tab representing a particular ApiExample.language value) in the examples component.
  2. The examples component updates its internal selectedLanguage state.
  3. The page updates the lang query parameter in the URL to the selected language (api_datasets_create.html?docPageId={id}&lang={language}...), preserving other parameters.
  4. The examples component filters the examples array to those matching the selected language and exampleType and updates the displayed code block accordingly.
  5. If the URL is shared, another user opening that URL gets the same selected language after the initial data fetch, because the component re-reads the lang parameter from the URL on load.

Copy a cURL example for the current docs version:
  1. User ensures the global docs version selector (outside this page) is set to the desired version (e.g., a newer API version).
  2. The global docs version selector triggers a version-change event handled by the page shell, which re-invokes getApiEndpointPageDetails(docPageId) and getApiReferenceSidebarData so that the returned apiEndpoint and examples now correspond to the new docs version.
  3. In the examples panel, the user selects the language corresponding to cURL (via the language tabs) if it is not already selected, which also updates the lang query parameter.
  4. The user clicks the copy button associated with the visible cURL request example.
  5. The copy button handler calls copyApiExample with that example's id.
  6. On success, the UI copies the returned code string to the clipboard and optionally presents a non-blocking confirmation message using the message returned from copyApiExample.

Navigate back to the Datasets group in the API Reference:
  1. User clicks the "API Reference" breadcrumb or the "Back to Datasets" link in the page header.
  2. The page looks up the current endpoint's group by matching the current apiEndpoint.id against the endpoints returned from getApiReferenceSidebarData.
  3. The page constructs a navigation URL to api_reference.html including query parameters such as groupId (from the matched group.id) and optionally endpointId (from the current apiEndpoint.id) so the API Reference page can focus the Datasets group.
  4. Browser navigates to the constructed api_reference.html URL.

Jump to a specific documentation section via URL parameters:
  1. A user opens a deep link like api_datasets_create.html?docPageId={id}&section=request-body.
  2. The page shell reads the section query parameter.
  3. After getApiEndpointPageDetails resolves and the content is rendered, the page uses section to scroll to the corresponding section (e.g., path-parameters, query-parameters, request-body, examples).
  4. When the user clicks in-page navigation links (e.g., from a mini table of contents) the page updates the section parameter accordingly, ensuring the URL stays in sync with the current section.
interactions:
Sidebar navigation click:
  - Triggered when a user clicks an endpoint item in the API Reference sidebar.
  - Reads the target endpoint's docPageId from the sidebar data (from getApiReferenceSidebarData).
  - Navigates to api_datasets_create.html?docPageId={targetDocPageId}, clearing or preserving additional parameters as needed.
  - On navigation, the page re-runs getApiEndpointPageDetails and updates all bound components.

Breadcrumb click (to API Reference):
  - Triggered when the user clicks the breadcrumb item representing the API Reference.
  - Identifies the current endpoint's group via the groups/endpoints data from getApiReferenceSidebarData.
  - Navigates to api_reference.html?groupId={groupId} so the API Reference page can open the relevant group.

"Back to Datasets" link click:
  - Triggered when the user clicks the dedicated back link in the endpoint header.
  - Uses the same logic as the breadcrumb: resolves the endpoint's ApiGroup via getApiReferenceSidebarData.
  - Navigates to api_reference.html?groupId={groupId}.

Language tab click in examples:
  - Triggered when a user selects a language tab.
  - Updates internal selectedLanguage state and writes lang={language} into the URL query string, preserving docPageId and other parameters.
  - Filters the examples array for ApiExample.language == selectedLanguage and updates the visible snippet.

Example type toggle (request/response) click:
  - Triggered when a user toggles between viewing request and response for the current language.
  - Updates internal selectedExampleType state and writes exampleType={type} into the URL query string.
  - Filters examples by both language and exampleType and updates the displayed code.
  - If multiple examples exist for the same language and type, also updates exampleId in the URL when a specific example is chosen.

Copy example button click:
  - Triggered when a user clicks the copy button in the examples panel.
  - Retrieves the currently displayed ApiExample.id from component state.
  - Calls copyApiExample({ apiExampleId }).
  - If copyApiExample returns success == true, writes the returned code to the clipboard and shows a transient confirmation using the returned message.
  - If success == false, shows a non-blocking error message using the returned message.

Docs version change event (from global header):
  - Triggered when the global docs version selector changes outside this page.
  - The page shell listens to this event and, upon change, re-invokes getApiEndpointPageDetails with the current docPageId and getApiReferenceSidebarData.
  - Components update based on the new apiEndpoint.apiVersion, pathTemplate, and examples.
  - The URL query parameters remain unchanged so existing deep links still resolve, but the underlying examples and endpoint path now reflect the new docs version.

Section navigation click (internal TOC):
  - Triggered when the user clicks a link in an in-page section navigation (e.g., Request body, Path parameters, Examples).
  - Updates the section query parameter to the corresponding section identifier.
  - Scrolls the viewport to the selected section component once rendered.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters as the primary state mechanism, avoiding localStorage. The key parameters are:
- docPageId: Required. Identifies which DocPage and corresponding ApiEndpoint to fetch. On initial load, the page reads docPageId from the URL and passes it to getApiEndpointPageDetails. All internal navigation between endpoints updates this parameter.
- lang: Optional. Represents the selected example language (matching ApiExample.language). On load, the examples component reads lang and, if it matches at least one example, sets selectedLanguage accordingly. When a user clicks a language tab, the component updates lang in the URL using history APIs while preserving other parameters.
- exampleType: Optional. Represents whether the user is viewing request or response examples (matching ApiExample.exampleType). On load, the examples component reads this parameter and sets its selectedExampleType state. Toggling request/response updates exampleType in the URL.
- exampleId: Optional. Represents a specific ApiExample.id to show when there are multiple examples for the same language and type. If present, the examples component prioritizes this example; if the ID is not found in the fetched examples, it falls back to the first match for the selected language/type. Selecting a specific example updates exampleId in the URL.
- section: Optional. Encodes the currently focused section (e.g., path-parameters, query-parameters, request-body, examples). On load, after data fetch and render, the page scrolls to the section specified by this parameter if it matches a known section. Internal section navigation keeps this parameter updated.

When the global docs version changes (outside this page), the backend logic behind getApiReferenceSidebarData and getApiEndpointPageDetails is expected to respect the current docs version implicitly, so the same docPageId will yield version-appropriate ApiEndpoint and examples. The page does not change URL parameters when the version changes; instead it re-fetches data and lets components re-bind to the new ApiEndpoint.apiVersion, pathTemplate, and examples while preserving docPageId, lang, exampleType, exampleId, and section.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching, URL parameter parsing, and high-level layout for the Create Dataset endpoint page. Invokes getApiEndpointPageDetails and getApiReferenceSidebarData, then passes DocPage, ApiEndpoint, and ApiExample data into child components, and re-fetches data when the global docs version changes.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read docPageId, lang, exampleType, exampleId, section from URL query and call getApiEndpointPageDetails({ docPageId }) and getApiReferenceSidebarData().
onDocsVersionChange: Re-call getApiEndpointPageDetails with the current docPageId and re-call getApiReferenceSidebarData so all child components receive version-appropriate data.

onUrlChange: When navigation within the SPA updates query parameters (e.g., via language or section changes), re-parse relevant parameters and propagate them to children without reloading data unless docPageId changed.
onEndpointChange: When user navigates to a different endpoint via sidebar, parse new docPageId from the target link and trigger a new getApiEndpointPageDetails call.

components.functionality: Displays navigation breadcrumbs such as Home > API Reference > Create Dataset. Provides a clickable API Reference crumb that navigates back to api_reference.html with appropriate groupId derived from the current endpoint's ApiGroup.
event_handlers: onApiReferenceClick: Resolve the group associated with the current ApiEndpoint by matching apiEndpoint.id against endpoints returned by getApiReferenceSidebarData, then navigate to api_reference.html?groupId={group.id}.
onHomeClick: Navigate to the docs home page (outside this page's API concerns) without invoking additional data interfaces.

components.functionality: Provides a context-specific "Back to Datasets" control that returns the user to the Datasets group within the API Reference page.
event_handlers: onClick: Locate the ApiGroup representing the Datasets group by finding the group whose endpoints include the current apiEndpoint.id in the getApiReferenceSidebarData result, then navigate to api_reference.html?groupId={group.id}.

components.functionality: Renders the API Reference navigation tree using groups and endpoints returned by getApiReferenceSidebarData, filtered implicitly by the current docs version. Highlights the currently active endpoint based on the page's docPageId and allows the user to navigate to other endpoint pages.
event_handlers: onSidebarItemClick: When a user clicks an endpoint item, read that endpoint's docPageId and navigate to api_datasets_create.html?docPageId={targetDocPageId}, optionally preserving lang/exampleType/exampleId/section as needed or resetting them.
onGroupToggle: Expand or collapse a group in the sidebar; this is a local UI state change and does not call any interfaces.

components.functionality: Shows the endpoint name, HTTP method, API version, and path template for the Create Dataset endpoint. Also surfaces key metadata such as whether a request body is required.
event_handlers: onCopyPathClick: Copies the apiEndpoint.pathTemplate to the clipboard (using the value from getApiEndpointPageDetails) via a client-side clipboard call without needing additional interfaces.
onMethodBadgeClick: Optional no-op or local explanation tooltip toggle; no external interfaces.

components.functionality: Displays a table of path parameters for the endpoint, using apiEndpoint.pathParams when apiEndpoint.hasPathParams is true. Each parameter row indicates name, whether it is required, and a description.
event_handlers: onSectionVisible: When the section becomes visible in the viewport (e.g., via scroll), optionally update the section query parameter to indicate that path-parameters is the active section.
onParamNameClick: Optional local interaction (e.g., highlighting a parameter in the path template) that does not require external interfaces.

components.functionality: Shows a table of query parameters for the endpoint based on apiEndpoint.queryParams, marking each parameter as required or optional and showing descriptions.
event_handlers: onSectionVisible: Update section=query-parameters in the URL when this section is the primary visible block, enabling deep links to query parameter documentation.
onParamRowHover: Local UI effect only (e.g., highlight row); no interface calls.

components.functionality: Describes the request body schema for creating a dataset, including field names, required/optional designation, types, and descriptions. Uses the textual or structured content associated with the DocPage returned by getApiEndpointPageDetails, which is expected to encode request-body details for this endpoint.
event_handlers: onSectionVisible: When this section scrolls into focus, update section=request-body in the URL query parameters.
onFieldNameClick: Local interaction (e.g., copy field name); does not call any additional interfaces.

components.functionality: Displays language- and type-specific examples for the endpoint. It uses the examples array from getApiEndpointPageDetails, groups them by language and exampleType, exposes language tabs (e.g., for each ApiExample.language present), and allows toggling between request and response examples. It also coordinates the display of the currently selected example code block.
event_handlers: onLanguageTabClick: Set selectedLanguage in component state, update lang={language} in the URL, and re-compute visible examples based on ApiExample.language.
onExampleTypeToggle: Set selectedExampleType (request/response), update exampleType={type} in the URL, and re-compute visible examples based on ApiExample.exampleType.
onExampleSelectionChange: If multiple examples share the same language and type, allow the user to select one (e.g., from a dropdown) and update exampleId={ApiExample.id} in the URL.
onSectionVisible: Update section=examples in the URL when this block becomes the primary visible section.

components.functionality: Renders the currently selected ApiExample.code as a syntax-highlighted code block, based on the selected language and exampleType computed by the api-examples-panel component. It shows or hides the copy button based on ApiExample.hasCopyButton and respects ApiExample.isEditable for inline editing capabilities if enabled by the UI.
event_handlers: onCodeEdit: If ApiExample.isEditable is true, update the displayed code text locally; this does not call any interfaces and does not persist changes.
onCopyButtonClick: Delegates to the api-example-copy-button component or triggers its own copyApiExample call for the selected ApiExample.id.

components.functionality: Provides a dedicated control to copy the full content of the currently selected ApiExample using the copyApiExample interface, ensuring that the copied code reflects the current docs version and selected language.
event_handlers: onClick: Retrieve the currently selected ApiExample.id from the parent examples component, call copyApiExample({ apiExampleId: selectedExampleId }), and on success write the returned code to the clipboard and show feedback using the returned message.

components.functionality: Provides an in-page table of contents with links to Path parameters, Query parameters, Request body, and Examples. Keeps the section query parameter synchronized with the active section to allow deep-linking and scroll restoration.
event_handlers: onTocItemClick: When a user clicks a TOC item, update section={targetSectionId} in the URL and scroll to the corresponding component's anchor.
onSectionChangeFromScroll: Listen to scroll events (via the page shell) and mark the appropriate TOC item active when a section comes into view, also updating the section query parameter.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters (docPageId, lang, exampleType, exampleId, section) and propagates these values to other components. Ensures that state changes in components are synchronized into the URL without reloading the page and that initial load uses URL state to set component defaults.
event_handlers: onInitialLoad: Parse existing query parameters and initialize component states for language selection, example type, example selection, and section focus.

onStateChangeFromComponents: When child components signal a state change (e.g., language tab click, TOC item click), update the corresponding query parameters (lang, exampleType, exampleId, section) in the URL using history APIs.
onPopState: When the user navigates back/forward in the browser history, re-parse the URL parameters and update child component states accordingly without re-fetching data unless docPageId changed.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a three-column documentation layout inside a fixed global shell: a left API reference sidebar, a central endpoint content column, and a right in-page TOC. Within the central column, content flows top-to-bottom in this order: breadcrumbs, back-to-datasets link, endpoint header (method, path, title, description), then the parameter sections (path, query), request body docs, and finally the examples panel.
