Within the examples panel, language tabs sit above a code block with a copy button in the top-right corner.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a three-column documentation layout inside a fixed global shell: a left API reference sidebar, a central endpoint content column, and a right in-page TOC. Within the central column, content flows top-to-bottom in this order: breadcrumbs, back-to-datasets link, endpoint header (method, path, title, description), then the parameter sections (path, query), request body docs, and finally the examples panel. Within the examples panel, language tabs sit above a code block with a copy button in the top-right corner.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On desktop, the left sidebar occupies about 20–22% of the total width, the central content column about 56–60%, and the right TOC about 20–22%. The global header spans 100% width at the top with modest vertical height. The central content has a max-width feel (roughly 960–1120px for the combined three-column interior) with generous margins, while sidebars are narrower but tall, running nearly the full viewport height below the header.
layout_strategies.content_density: The central content is moderately spacious with generous line-height and 24–32px gaps between major content blocks and 16–24px between tightly related elements. Text blocks are not crowded, and tables are padded with 16–24px internal padding. Sidebars are slightly denser: navigation items stack closely but still keep 8–12px vertical breathing room for scannability. Nothing touches edges; all blocks have at least one base unit (8px) of internal padding and 16–24px external separation.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the fixed top header (logo left, search centered, primary CTA right), then drops into the central content: breadcrumbs and back link first, then the endpoint header as the primary anchor. After understanding the endpoint at a glance, the eye naturally scans down the central column to parameters and request body. As the user scrolls, the right-hand TOC column remains in peripheral view as a vertical map of sections, creating a secondary left-to-right reading loop between the central content and right TOC. The left sidebar provides global API navigation and is typically consulted before or between endpoints, not continuously while reading.

layout.overall_description: The page sits inside a full-width, fixed-header documentation shell. Below the header, the viewport is split into three vertical regions on a light, minimalist canvas: a left API navigation rail, a wide central reading column, and a narrower right TOC panel. The left sidebar anchors the overall context with API sections and endpoints arranged in grouped vertical lists. The central column is the focal reading area: it begins with subtle breadcrumbs and a back-to-datasets link, then presents a bold endpoint header that shows the HTTP method and path, followed by a short explanatory description. Beneath this, clearly separated blocks describe path parameters, query parameters, and the request body, each with its own heading and lightly bordered table layout. At the bottom of the central stack sits a prominent examples panel with language tabs and a dark, copyable code block that runs nearly the full width of the column. On the right, the in-page TOC mirrors the central sections with a tidy vertical list, staying aligned as the user scrolls. Overall, the layout feels clean, structured, and optimized for scanning: the main content is easy to read, the sidebars provide orientation without competing for attention, and interactive elements like tabs and copy buttons are placed where the eye naturally lands during usage.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell spans the full browser viewport, providing a fixed-height global header at the very top and a three-column content grid below. The header runs edge-to-edge horizontally with a light background: logo and product name on the far left, a centered search bar occupying roughly 40–50% of the header width, and primary CTAs (e.g., sign in, user menu) on the far right. Directly beneath the header, the shell defines a max-width central area centered on the page; inside this area, the horizontal space is divided into three columns: a left navigation column (~20–22% width), a central content column (~56–60% width), and a right TOC column (~20–22% width). Vertical spacing between the header and the first row of content is around 24–32px, giving breathing room. The shell manages the overall scroll behavior: the main content and sidebars scroll within the viewport, while the header remains fixed at the top. Internal padding of 24px on the left and right edges of the content area ensures that none of the columns touches the browser edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The shell itself is static but contains a fixed-position global header and a scrollable three-column layout. It can also reserve sticky behavior zones for the sidebars within the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs are positioned in the central column as the very first element below the global header. They are left-aligned, starting at the same left edge as the main H1 title that appears below them. Horizontally, the breadcrumbs line spans only the text content; it does not need to stretch beyond roughly 60–70% of the central column width, maintaining a subtle, unobtrusive presence. Vertically, there is a 24–32px gap between the bottom of the header and the top of the breadcrumbs, and roughly 4–8px between the breadcrumbs and the back-to-datasets link below. The text is small and neutral, with light separators (e.g., chevrons) between levels, visually sitting in the upper-left of the reading area as a contextual hint without drawing too much attention.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the central content; it is not sticky. It marks the top of the endpoint page’s content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-datasets link sits directly under the breadcrumbs within the central column, aligned to the same left margin. It appears as a small, subtle text link with an optional left-pointing arrow icon, spanning only as wide as its label text (typically under 20% of the central column width). There is a tight vertical gap of about 4–8px above it from the breadcrumbs and a slightly larger gap of 12–16px separating it from the prominent endpoint header that follows below. This creates a compact contextual zone at the very top of the content: breadcrumbs on top, then the back link, then the main endpoint information, all left-aligned within the central reading column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the main content. It behaves like a regular inline element at the top of the central column, without sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API reference sidebar occupies the entire height of the left column beneath the global header. It is anchored to the left side of the content area and runs from just below the header down to near the bottom of the viewport, with 24px of padding from the top of its own column and 16–24px padding on its inner sides. Within this column, navigation is arranged in a vertical stack: at the top, a small row of top-level documentation tabs (e.g., Documentation, API Reference, Blog) spans nearly the full width of the sidebar; below that, grouped navigation sections (like 'Welcome', 'Datasets', 'Evaluations') are stacked with 16–24px gaps between groups. Each group title is left-aligned, with its nested links indented by about 12–16px, showing a clear hierarchy. The currently active endpoint is highlighted with a pill-like background or a colored left border. The sidebar typically consumes about 20–22% of the total page width, ensuring that its line lengths remain short and scannable. There is a clear gutter of 24–32px between the right edge of this sidebar and the start of the central content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sidebar is effectively sticky within its column: it remains visible as the user scrolls, pinned starting just below the fixed header. Internally, its navigation list scrolls only if its height exceeds the viewport, while its top-level heading and tabs stay in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The endpoint header is the first major content block in the central column and acts as the primary visual anchor. It spans the full width of the central column, with 16–24px of internal padding on left and right (aligned with the column edges) but no heavy card border; it sits on the same light background as the page. At the top of this block, a small label row might show the HTTP method (e.g., POST) in a colored pill followed by the endpoint path (e.g., /v1/datasets) in mono-spaced text, all left-aligned and occupying roughly 70–80% of the column width. Just below, a large, bold H1 title like 'Create Dataset' stretches across most of the central column, with a generous vertical gap of 8–12px between the path row and the title. Under the title, a concise subtitle or one-line description sits with 8–12px spacing, followed by a slightly larger 16–24px gap before the first parameter section begins. The overall block height consumes roughly the upper 20–25% of the initial viewport in the central column, making the endpoint understandable at a glance when the page loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the central content. It defines the starting anchor for the in-page TOC and may serve as the top section that the TOC highlights when the page first loads.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The path parameters section appears directly below the endpoint header in the central column, separated by a vertical gap of about 24px. It spans the full width of the central column and is structured as a block with a section heading at the top and a parameters table beneath. The heading (e.g., 'Path parameters') is left-aligned and occupies only as much width as the text, followed by an 8–12px gap before the table. The table itself is horizontally aligned to match the central column’s left and right edges and uses a light border or subtle row separators, with 12–16px vertical padding in each row and 16–24px of padding left and right inside the table. Overall, this section typically takes up about 15–20% of the central column height above the fold (depending on parameter count), remaining visually compact but clearly separated by white space from both the endpoint header above and the following sections below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal scroll flow. It is an anchor target for the right-hand TOC, so its top is aligned to a predictable vertical rhythm that works well with sticky TOC highlighting.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The query parameters section sits directly under the path parameters section, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap to signal a new conceptual block. Like the path parameters, it spans the full width of the central column and follows the same internal structure: a left-aligned section heading, a small gap, then a table listing query parameters. The table width, padding, and typography are consistent with the path parameters table, creating a visual rhythm of stacked, similarly styled blocks. This section’s height scales with the number of parameters but is generally moderate, not exceeding the width of the central column. There is another 24–32px gap below this block before the request body section begins, allowing the user to easily distinguish between parameter types as they scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the central content. It serves as another scroll anchor referenced by the TOC, aligning with the same horizontal and vertical rhythm as adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The request body section appears below the query parameters, separated by a slightly larger vertical gap of about 32px to emphasize its importance and conceptual shift. It occupies the full width of the central column and may be one of the taller content blocks. At the top, a clear section heading (e.g., 'Request body') is left-aligned, followed by a short explanatory paragraph or note with 8–12px spacing, and then a structured representation of the body schema—either as a nested table, property list, or JSON-like schema view. This content is contained within a lightly bordered or subtly shaded area with 16–24px internal padding, visually resembling a documentation card while still aligning flush with the central column’s edges. Vertical spacing inside this section is consistent: 8–12px between heading and description, 12–16px between description and schema, and 16–24px between schema items. Below this block, a 32–40px gap separates it from the examples panel to create a clear sense that the descriptive documentation is complete before the user reaches the executable examples.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the central content and acts as a major anchor for the TOC. Its top edge aligns with the other section headings to keep scrolling and TOC highlighting predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The examples panel is the final major block in the central column and is visually prominent due to its dark code background contrasting against the light page. It is positioned directly beneath the request body section with a generous 32–40px vertical gap, spanning the full width of the central column. The panel itself behaves like a contained card: a horizontal tab strip at the top allows the user to switch between examples (e.g., 'cURL', 'Python', 'JavaScript'), and the selected tab’s content appears below as a code block. The tabs run from the left edge of the column across about 60–80% of its width, with 12–16px internal horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding per tab, leaving some whitespace to the right. Below the tabs, the code block fills nearly the entire width of the panel, with 16–24px padding inside its dark background. Vertically, the panel occupies a sizable portion of the lower viewport—often 30–40% of the visible height—so that most of the code is visible without scrolling within the code block itself. A small, subtle footer margin of 32–48px below the panel provides breathing room before the natural end of the page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content. Within the panel, tab content changes without affecting the page scroll position. The panel is an important interaction zone that works closely with URL state synchronization so that selected examples are reflected in the URL.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The example code block sits inside the examples panel, directly beneath the language tabs, and is the dominant visual element within that panel. It uses a dark background contrasting with the light page—extending from the left edge of the central column nearly to its right edge, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. The code lines are monospaced with comfortable line-height, and the block height is tuned so that a typical request fits mostly within the visible area, minimizing vertical scrolling inside the code block. When the content is longer, a subtle internal scrollbar appears. The top-right corner of this code block is reserved space for the copy button, which overlays or sits within the code block’s header area without obscuring the code itself. Horizontally, the code text stays within a safe reading width, never reaching the page edge due to the internal padding, maintaining the minimalist, developer-focused aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is a scrollable region within the examples panel when code exceeds the available height, but otherwise remains static. It responds to tab changes by updating its content without moving its position on the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The copy button is a small, discrete control positioned at the top-right corner of the example code block. It sits within the code block’s boundaries, inset by about 12–16px from the top and right edges, either floating over the dark background or anchored to a faint header strip above the first line of code. The button is compact—roughly 32–40px wide and 24–32px tall—with 8–12px internal padding. It uses a subtle border or faint fill that contrasts slightly against the dark code background but does not overpower the content. Visually, it occupies only a small fraction (under 10%) of the code block’s width and height, making it easy to find once noticed but not a dominant focal point. The alignment ensures it doesn’t overlap important code lines, leaving at least 8–12px clearance before the first line of text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It remains fixed relative to the top-right of the code block’s visible area; when the code block scrolls internally, the button stays pinned at the top-right corner of the block, not scrolling out of view with the code lines.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in-page TOC occupies the entire right column beneath the global header, forming a narrow vertical navigation panel aligned with the top of the central content. It begins roughly level with the breadcrumbs and endpoint header in the central column, with 24px of top padding within its column. The TOC content is a vertical list of links corresponding to sections in the central column (e.g., 'Overview', 'Path parameters', 'Query parameters', 'Request body', 'Examples'). Top-level sections are left-aligned within the TOC column, with sub-items indented by 8–12px, using a slightly smaller or lighter type to show hierarchy. The list runs downwards with 8–12px vertical spacing between items, taking up about 20–22% of the total page width. As the user scrolls, the TOC stays visible within the right column: the active section is highlighted with a subtle accent color or a thin left border, mirroring the user’s position in the central content. A 24–32px horizontal gap separates the TOC from the central column, creating a clear gutter and preventing visual crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar: it remains fixed within the viewport starting just below the global header while the central content scrolls. The internal list may scroll independently if it exceeds the viewport height, but for this page it is typically fully visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component is non-visual and occupies no explicit on-screen space; instead, it is conceptually layered over the page shell and main content. It tracks user interactions with key components—such as the selected example tab in the examples panel and the current section in view as indicated by the central scroll position—and encodes these states into the URL (e.g., as query parameters or hash fragments). Spatially, it is associated with the central column, the examples panel, and the in-page TOC: when the user clicks a section in the TOC or scrolls to a new section, the corresponding anchor moves into view and the URL is updated. Similarly, changing the language tab within the examples panel updates the URL, keeping the layout unchanged but ensuring the state can be restored or shared. Its presence is felt only through the way the page behaves rather than through any visible element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible but persistent, it is logically fixed to the page shell: it does not move with scroll and instead reacts to the spatial behavior of other components, updating the URL and syncing on load without affecting layout.

name: Concepts Overview
title: Concepts Overview
description: Provides a structured overview of core concepts for the AI evaluation platform and routes users to detailed conceptual documentation pages.

core_features: Load Concepts overview metadata, sidebar items, and conceptual topic list using the getConceptsOverviewPage interface.
Render a Concepts sidebar that links to related conceptual documentation pages, including the Metrics overview page and other concepts.
Display a list of conceptual topics (with titles and summaries) that guide users to which concepts to read next before using APIs or SDKs.
Synchronize the currently focused or highlighted concept topic with URL parameters so users can deep-link into a specific concept from elsewhere in the docs.
Provide navigation interactions that, on click, route users from the Concepts overview to detailed concept pages (e.g., Metrics overview) using URL-based navigation.

user_workflows.name: Browse concepts overview on initial page load
steps: User navigates to concepts.html (via header navigation link or direct link).
Page loader component parses URL parameters (e.g., topicId, sidebar) to determine initial UI state (selected topic, sidebar expanded/collapsed).
Page loader calls getConceptsOverviewPage with no parameters.
When the response resolves, the page stores docPage, sidebarItems, and conceptTopics in in-memory state for this view.
The sidebar component renders sidebarItems as a navigable list of conceptual links, highlighting the link that corresponds to the current docPage.id when applicable.
The topic list component renders conceptTopics (title and summary) as a clickable list of conceptual entries.
If a topicId URL parameter is present, the topic list component highlights the matching topic and scrolls it into view.

user_workflows.name: Navigate to Metrics overview (or another concept) via sidebar
steps: User clicks a sidebar item displayed by the Concepts sidebar component.
The sidebar component reads the clicked SidebarItem.targetPageId and determines whether the target is the current page or another concepts-related page.

If the target is not the current docPage.id, the component constructs a navigation URL based on the docs routing convention, embedding the targetPageId as a URL parameter (for example, when the target is the Metrics overview, the URL is constructed to concepts_metrics.html with a query parameter that includes the SidebarItem.targetPageId).
The browser navigates to the constructed URL; the target page then uses its own assigned interface to load its content.
If the clicked item points back to the current page (targetPageId matches docPage.id), the page does not navigate but updates URL parameters (e.g., clears topicId) and re-renders the selected state.

user_workflows.name: Navigate to a specific concept (e.g., Metrics overview) via topic list
steps: User scans the conceptTopics list on the Concepts overview page.
User clicks a topic entry rendered by the topic list component.
The topic list component retrieves the topic's targetDocPageId from the conceptTopics array.

If the topic refers to a different conceptual page, the component constructs a navigation URL to that page, embedding targetDocPageId as a URL parameter; when the topic corresponds to the Metrics overview, the URL is constructed to concepts_metrics.html with the topic’s targetDocPageId in the query string.
The browser navigates to the new page; that page then loads its content using its own interface.
If the topic refers to the current docPage.id, the component only updates the URL topicId parameter and the in-page highlight state without triggering a full navigation.

user_workflows.name: Deep-link into a specific concept topic within the Concepts overview
steps: User arrives at concepts.html with a topicId URL parameter (for example, from a link on the Documentation Home page).
The page loader parses topicId from the URL before calling getConceptsOverviewPage.
After getConceptsOverviewPage returns, the topic list component searches the conceptTopics array for a topic whose targetDocPageId or another agreed identifier matches topicId.
If a matching topic is found, the component sets it as the selected topic, highlights it, and scrolls it into view.
If no matching topic is found, the component falls back to default behavior (e.g., no topic highlighted) while leaving the URL unchanged.

behavior: Navigates to concepts.html without additional URL parameters, unless the navigation source explicitly provides them.
On load, the page calls getConceptsOverviewPage and renders sidebarItems and conceptTopics.
Reads SidebarItem.targetPageId from the clicked item.
If the targetPageId differs from the current docPage.id, constructs a new URL for that documentation page with a query parameter referencing targetPageId, then triggers navigation.

If the target page is the Metrics overview, the constructed URL points to concepts_metrics.html with the targetPageId as a URL parameter so that the Metrics overview page can load the correct DocPage.
If the targetPageId equals the current docPage.id, prevents full-page navigation and only updates URL parameters related to in-page state (e.g., clears topicId) and updates selection highlighting.

interactions.interaction: Click a concept topic in the topic list
behavior: Reads the clicked topic object's targetDocPageId from the conceptTopics array.
If the targetDocPageId is different from the current docPage.id, constructs a URL for the target conceptual page embedding targetDocPageId in the query string and navigates the browser to that URL.
If the clicked topic corresponds to the Metrics overview, the URL is constructed to concepts_metrics.html with the appropriate query parameter; navigation then occurs.

If the targetDocPageId equals the current docPage.id, the component only updates the topicId URL parameter to the identifier of the selected topic, updates selection highlighting, and optionally scrolls the topic into view.

interactions.interaction: URL parameter change via in-page navigation (no full reload)
behavior: When the page updates URL parameters using the History API (e.g., changing topicId while staying on concepts.html), the components that depend on those parameters (such as the topic list and sidebar) re-read the current URL.
Components adjust their selected/highlighted state based on the updated parameters without re-calling getConceptsOverviewPage, as the underlying data is already loaded.

url_parameters.name: id
url_parameters.purpose: Identifies the current DocPage to the routing layer when navigating to or from Concepts-related pages. While getConceptsOverviewPage does not require parameters, the id parameter is used for consistency across the docs site and for linking from other pages.
behavior: When navigating to concepts.html, the router may include an id parameter corresponding to docPage.id.
The page can compare the id parameter with the docPage.id returned by getConceptsOverviewPage to confirm that the loaded page matches the URL context.
If id is missing or does not match the returned docPage.id, the page still renders using getConceptsOverviewPage’s response but can avoid relying on id for selection logic.

url_parameters.name: topicId
url_parameters.purpose: Encodes which conceptual topic within conceptTopics should be treated as selected or focused when the Concepts Overview page loads.
behavior: On initial load, before rendering, the page parses topicId.

After getConceptsOverviewPage returns, the topic list component looks up the topic whose identifier matches topicId; this identifier can be derived from targetDocPageId or another consistent field agreed in the implementation.
If a matching topic is found, that topic is highlighted and scrolled into view.
If topicId does not match any current topic, no specific topic is selected.
When the user selects a different topic within the same page (without leaving concepts.html), the page updates topicId in the URL using the History API to allow deep-linking without a full reload.

url_parameters.name: sidebar
url_parameters.purpose: Controls whether the Concepts sidebar is initially expanded or collapsed (helpful for narrow viewports or embedded contexts).
behavior: On load, the page reads the sidebar parameter; recognized values control the initial open/closed state of the sidebar.
Toggling the sidebar open/closed in the UI updates the sidebar parameter in the URL using the History API.
The sidebar state is not persisted in localStorage or any other client storage; it is solely derived from and written to the URL.

url_parameters.name: from
url_parameters.purpose: Optionally indicates which section of the docs the user navigated from (e.g., Documentation Home, API Reference) so the Concepts overview can adjust contextual hints.
behavior: On load, the page reads from but does not require it.
Components may use from to conditionally show contextual hints or recommended next topics (using only the already loaded conceptTopics) without altering the underlying data.
The from parameter is purely informational and does not change which data is fetched, since getConceptsOverviewPage takes no parameters.
When the page first loads or is reloaded, it immediately calls getConceptsOverviewPage before rendering core content.
While awaiting the response, the page can render structural placeholders (e.g., an empty sidebar and topic list) without hardcoded content.
No additional data requests are made from this page.
If getConceptsOverviewPage fails, the page displays a generic error state that does not include static or hardcoded domain data.
The error state allows the user to retry the load action, which simply calls getConceptsOverviewPage again.
URL parameters remain unchanged; they are re-used if the user retries loading.

components.functionality: Initializes the Concepts Overview page by parsing URL parameters, calling getConceptsOverviewPage once, and distributing the returned docPage, sidebarItems, and conceptTopics to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onLoadSuccess
onLoadError

components.functionality: Renders a navigable sidebar for conceptual documentation using sidebarItems, manages selected state based on current URL and docPage, and handles navigation to other conceptual pages (including the Metrics overview).
event_handlers: onClickSidebarItem
onToggleSidebar

components.functionality: Displays the conceptTopics list with titles and summaries, highlights the topic indicated by the topicId URL parameter, and handles navigation when a user chooses a topic that links to another conceptual page (such as the Metrics overview).
event_handlers: onClickTopicItem

components.functionality: Provides minimal breadcrumb context (e.g., Documentation > Concepts > Current page) using docPage metadata, and offers a clickable link back to higher-level sections using URL navigation without invoking additional APIs.
event_handlers: onClickBreadcrumbItem

components.functionality: Synchronizes the global header navigation with the current page by using docPage.category to indicate that the Concepts section is active; does not fetch data on its own and only reads from already loaded docPage.
Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters (id, topicId, sidebar, from) and exposes state changes to other components without using localStorage or additional network requests.
event_handlers: onUrlChange
onStateUpdate

components.functionality: Listens for load failures from concepts-page-loader, displays a non-domain-specific error message, and exposes a retry interaction that triggers a new call to getConceptsOverviewPage.
event_handlers: onClickRetry

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the documentation template from the mockup: a fixed global header at the very top, then a three-column content area underneath. In the main center column, the vertical order is: breadcrumb navigation at the top, then the page category label (e.g., “Concepts”), the H1 page title (“Concepts Overview”), a short subtitle/lead line, a brief introductory paragraph, and then the concepts topic list as the main body content. The left column hosts the persistent documentation sidebar navigation. The right column remains available for future in-page navigation, but on this specific page it stays visually quiet or empty. Cross-cutting infrastructure components (navigation state sync, URL state manager) do not have visible UI but influence the states of the sidebar and main content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the documentation template from the mockup: a fixed global header at the very top, then a three-column content area underneath. In the main center column, the vertical order is: breadcrumb navigation at the top, then the page category label (e.g., “Concepts”), the H1 page title (“Concepts Overview”), a short subtitle/lead line, a brief introductory paragraph, and then the concepts topic list as the main body content. The left column hosts the persistent documentation sidebar navigation. The right column remains available for future in-page navigation, but on this specific page it stays visually quiet or empty. Cross-cutting infrastructure components (navigation state sync, URL state manager) do not have visible UI but influence the states of the sidebar and main content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content area beneath the global header is divided approximately into a 12-column grid: about 22% width for the left sidebar, 56% for the central content, and 22% reserved for the right side. The left sidebar is fixed-width (around 240–280px on a typical desktop viewport), the center column is fluid and optimized for reading (around 720–840px max), and the right side occupies the remaining width. The breadcrumb, error banner, and loader span the main content width (with the loader optionally covering the full viewport). All vertical spacing follows the 8px base unit: 24–32px between structural blocks and 16–24px padding within cards and list items.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page preserves a moderately spacious, reading-first density. The center column has generous line-height, ample white space between the heading stack and the topic list, and clear separation between concept topic items (e.g., 24–32px gaps). The left sidebar navigation is denser but still has 8–12px vertical padding around each item and grouping labels separated by 16–24px. The error banner uses compact but readable padding (around 12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) and is separated from the content below by roughly 16–24px.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual attention starts in the global header (logo → central search → right-side CTAs), then drops to the left sidebar to confirm context, then into the main content column where the breadcrumb and the H1 title anchor understanding. From there the eye flows down through the subtitle and intro into the concepts topic list, scanning individual concept entries. If present, an error banner interrupts just under the header, ensuring visibility before users read the main content. Side effects like navigation state sync and URL management are invisible but ensure the left sidebar and URL accurately reflect the user’s position as they move through the concepts list.

layout.overall_description: Under a fixed global header, the page opens into a three-column documentation layout stretching across the viewport. On the left, a fixed-width sidebar runs from just below the header to the bottom of the screen, listing documentation sections in a vertical navigation with subtle group labels and an active state highlighting the current “Concepts” area. The central column, slightly offset from the sidebar with a comfortable horizontal gutter, carries the main reading experience: a slim breadcrumb at the top, followed by a concise page label, a prominent “Concepts Overview” title, a one-line supporting subtitle, and a short paragraph framing how concepts fit into the AI evaluation platform. Below this heading stack, the concepts topic list fills the rest of the column, each topic item spaced so they read like a clean directory of cards or rows. The right side of the layout is reserved as an optional secondary column, remaining visually quiet on this page to maintain focus on the overview list. A slim error banner, when triggered, appears under the global header and above all three columns, spanning the main content area and pushing content down. During initial loading or route transitions, a soft, dimmed overlay with a centered spinner or progress indicator covers the content area, maintaining the underlying three-column structure while assuring users that data is being fetched. Invisible infrastructure components keep the sidebar’s active states and the URL synchronized with the user’s current concept selection, preserving a cohesive, modern documentation experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay that covers the full viewport, sitting on top of the global header, left sidebar, main content, and any banners. It is anchored to the viewport edges (top, right, bottom, left at 0) so it always fills 100% of the screen regardless of scroll position. At the visual center of the screen, a small loading indicator (spinner or progress icon) is placed, with about 24–32px of padding around it to maintain breathing space. The underlying three-column layout remains visible but dimmed or blurred, reinforcing that the page context is loading rather than completely changing. When loading is partial (e.g., only the concepts list is fetching), the loader may instead be scoped to the central content column, spanning from just below the breadcrumb to the bottom of the viewport and aligning with the width of the main content column while leaving the left sidebar visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay that covers the viewport or central content column during loading; appears above all other components and does not affect underlying layout sizing, only interaction.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The concepts sidebar is positioned on the left side of the page, directly below the global header, occupying roughly 22% of the viewport width (about 240–280px on a standard desktop screen). It extends from just under the header to the bottom edge of the viewport and can scroll independently when its content exceeds the vertical space. Inside, the navigation items are arranged in a vertical stack: at the top, a shallow horizontal segment for high-level tabs (e.g., Documentation / API Reference / Blog) spans the sidebar width with 16–24px horizontal padding. Below that, section labels like “Welcome” or “Concepts” appear as subtly styled headings, each followed by indented child links that have about 8–12px vertical spacing between them. The current page’s item (e.g., “Concepts Overview”) is highlighted with a brand-accent pill or background, aligning its left edge with other links but visually distinguished. A clear gutter (16–24px) separates the right edge of the sidebar from the central content column, ensuring the main content doesn’t feel cramped against the navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Left-aligned, fixed-width column that is sticky beneath the global header; its content area scrolls vertically when needed while the overall sidebar frame remains anchored.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The concepts topic list sits in the central content column as the main body of the page. It begins directly beneath the introductory text (after the page label, H1 title, subtitle, and overview paragraph), with a generous top margin of roughly 32px to visually separate the intro from the list. The topic list is constrained to the same max width as the article text (around 720–840px) and is horizontally centered in the available center column space, leaving consistent gutters between it and the left sidebar and the quiet right column. Each concept entry appears as either a full-width row or a light card spanning 100% of the central column’s width, with 16–24px internal padding and a vertical gap of 16–24px between items. Titles within each topic item align with the H1’s left edge, with short descriptions beneath using the same readable line-height as body text. If the topic list is grouped (e.g., “Core Concepts”, “Advanced Concepts”), group headings follow the same pattern as section headings in documentation: a heading label, then the list of concept rows underneath. The list scrolls naturally with the page; as the user scrolls downward, the topic list extends below the fold while the sidebar remains visible on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the central content column, participating in normal document flow; expands vertically as topics are added, driving the main page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is positioned at the very top of the central content column, directly underneath the global header and horizontally aligned with the main text block. It spans the full width of the central column but remains visually light: a compact horizontal line of text and separators (e.g., “Docs / Concepts / Overview”), with about 8–12px of vertical padding above and below. A vertical gap of approximately 16–24px separates the breadcrumb from the page category label and H1 title below, so the heading stack feels clearly anchored but not crowded. The left edge of the breadcrumb aligns with the left edge of the main content text, maintaining a consistent text column. It does not encroach into the left sidebar area and leaves a clear horizontal gutter between itself and the sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the main content flow; it scrolls away with the central column content and does not stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header state sync component has no visible UI footprint. Conceptually, it sits alongside the global header and the left sidebar, orchestrating which top-level tab (e.g., Documentation) and which sidebar group (e.g., Concepts) appear active based on the current page. Spatially, it can be considered to occupy a zero-height, zero-width layer tied to the top of the page structure. Its effect is that when the Concepts Overview page is active, the appropriate global header item and the corresponding sidebar section are highlighted, reinforcing the user’s location without adding any new visual elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, layout-neutral behavior; it attaches logically to the global header and sidebar but does not consume space or affect the flow of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is also invisible and does not occupy any visual space. It operates in the background within the page’s main layout context, ensuring that interactions with the concepts topic list (such as clicking into a specific concept) reflect appropriately in the browser’s URL and history. Conceptually, it sits at the root of the central content column, watching changes in selection state and synchronizing them with the URL. This allows deep-linking to particular concepts without adding any additional UI elements or changing the physical arrangement of components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, layout-neutral; it runs in the background alongside the main content without affecting spatial positioning of any component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The load error banner appears near the top of the page content area whenever there is a problem loading the concepts data. It is positioned directly beneath the global header and above the three-column layout, effectively spanning the full content width from the left edge of the sidebar to the right edge of the right column. Internally, the banner has 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, with text aligned to the same left margin as the central content text. A subtle but noticeable background color (e.g., soft red or amber) differentiates it from the white content area, and its bottom edge has a 16–24px margin separating it from the breadcrumb and top of the central content. When present, this banner pushes the entire content area (sidebar and central column) downward, maintaining the grid alignment and not overlapping the sidebar or main text. On narrow viewports, it remains full-width across the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that appears in the normal flow just under the global header; it affects vertical layout by pushing other components down but remains fixed in place as the user scrolls.

name: Metrics Overview
title: Metrics Overview
description: Displays all evaluation metrics available in the platform, with categorization, billing-related indicators, and links to each metric’s detail page.

core_features: Fetch and display dynamic documentation metadata and the full metrics list using the getMetricsOverviewPage interface.
Group and filter metrics by their category field so users can focus on metrics relevant to their task (for example, classification, generation, latency, quality, cost, or other).
Highlight metrics related to billing using the billingMetricIds list and each Metric.isTokenBillingRelated flag, including the token-based billing metric.
Provide a filter or quick toggle to show only billing-related metrics.
Support text-based filtering of metrics by name, identifier, or shortDescription to quickly locate a specific metric.
Allow users to click any metric row or card to navigate to its corresponding metric_detail.html page with the metricId URL parameter set.
Expose a URL-driven state model (query parameters) so that filter settings and search queries are shareable and restorable on page reload.
user_workflows: Discover metrics for a specific use case: (1) User navigates to concepts_metrics.html from the Concepts section. (2) Page calls getMetricsOverviewPage and loads all metrics. (3) User selects a metric category from the filter toolbar to narrow the list. (4) User optionally refines via search. (5) User clicks a metric to open metric_detail.html?metricId=<selected metric id>.

Identify billing-related metrics: (1) User opens concepts_metrics.html. (2) Billing highlight panel indicates how many metrics are related to billing, based on billingMetricIds and Metric.isTokenBillingRelated. (3) User clicks a “Show billing-related metrics only” control in the panel or toolbar. (4) URL updates to reflect the billing-only view. (5) Filtered list shows only billing-related metrics, including the token-based billing metric. (6) User clicks the token-based billing metric entry to navigate to metric_detail.html with the corresponding metricId.

Search and deep link to a metric: (1) User enters a search term in the search input (for example, a metric identifier or part of its display name). (2) User submits the search; URL query parameter q is updated. (3) Page filters the metrics list using q, against Metric.id, Metric.displayName, and Metric.shortDescription. (4) User copies the page URL and shares it. (5) Another user opens the link; on load, the page reads q from the URL and automatically applies the same search filter, showing the same subset of metrics.

Share a pre-filtered metrics view by category and billing mode: (1) User chooses a category in the category filter and enables or disables the billing-only toggle. (2) The page updates the category and billing query parameters in the URL. (3) User copies the URL and shares it. (4) When the URL is opened elsewhere, the page reads the category and billing parameters, calls getMetricsOverviewPage, and then immediately applies the same filters to the metrics list.

Return from a metric detail page with a highlighted metric: (1) User navigates to metric_detail.html from the overview by clicking a metric; the destination URL includes metricId. (2) When navigating back to concepts_metrics.html, a caller may include a highlightMetricId query parameter equal to the metricId the user came from. (3) On load, the overview page reads highlightMetricId, calls getMetricsOverviewPage, and then visually emphasizes the corresponding metric entry in the list (for example, auto-scrolling to it or adding a highlight state) while leaving all other filters unchanged.
interactions: On page load, the page parses URL query parameters (such as category, billing, q, sort, and highlightMetricId) and then calls getMetricsOverviewPage to obtain docPage metadata, metrics, and billingMetricIds.

Changing the category filter in the toolbar triggers an onCategoryChange handler that updates the category query parameter in the URL and recomputes the filtered metrics list from the already-loaded metrics.

Toggling the billing-related filter (for example, between showing any metrics and only billing-related metrics) triggers an onBillingModeChange handler that updates the billing query parameter in the URL and recalculates the visible list using billingMetricIds and Metric.isTokenBillingRelated.

Typing into the search field updates internal component state; submitting the search form triggers onSearchSubmit, which writes the q query parameter to the URL and filters the metrics list in-memory.

Clicking a “Clear filters” control triggers onClearFilters, which removes or resets relevant query parameters (category, billing, q, sort, highlightMetricId) and reverts the visible list to all metrics.

Clicking a column header or sort control triggers onSortChange, which updates the sort query parameter and reorders the currently filtered metrics list (for example, by Metric.displayName or Metric.category) without additional API calls.
Clicking a metric entry row or card triggers onMetricClick, which navigates to metric_detail.html?metricId=<clicked metric.id> using the outgoing navigation definition.

Clicking on a billing-metric shortcut in the billing highlight panel triggers onShowBillingOnlyClick, which sets the billing query parameter to the value representing billing-only mode and optionally clears other filters.

When the browser’s back or forward buttons change the URL, an onUrlChange handler re-parses the query parameters and re-applies filters to the already-loaded metrics, keeping the view in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is managed entirely through URL query parameters and data returned by getMetricsOverviewPage, without using localStorage or other persistent client storage. On initial load and on every URL change, a URL-state synchronization component reads query parameters and derives the visible state from them:

- category: A string representing the selected Metric.category. If absent or set to a special 'all' value, no category filter is applied. When the user changes the category via the filter toolbar, the onCategoryChange handler updates the category parameter accordingly, and the metrics list is filtered so that only metrics whose category matches the parameter are shown.

- billing: A string representing the billing filter mode (for example, 'only' to show only metrics related to billing, or 'any'/'all' to show all metrics regardless of billing relationship). The billing filter is applied by checking whether each metric.id is present in billingMetricIds or whether Metric.isTokenBillingRelated is true. The onBillingModeChange handler updates the billing parameter, and the visible metrics are recalculated without new API calls.

- q: A string representing the current free-text search query. If q is present, the list of metrics (after applying category and billing filters) is further filtered by checking whether q appears in Metric.id, Metric.displayName, or Metric.shortDescription using client-side matching. The onSearchSubmit handler updates q in the URL, while clearing the search removes q from the URL and restores the unfiltered (but still category/billing-constrained) list.

- sort: A string representing the current sort mode (for example, alphabetic by displayName, by category, or any other defined sort option). If sort is absent, a default sort mode is applied (such as alphabetic by Metric.displayName). The onSortChange handler updates the sort parameter; the metrics list is then sorted in-memory based on the selected mode.

- highlightMetricId: An optional string used to highlight a specific Metric entry on the overview page. When present, after metrics are loaded and filtered, the list component identifies the metric whose id equals highlightMetricId and applies a highlight state (for example, auto-scroll into view and visually distinguish it). This parameter does not affect which metrics are visible; it only affects emphasis. Removing this parameter clears the highlight. All navigation from the overview to metric_detail.html passes only metricId to the target page, while callers that want highlighting can add highlightMetricId when linking back to the overview.

The getMetricsOverviewPage interface is called once per page load (or reload) without parameters. Subsequent state changes (filters, search, sorting, highlighting) are all handled client-side using the data from the initial response and the URL parameters. No static metric identifiers, names, or categories are hardcoded; all such values are taken from the Metric entities returned by the interface.

components.functionality: Invokes the getMetricsOverviewPage interface on page initialization (and on full reload) to retrieve the DocPage metadata, the full metrics collection, and the billingMetricIds list. It normalizes and exposes this data to child components via a shared state mechanism or props.
event_handlers: onLoadStart
onLoadSuccess
onLoadError

components.functionality: Parses URL query parameters (category, billing, q, sort, highlightMetricId) into a structured in-memory state object and keeps it synchronized with browser navigation. When query parameters change (via user actions or browser back/forward), it emits state updates consumed by the filter toolbar, metrics list, and billing highlight components.
event_handlers: onUrlChange
onStateChange
onPushUrlState
onReplaceUrlState

components.functionality: Displays functional page-level information derived from the DocPage object obtained via getMetricsOverviewPage, such as the page title and description. It ensures that the visible title reflects docPage.name and that any short explanatory text aligns with docPage.description when present.

Provides interactive controls for filtering and searching metrics, including a category selector, billing-related toggle, text search field, sort selector, and a clear-filters action. It derives available categories dynamically from the Metric.category values and initializes its controls based on the current URL-derived state.
event_handlers: onCategoryChange
onBillingModeChange
onSearchInputChange
onSearchSubmit
onSortChange
onClearFilters

components.functionality: Highlights metrics related to billing by using billingMetricIds and Metric.isTokenBillingRelated. It computes the subset of metrics that are billing-related, displays their count, and may list them or show a concise summary. It also exposes quick actions, such as a control to switch the view to billing-only mode or to jump to a highlighted billing metric.
event_handlers: onShowBillingOnlyClick
onBillingMetricSelect

components.functionality: Renders the main metrics collection as a list or table, based on the metrics data from getMetricsOverviewPage and the currently active filters and sort state derived from the URL. It displays each Metric’s displayName, id, shortDescription, category, and a visual indicator for billing-related metrics (using billingMetricIds and Metric.isTokenBillingRelated). It also applies a highlight state to the metric matching highlightMetricId when present.
event_handlers: onMetricClick
onMetricRowFocus
onSortChangeRequest

components.functionality: Receives metric selection events from the metrics-list-view and billing-metrics-highlight-panel components. When a metric is selected, it constructs a URL to metric_detail.html with a metricId query parameter equal to the selected Metric.id and initiates navigation according to the outgoing connection definition.
event_handlers: onMetricSelect
onNavigateToMetricDetail

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same three-column documentation layout from the mockup: a fixed global header across the top, a left navigation sidebar for product docs sections, a central content column for the 'Metrics Overview' article and interactive controls, and a right sidebar for the in-page TOC. Within the central column, content flows top-to-bottom: docpage header (category label, H1, subtitle, intro paragraph) at the top; then a contextual billing highlight panel; then a horizontal filters toolbar; and finally the main metrics list view occupying the bulk of the scrollable area. Non-visual logic components (data loader, URL state sync, navigation handler) are mounted in the background within the central content region and do not break the visual flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same three-column documentation layout from the mockup: a fixed global header across the top, a left navigation sidebar for product docs sections, a central content column for the 'Metrics Overview' article and interactive controls, and a right sidebar for the in-page TOC. Within the central column, content flows top-to-bottom: docpage header (category label, H1, subtitle, intro paragraph) at the top; then a contextual billing highlight panel; then a horizontal filters toolbar; and finally the main metrics list view occupying the bulk of the scrollable area. Non-visual logic components (data loader, URL state sync, navigation handler) are mounted in the background within the central content region and do not break the visual flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Horizontally, the left sidebar uses roughly 22% of the width, the central content column about 56%, and the right TOC sidebar about 22%, following the mockup’s proportions. The global header spans 100% width with modest vertical height (~64–72px) and sits fixed at the top. In the central column, the docpage header occupies the full column width with ample vertical space (~200–260px height depending on text). The billing highlight panel and filters toolbar each span the full central column width but are slimmer bands with comfortable padding. The metrics list view spans the full central column width and uses the majority of vertical space below the fold. Right and left sidebars remain relatively narrow and vertically scroll alongside the central content, with the right TOC potentially sticky within its column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography and spacing mirror a modern, developer-doc style: generous line-height and 24–32px vertical gaps between major blocks (header, panel, toolbar, list). The docpage header is airy and low-density for easy reading. The billing highlight panel and filters toolbar are slightly denser but still have 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding for clarity. The metrics list is the most information-dense element but maintains readable row height (8–12px vertical padding per row) and breathing room between sections of metrics. No component feels crowded; each major block is separated by at least 16–24px to keep scanning easy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the same Z-pattern: logo at top-left, search and primary controls centered in the global header, and any account/CTA at the top-right. From there, attention drops diagonally to the left sidebar to confirm the user is in the doc section, then into the central column where the small category label and large 'Metrics Overview' H1 are the first content focal points. After absorbing the intro text, the eye moves down to the billing highlight panel, which subtly calls out billing-related metrics. Next, the gaze continues down to the filters toolbar to refine the view, and then into the metrics list as the primary interactive/content area. As users scroll, the right-hand TOC provides a secondary, vertical scan path for subtopics (e.g., metric categories) without competing with the main flow.

layout.overall_description: The page sits inside a modern three-column documentation shell: a slim left navigation rail, a broad central reading column, and a narrower right TOC column, all beneath a fixed global header. The central column opens with a clean documentation-style header for the 'Metrics Overview' page: a small colored section label sits above a large H1, followed by a concise subtitle and a short explanatory paragraph, all left-aligned with comfortable whitespace above and below. Immediately beneath this introduction, a full-width billing highlight panel appears as a subtle card, drawing attention to billing-related behaviors of metrics without overwhelming the page. Below that, a full-width filters toolbar forms a horizontal strip of controls aligned to the left and right edges of the content grid, visually attached to the metrics list view that sits directly beneath it. The metrics list itself is a tall, scrollable block filling the rest of the central column, with organized rows or grouped sections of metrics that feel structured yet not cramped. Throughout, the left sidebar keeps the user oriented in the broader documentation, while the right sidebar quietly lists in-page sections like metric categories or billing-related headings, aligning vertically with the top of the central H1 and scrolling in parallel. The whole layout feels balanced, spacious, and optimized for scanning and exploration of metrics rather than flashy visuals.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is mounted logically at the top of the central content column, conceptually sitting just inside the main content area beneath the global header but above the visual docpage header. It has no visible chrome in the default state and occupies no explicit space in the layout; the surrounding components (docpage header, billing panel, filters toolbar, list) appear in their normal positions as soon as data is available. During loading states, it can manifest as central-column skeletons: a wide rectangular placeholder where the docpage header text will appear, a slim placeholder bar where the billing panel and filters toolbar will be, and a tall stack of placeholder rows taking up the area where the metrics list will load. These skeletons respect the same widths as the actual components, aligning with the central column grid and maintaining 16–24px vertical spacing between each block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual by default; appears only as transient skeleton or loading overlay in the central content column, overlaying or replacing the header, filters, and list blocks while keeping their spatial structure intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component is an invisible behavior layer tied to the central column’s filters toolbar and metrics list. It does not render any visible elements or reserve any space on the page. Conceptually, it sits alongside the filters toolbar at the top of the metrics interaction zone, capturing filter changes and list state and reflecting them in the browser URL. The layout of visible components (billing panel, filters toolbar, metrics list) is unaffected; they remain stacked vertically with consistent spacing and full-width alignment within the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral; no visual footprint, no impact on spacing. It scrolls with the document as part of the central column’s logical structure but remains invisible to the user.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The docpage header sits at the very top of the central content column, directly below the fixed global header and horizontally aligned with the column’s grid (roughly 56% of the viewport width). It spans the full width of the central column, starting flush with the left content margin and ending at the right margin, with 24–32px padding from the global header above. Inside this block, a small, colored category label (e.g., 'Guides' or 'Metrics') appears at the top-left, followed by 8–12px of vertical space, then a large H1 'Metrics Overview' that dominates the block’s visual weight. Beneath the H1, a subtler subtitle (H2-style) sits with about 8–12px spacing, and then a short introductory paragraph follows with another 12–16px gap. Line height is generous and the overall height of the header section is around 200–260px, depending on text length, creating an airy top-of-page feel. The header’s bottom edge is separated from the next block (billing highlight panel) by about 24–32px, reinforcing it as a distinct introductory section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable central column. It scrolls off the screen as the user moves down, while the global header and sidebars maintain their own fixed or sticky behaviors.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters toolbar is positioned in the central content column beneath the billing metrics highlight panel and above the metrics list view. It is a full-width horizontal strip aligned to the same left and right margins as the docpage header, giving it a clear structural connection to the content grid. There is approximately 24px of vertical space separating its top edge from the bottom of the billing panel, and 16–24px of space between its bottom edge and the top of the metrics list. The toolbar itself is relatively slim, with about 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding around the filter controls. Filter elements (dropdowns, tags, search input) are left-aligned in a row, potentially wrapping to a second line on narrower viewports while maintaining the same left alignment. On the right side of the toolbar, there may be auxiliary controls (e.g., sort, reset filters) aligned to the right edge, creating a balanced horizontal layout. The background typically matches the main page background or is only very subtly differentiated, so it feels seamlessly attached to the list below rather than like a separate card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrolls with the content. It is not fixed or sticky; as the user scrolls down through the metrics list, the toolbar moves off-screen, keeping interaction simple and consistent with the mockup’s non-sticky main controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing metrics highlight panel is placed in the central column directly below the docpage header, acting as the first interactive/visual block after the introductory text. It spans the entire width of the central content column, with its left and right edges aligned to the same margins as the H1 and body text. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the intro paragraph in the docpage header and the top of this panel, clearly separating it as a distinct contextual callout. The panel is styled as a low-profile card: a subtle contrasting background (e.g., a very light tint of the brand color), a faint border or shadow, and internal padding of roughly 16–24px on all sides. Inside, concise text about billing-related aspects of metrics (e.g., which metrics are billable, how they affect usage) may be accompanied by a small icon or badge aligned to the left, with the content stacked vertically in 1–2 short lines. The card’s height is modest—around 72–120px—so it feels informative but not heavy. The bottom of the panel is separated from the filters toolbar by about 24px, giving users a small visual pause before reaching interactive controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content card within the central column. It scrolls normally and does not stick; it briefly occupies the upper viewport after the header and then scrolls out of sight as users move down to the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metrics list view is the dominant content block in the central column and is positioned directly beneath the filters toolbar. It spans the full width of the central content column, aligned to the same left and right margins as the header and panel, with about 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the bottom of the filters toolbar. Vertically, it extends downward as far as needed, typically occupying most of the viewport height and continuing below the fold. The top of the list may feature a header row with column labels (e.g., Metric name, Category, Type, Billing) styled with slightly stronger typography and about 8–12px padding above and below. Each metric entry is presented as a row or compact card in a vertical stack, with approximately 8–12px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding inside each row, resulting in moderate density that balances scannability with information richness. Rows are separated by subtle dividers (hairline borders or very light alternating backgrounds) rather than heavy cards, keeping the list unified. The entire list sits on the page background rather than inside a large card, mirroring the mockup’s screenshot-like full-width content block. Each row is clearly clickable, and the list’s bottom edge transitions into standard page whitespace with 32–48px of padding before any footer or next major section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable as part of the main document flow. The list does not have its own internal scroll container; instead, it extends with the page, matching the documentation layout’s simple vertical scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metric detail navigation handler is a non-visual interaction layer associated with each row in the metrics list view. It does not render any visible UI and does not take up its own space; instead, it hooks into the clickable area of each list row so that selecting a metric navigates to its detail documentation page. Spatially, it is co-located with the metrics list: wherever a metric row appears, the handler conceptually wraps that row’s bounds, but the layout remains entirely driven by the list view’s structure. The widths, paddings, and row alignment are defined by the list, and the handler simply follows those shapes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral and fully embedded in the metrics list’s interactive regions. It scrolls with the list as part of each row’s click target, with no independent positioning or stickiness.

name: Metric Detail
title: Metric Detail
description: Displays documentation and configuration for a selected evaluation metric, including an editable JSON configuration for token-based cost metrics and a copyable code snippet.

core_features: Load metric details and documentation for the metric specified by the metricId URL parameter using getMetricDetailPage.
Render metric metadata (name, category, whether it supports weights, whether it is token-billing-related) and associated documentation content.
Display all configuration-related CodeSnippets returned in configSnippets, with language-aware formatting and a copy action powered by copyCodeSnippet.
For token-billing-related metrics that expose a JSON metric_config snippet, provide an inline configuration editor for price_per_1k_tokens, currency, and unit using configureCostMetricJsonConfig.
Allow users to update the JSON configuration example through structured form inputs and immediately reflect the result in the JSON code block.
Enable copying of the current (potentially edited) JSON configuration to the clipboard via a dedicated copy action bound to copyCodeSnippet.
Provide navigation back to the metrics overview (concepts_metrics.html) and ensure deep-linking via the metricId URL parameter.
user_workflows:
View metric details:
1. User navigates to metric_detail.html with a metricId URL parameter from either concepts_metrics.html or search_results.html.
2. On page load, the controller component reads metricId from the URL.
3. The controller calls getMetricDetailPage(metricId).
4. When the response returns, the page displays the metric header (name, category, short description) and documentation body from docPage.
5. The page lists any returned configSnippets as read-only or editable blocks depending on metric properties and snippet metadata.

Configure cost metric JSON:
1. User opens metric_detail.html for a metric where the getMetricDetailPage response indicates isTokenBillingRelated is true.
2. The controller identifies the JSON CodeSnippet in configSnippets that represents a metric_config for this metric.
3. The JSON config editor component extracts current values for price_per_1k_tokens, currency, and unit from the snippet code, and pre-populates inline form fields.
4. The user adjusts one or more of these form fields (e.g., pricePer1kTokens, currency, unit).
5. When the user submits the configuration update (e.g., clicks an "Apply" or similar action), the editor calls configureCostMetricJsonConfig with the current codeSnippetId and the updated field values.
6. The interface returns updatedCodeSnippet; the editor replaces the corresponding snippet in page state and re-renders the JSON block.
7. The user can repeat edits as needed; each submission results in another configureCostMetricJsonConfig call and updated JSON.

Copy JSON configuration:
1. After reviewing or editing the metric configuration JSON, the user clicks the "Copy JSON" action associated with that snippet.
2. The snippet component calls copyCodeSnippet with the relevant codeSnippetId.
3. The interface returns an object containing success, code, and message.
4. If success is true, the UI copies the returned code string to the clipboard (using client-side clipboard APIs) and may show a non-intrusive confirmation based on the message.
5. If success is false, the UI displays the message as an error and does not override the clipboard.

Switching metrics via URL:
1. While viewing one metric, the user navigates (via internal link or manual URL change) to another metric_detail.html page with a different metricId URL parameter.
2. The controller detects that metricId has changed compared to the current in-memory state.
3. The controller resets page state to a loading state and calls getMetricDetailPage with the new metricId.
4. When data loads, all metric-specific sections (header, documentation, config snippets, editor) update to reflect the new metric.
5. Any previous metric-specific UI state (e.g., edited JSON) is discarded in favor of the new metric’s data.

Navigate back to metrics overview:
1. User clicks a "Back to metrics" link or a breadcrumb element intended for backward navigation.
2. The page navigates to concepts_metrics.html without persisting any additional state beyond what that page manages itself.
3. If the user returns again to metric_detail.html via a metric selection on metrics overview, a fresh getMetricDetailPage call is made based on the new metricId in the URL.
interactions:
Page load and metric selection:
- Trigger: metric_detail.html is loaded or the metricId URL parameter changes.
- Behavior: The controller reads metricId from the query string and calls getMetricDetailPage(metricId). On success, it binds metric, docPage, and configSnippets to child components. On failure, it renders an error state with a retry option that re-invokes getMetricDetailPage.

Configuration snippet identification:
- Trigger: getMetricDetailPage response arrives.
- Behavior: The controller passes all configSnippets to a snippet list component. The snippet list determines which snippets are:
  - Eligible for structured cost configuration via configureCostMetricJsonConfig (e.g., JSON language, metric_config snippetType, and metric.isTokenBillingRelated true).
  - Rendered as read-only examples (no configuration editor) if they do not meet the above conditions.

Inline configuration field editing:
- Trigger: User types into pricePer1kTokens, currency, or unit input fields within the JSON config editor for the cost metric.
- Behavior: The editor updates its local form state for those fields without immediately calling configureCostMetricJsonConfig. It may also perform lightweight validation on the client (e.g., ensuring pricePer1kTokens is numeric) without altering the underlying CodeSnippet until the user submits.

Apply updated configuration:
- Trigger: User activates the primary action to apply changes (e.g., clicks an "Apply configuration" button) in the JSON config editor.
- Behavior: The editor collects the current form values (pricePer1kTokens, currency, unit) and the associated codeSnippetId, then calls configureCostMetricJsonConfig(codeSnippetId, pricePer1kTokens, currency, unit).
- On success: The editor receives updatedCodeSnippet, updates the matching snippet in the snippet list state, and re-renders the JSON block with the new values.
- On failure: The editor displays the error feedback and does not change the snippet code.

Copy configuration JSON:
- Trigger: User clicks the copy action in a snippet that has hasCopyButton enabled.
- Behavior: The snippet component calls copyCodeSnippet with the snippet’s codeSnippetId.
- On success: The component uses the returned code property to copy text to the clipboard and may show success feedback using message.
- On failure: The component surfaces message as error feedback and avoids altering the clipboard.

Toggle between documentation sections:
- Trigger: User clicks in-page navigation (e.g., a small tab or anchor link between "Overview" and "Configuration JSON" sections).
- Behavior: The page scrolls or focuses to the appropriate section; the underlying metric and snippet state do not change. The current section may be reflected in the URL fragment (hash) for in-page navigation, but metricId remains the key query parameter.

Back to metrics navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks "Back to metrics" or a breadcrumb pointing to the metrics overview.
- Behavior: The browser navigates to concepts_metrics.html. No extra state is persisted from metric_detail.html beyond standard browser history.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the metricId URL query parameter. On every page load or when metricId changes, the page invokes getMetricDetailPage(metricId) to retrieve the current metric, its DocPage metadata, and configSnippets. The page does not store or restore state from localStorage; instead, it treats each navigation to a different metricId as a fresh request. The in-memory state includes: (1) metric details (Metric entity), (2) associated documentation (DocPage), (3) configuration snippets (CodeSnippet[]), and (4) ephemeral UI state such as loading status, error messages, and unsaved edits in inline form fields. For token-billing-related metrics (isTokenBillingRelated true), the presence of an appropriate JSON metric_config snippet in configSnippets enables the JSON configuration editor component. The editor maintains transient form state for pricePer1kTokens, currency, and unit that is not encoded in the URL; only the metricId is encoded. Successful calls to configureCostMetricJsonConfig replace the relevant CodeSnippet in memory with updatedCodeSnippet, ensuring that the JSON displayed and copied reflects the most recent configuration. The copyCodeSnippet interface uses the current codeSnippetId and does not modify any server-side state; its result is used immediately for a clipboard operation and optional user feedback.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading based on the metricId URL parameter, manages in-memory page state for metric details, documentation, configuration snippets, and orchestrates communication between child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onMetricIdChange
