onGetMetricDetailSuccess
onGetMetricDetailError
onSnippetUpdated
onRetryFetch

components.functionality: Displays the selected metric’s display name, category, short description, and key flags such as supportsWeight and isTokenBillingRelated. It also renders the simple breadcrumb or back-link to the metrics overview.
event_handlers: onBackToMetricsClick

components.functionality: Shows the main descriptive content for the metric using fields from docPage and any extended description provided by getMetricDetailPage. It may include anchors or subsections such as overview, formula, and usage notes.
event_handlers: onAnchorClick

components.functionality: Renders all configuration-related CodeSnippets returned for the metric. For each snippet, it determines whether the snippet should be treated as a read-only example or as a configurable cost JSON snippet, and passes the appropriate metadata to child snippet components.
event_handlers: onSnippetSelect
onSnippetCopyRequested
onSnippetConfigurationRequested
onSnippetReplace

components.functionality: Displays a single CodeSnippet, including language-aware formatting and an optional copy button. When the snippet is identified as the primary JSON metric_config for a token-billing-related metric, it also exposes the configuration editor trigger and renders the JSON editor component alongside the code.
event_handlers: onCopyClick
onConfigureClick
onSnippetUpdated

components.functionality: Provides structured inputs for editing price_per_1k_tokens, currency, and unit for a cost-related JSON configuration snippet. It parses the current CodeSnippet.code to initialize these fields, handles inline user edits, and calls configureCostMetricJsonConfig to persist and regenerate the JSON, then updates the snippet in the parent list.
event_handlers: onInitFromSnippet
onFieldChange
onApplyConfig
onConfigUpdateSuccess
onConfigUpdateError

components.functionality: Handles the copy behavior for a CodeSnippet. Invokes copyCodeSnippet with the snippet’s ID, then copies the returned code to the clipboard and provides success or error feedback.
event_handlers: onCopyClick
onCopySuccess
onCopyError

components.functionality: Shows loading indicators while getMetricDetailPage is in progress and displays error messaging with a retry option if the request fails.
event_handlers: onStartLoading
onStopLoading
onError
onRetryClick

components.functionality: Allows users to jump between major sections of the metric detail page (e.g., overview, formula, configuration JSON) using anchors and optional URL hash updates without affecting the metricId query parameter.
event_handlers: onSectionLinkClick
onHashChange

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a three-column documentation layout beneath a fixed global header. The left column is a persistent documentation navigation (handled by the page controller shell), the center column contains all metric-specific content in a vertical, linear flow, and the right column is a sticky in-page navigation for the currently visible metric documentation. Within the central column, the order is: metric header (category label, H1, short summary), followed by the main documentation section, then the JSON configuration editor as a full-width, code-like media block, and finally a vertically stacked list of code snippets. Interactive code-related elements (JSON editor and snippets) are grouped visually as a configuration/usage section after the explanatory documentation.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a three-column documentation layout beneath a fixed global header. The left column is a persistent documentation navigation (handled by the page controller shell), the center column contains all metric-specific content in a vertical, linear flow, and the right column is a sticky in-page navigation for the currently visible metric documentation. Within the central column, the order is: metric header (category label, H1, short summary), followed by the main documentation section, then the JSON configuration editor as a full-width, code-like media block, and finally a vertically stacked list of code snippets. Interactive code-related elements (JSON editor and snippets) are grouped visually as a configuration/usage section after the explanatory documentation.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main body under the fixed header is divided into roughly 20–24% width for the left sidebar, 52–56% for the central content, and 20–24% for the right in-page navigation at a typical desktop viewport. The global header spans full width at the top with modest height (about 64–72px) and comfortable horizontal padding. Within the center column, content is constrained to a readable line length (around 720–800px) with generous vertical spacing: ~32–40px margin between major sections (header, documentation, configuration editor, snippet list) and 16–24px between related elements (titles, subtitles, buttons inside cards).
layout_strategies.content_density: Textual documentation in the center column is low-to-medium density with generous line-height and white space between paragraphs, optimized for comfortable reading. Navigation lists in the left and right sidebars are more compact but still padded sufficiently to be easily scannable, with 8–12px vertical padding per item and clear grouping by headings. Code-related blocks (JSON editor and snippets) appear as card-like sections with 16–24px internal padding and enough breathing room above and below so they feel like distinct, focused work areas rather than cramped widgets.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the global header (logo on the left, centered search, right-aligned CTA) and then drops diagonally to the left sidebar to orient the user within the broader documentation. The active metric/section nav item in the left sidebar anchors context. From there, the eye moves into the central column, first meeting the metric header (category label and H1) and short intro. After reading the initial documentation, attention naturally flows downward to the JSON configuration editor as the main interactive focus, and then further down to usage snippets. Once the user starts scanning for specific subtopics, their gaze shifts to the right-hand in-page navigation, using its vertically arranged links as a secondary guide to jump between sections like "Overview", "Configuration", and "Usage examples".

layout.overall_description: The Metric Detail page sits within a classic three-column documentation shell. A fixed global header runs across the top with ample horizontal padding, a compact height, and a clear hierarchy from logo on the left through a centered search bar to a right-aligned primary CTA, mirroring a modern SaaS docs experience. Below this header, the page body is split into three vertical regions: a left navigation sidebar of fixed width, a fluid central content column, and a narrower right sidebar. The left sidebar anchors the user within the overall documentation, listing top-level sections and nested pages, with the current metric’s parent section highlighted. The central column is visually dominant and slightly inset from the sidebars, with a maximum readable width and generous vertical rhythm. At the top of this column, the metric header presents a small, colored category label, a bold H1 metric name, an understated subtitle, and a short introductory paragraph, all spaced to breathe. Immediately below, the main documentation uses headings, paragraphs, and lists to explain the metric, with clear separation between conceptual chunks. Further down, a full-width JSON configuration editor spans the central column like a code-focused card, followed by a stacked list of code snippets, each in its own card with a copy action aligned to the top-right. On the far right, an "On this page" panel mirrors the mockup’s right TOC, listing section anchors with subtle indentation and an active-state accent, pinned in place as the main content scrolls. The overall effect is a clean, spacious documentation page where reading flows naturally down the center, anchored on the left by global navigation and on the right by in-page navigation, with configuration and code examples presented as distinct, scannable modules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metric-detail-page-controller defines the entire documentation shell for this page. At the very top, it reserves space for a fixed global header that spans the full viewport width, with horizontal padding of roughly 24–32px on both sides and a height of about 64–72px. Below this fixed header, it creates a three-column layout that occupies the full remaining viewport height. The left column is a fixed-width sidebar (approximately 240–280px) positioned flush to the left edge, carrying the site-wide documentation navigation. The central column sits to the right of this sidebar with a 24–32px horizontal gap, occupying roughly 52–56% of the total page width and constrained to a maximum readable width (around 720–800px). The right column, to the immediate right of the central content with a similar 24–32px gap, is a narrower sidebar (approximately 220–260px) reserved for the metric-section-navigation. All metric-specific components (metric-header, metric-documentation-section, cost-json-config-editor, metric-config-snippet-list, metric-loading-state) are vertically stacked within the central column, starting a comfortable distance (around 32–40px) below the bottom edge of the global header. The entire central column scrolls vertically, while the global header remains fixed and the right in-page navigation can remain visible via a sticky behavior within its column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a three-column responsive grid under a fixed global header. The left sidebar is fixed-width and scrolls with the page. The central column is fluid and scrollable. The right column is fixed-width and houses a sticky in-page navigation that stays visible as the main content scrolls. The controller also provides a positioned container to allow the metric-loading-state to overlay the central column when needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metric-header is the first content block in the central column, aligned to the same left edge as all subsequent central content. It starts roughly 32–40px below the bottom of the global header, giving a clear separation between site chrome and page content. Within its block, it uses a vertical, left-aligned stack: at the very top, a small, uppercase or slightly condensed category label (e.g., "Metric" or "Cost metrics") sits in a subtle brand color, occupying only the natural width of its text and leaving generous whitespace to the right. Approximately 8–12px below this label, the primary H1 metric name spans most of the central column width, set in a large, bold type size. Another 8–12px down, a lighter-weight subtitle or one-line descriptor appears, with a maximum width slightly narrower than the H1 to keep it readable. Finally, 16–24px below the subtitle, a short introductory paragraph (2–4 lines) spans the central column width, with ample line height for comfortable reading. The bottom of the metric-header block leaves a 24–32px gap before the start of the metric-documentation-section, clearly signaling the transition from header info to the main body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flows with the central column content. Always appears at the top of the metric content stack and scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metric-documentation-section sits directly below the metric-header in the central column, sharing the same left and right alignment. It begins after a vertical gap of about 24–32px, creating a smooth but distinct separation from the header. Within this section, content is arranged as a vertically flowing article: H2 and H3 headings, paragraphs, lists, and inline callouts stack top-to-bottom. Each heading starts slightly indented from the left (consistent with the main text) and is given 24–32px of space above it to break the content into scannable segments. Paragraphs have a comfortable line-height and 16–24px spacing between them. If there are subsections like "Overview", "When to use this metric", or "Behavior", each begins with a heading that aligns with the in-page navigation on the right, making their vertical positions predictable. The overall width stays within the central column’s text width, leaving clear margins on both sides so the section feels airy rather than cramped. The bottom of this section leaves a larger buffer (around 32–40px) before transitioning into the more utilitarian cost-json-config-editor, similar to the mockup’s separation between text and a full-width image or code block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable as part of the main article flow. Its headings are mapped to the metric-section-navigation anchors, but the section itself does not have special sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cost-json-config-editor is positioned below the metric-documentation-section, acting as the main interactive block in the central column. It begins after a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px, mirroring how a full-width screenshot or diagram would be separated from preceding text in the mockup. The editor is contained in a card-like panel that spans nearly the full width of the central column, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides and a subtle border or soft background to distinguish it from the page background. At the top of this card, a small header row runs horizontally: on the left, a section title such as "JSON configuration" or "Token cost config" is aligned with the card’s left padding; on the right, optional secondary actions (like reset or format) can sit, aligned with the card’s right padding. Below this header, a monospace code editor area occupies the majority of the panel height, filling the width between the left and right padding. The editor’s height is substantial enough (approximately 280–360px) to show several lines of JSON without feeling cramped, and it allows vertical scrolling within if content exceeds its height. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of this card and the top of the subsequent metric-config-snippet-list, visually grouping it with code-related content while keeping it distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, scrolling with the page. The editor content itself can scroll internally if JSON exceeds the visible height, but the overall card position is fixed relative to surrounding content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metric-config-snippet-list is placed directly below the cost-json-config-editor, aligned with the same central column edges. It starts after a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px, visually indicating a related but distinct section focused on usage. At the top of this block, an optional section heading like "Usage examples" or "SDK snippets" may appear, left-aligned and given about 8–12px of space below it. Underneath, the list arranges multiple metric-config-snippet-item cards in a vertical stack. Each snippet item occupies the full width of the central column’s content area and is separated from the next by 16–24px of vertical spacing. The list itself may extend beyond the initial viewport, encouraging vertical scrolling, but maintains consistent left alignment with the rest of the content, ensuring a clean columnar feel. The bottom of the snippet list has a comfortable margin (around 48–64px) before any page footer or subsequent sections, echoing the mockup’s generous end-of-page spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked within the central column. The list grows in height based on the number of snippet items and scrolls with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each metric-config-snippet-item appears as an individual card within the metric-config-snippet-list. It spans nearly the full width of the central column, inset by the same margins used for text content. The card has 16–24px internal padding on all sides and a subtle border or shadow to set it off from the background. At the top of the card, there is a horizontal header row: on the left, the snippet title (e.g., "Python SDK", "cURL", or "JavaScript example") appears in medium-weight text; on the right, the copy-snippet-action is aligned with the card’s right edge, sitting on the same baseline as the title. About 8–12px below this header row, an optional one- or two-line description can appear, wrapping within the card width. Below that, separated by another 8–12px, a code block fills the remaining width of the card, using a monospace font and allowing horizontal scrolling if the code extends beyond the visible width. The bottom padding ensures at least 12–16px of breathing room below the code before the edge of the card. Each card is separated from the next by a 16–24px gap, echoing the spacing pattern of the mockup’s navigation lists and content blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the snippet list. The code area inside the card can scroll horizontally if needed, but the card itself maintains its position relative to other items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The copy-snippet-action is positioned within the top-right corner of each metric-config-snippet-item card’s header row. It appears as a medium-sized, pill-shaped or rectangular button with subtle emphasis—often an outlined or ghost style—to avoid overpowering the snippet title. The button’s right edge aligns with the card’s internal right padding, and its vertical center aligns with the baseline of the snippet title on the left. There is roughly 8–12px of horizontal spacing between the title and the button’s left edge, ensuring the two elements are clearly distinct. The button uses the standard interaction padding from the design DNA (about 8–10px vertically and 16–20px horizontally), making it comfortably clickable without visually dominating the card. In layouts with very narrow central columns, the button wraps to a second line only if necessary, but by default it stays in the same row as the title, reinforcing its association with the snippet.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its parent card. It does not stick or float as the page scrolls; it remains anchored in the snippet item’s header row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metric-loading-state overlays or replaces the central content area when the metric detail is being fetched or switched. It is positioned within the bounds defined by the metric-detail-page-controller’s central column, starting just below the global header and aligned with the same left and right edges as the main content. In its overlay mode, it can present a semi-opaque backdrop over the central column with a centered loader (spinner or skeleton blocks) while subtly dimming the underlying content. The loader is centered both horizontally and vertically within the visible portion of the central column, giving clear feedback without affecting the left navigation or right in-page navigation. In a non-overlay (skeleton) mode, it occupies the central column with placeholder bars mimicking the metric-header, documentation, and code blocks, stacked vertically and following the same spacing rhythm. The sidebars remain visible and static, maintaining the page’s overall structure even while data is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the contents of the central column while in a loading state. It is absolutely positioned relative to the central column container, and is removed or hidden once the metric content is loaded, restoring normal scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metric-section-navigation occupies the entire right sidebar column created by the metric-detail-page-controller. It is aligned to the right edge of the central content area, with a consistent horizontal gap of about 24–32px between the central column and itself. At the top of this sidebar, a small heading like "On this page" appears, left-aligned within the sidebar and given 8–12px of space below. Under this heading, a vertical list of navigation links corresponds to the major sections and subsections of the metric-documentation-section and related blocks (e.g., "Overview", "Configuration", "JSON schema", "Usage examples"). Each primary link is left-aligned with about 8–10px vertical padding, and secondary-level items (representing H3s) are indented an additional 12–16px to the right, creating a clear hierarchy. The active item is highlighted with a subtle typographic change (slightly bolder or colored text) and possibly a thin vertical accent line to the left. The entire navigation panel sits within the right column and is visually lighter than the central content to avoid competing for attention, but its consistent position makes it easy to glance at while reading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right sidebar: it remains fixed relative to the viewport once scrolled into place, typically starting just below the global header with a top offset of about 24px. As the central content scrolls, the navigation stays visible, updating active states based on scroll position.

name: Guides Overview
title: Guides
description: Overview of all task-oriented guides for configuring and running evaluations, with a sidebar and goal-based helper to choose the right guide.

core_features: Fetch and initialize the Guides overview content using getGuidesOverviewPage, including the main Guides doc page metadata, guides list, and guides sidebar items.
Render a navigable list of all available guides from the getGuidesOverviewPage.guides array, showing each guide's title and summary and enabling navigation to the selected guide.
Render a guides sidebar using getGuidesOverviewPage.sidebarItems, highlighting the guide marked as isConfigBuilder in getGuidesOverviewPage.guides.
Provide a goal-based guide helper: users can specify their goal (via a form or URL parameter) and the page filters/sorts guides dynamically based on title/summary matching.
Highlight the Config Builder guide across both the main list and the sidebar by detecting the guide object with isConfigBuilder = true.
Maintain and reflect UI state (selected guide, goal text, and sort mode) via URL parameters without using localStorage, allowing deep links into specific views of the Guides overview.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open the Guides overview
1. User clicks the "Guides" link in the site header or a direct link from another documentation page.
2. The browser navigates to guides.html, optionally with URL parameters (e.g., guides.html?goal=fast+setup&sort=recommended).
3. On page load, the guides-page-loader component calls getGuidesOverviewPage().
4. On successful response, the url-state-manager reads URL parameters (goal, guideId, sort) and initializes in-memory view state.
5. The guides-header displays the returned docPage.name and docPage.description.
6. The guides-sidebar renders sidebarItems and marks the guide whose targetDocPageId matches the isConfigBuilder guide as specially highlighted.
7. The guides-list renders the guides array, applying filters and sort based on the URL parameters and highlighting the selected guide (if guideId is present).

Workflow 2: Choose a guide based on a goal
1. User sees the goal filter form at the top of the main content.
2. User types a short free-text goal description (e.g., "compare models" or "optimize latency").
3. User submits the form.
4. The goal-filter-form prevents full page reload, updates the URL parameter goal to the submitted text using history.pushState, and informs the url-state-manager of the new goal.
5. The guides-list recomputes a relevance score for each guide based on simple matching between the goal text and each guide's title/summary from getGuidesOverviewPage.guides.
6. Guides are re-ordered (or filtered) according to this relevance. The list displays the updated ordering, and a small note (UI text) indicates that the list is filtered/sorted by the current goal.
7. The user clicks the most relevant guide entry to navigate to that guide's detail page.

Workflow 3: Navigate directly to the Config Builder guide
1. User notices a dedicated Config Builder highlight area in the main content and/or a highlighted item in the guides sidebar.
2. User clicks the highlighted Config Builder entry in the main list or the corresponding sidebar item.
3. The click handler determines the guide object in getGuidesOverviewPage.guides where isConfigBuilder is true and reads its targetDocPageId.
4. The page constructs a navigation URL that targets the Config Builder guide page (e.g., guide_config_builder.html) and includes the targetDocPageId as a URL parameter (e.g., guide_config_builder.html?id={targetDocPageId}&from=guides_overview), without hardcoding the id value itself.
5. The browser navigates to the Config Builder guide page. (This satisfies the outgoing navigation requirement for clicking the "Config builder" guide.)

Workflow 4: Use the sidebar to explore guides
1. User scans the guides-sidebar for relevant topics.
2. For each sidebar item, the component uses its targetPageId to find a matching guide in getGuidesOverviewPage.guides (if any) by comparing the targetPageId with guide.targetDocPageId.
3. When the user clicks a sidebar item that corresponds to a guide in the list view, the url-state-manager updates the guideId parameter in the current URL (e.g., guides.html?guideId={targetDocPageId}&goal=...) using history.pushState.
4. The guides-list receives the updated guideId from url-state-manager and marks that guide as selected (e.g., pinned to top or visually distinguished) and scrolls it into view.
5. If the sidebar item is intended for direct navigation to another page (e.g., the Config Builder guide), the click handler navigates directly to the target guide page URL, using the sidebar item's targetPageId to build the URL and optionally preserving context with from=guides_overview in the query string.

Workflow 5: Share a tailored Guides view
1. User filters/sorts guides using the goal filter and optionally selects a specific guide in the list.
2. Each of these interactions updates URL parameters (goal, guideId, sort) but does not reload the page.
3. User copies the current URL from the browser address bar.
4. Another user opens that shared URL.
5. On load, guides-page-loader fetches data via getGuidesOverviewPage, then url-state-manager parses the parameters.
6. guides-list and guides-sidebar render in the same filtered/sorted/selected state, reproducing the original user's context without additional configuration.
interactions: Page load: triggers getGuidesOverviewPage() via guides-page-loader; on success, initializes all components with docPage, sidebarItems, and guides.
Goal text input change: updates internal form state in goal-filter-form but does not immediately change URL or filtering until submitted.
Goal filter form submit: prevents default form submission, updates the goal URL parameter, notifies url-state-manager, and causes guides-list to recompute relevance and re-render.

Sort mode change (e.g., via a dropdown or toggle): updates the sort URL parameter (e.g., sort=recommended or sort=alphabetical), notifies url-state-manager, and causes guides-list to re-sort the guides array accordingly.

Click on a guide list item (non-Config-Builder guides): uses the clicked item's targetDocPageId to construct a navigation URL to that guide's page (e.g., generic guide page such as a template like guide.html?id={targetDocPageId}), then navigates the browser to that URL.

Click on the Config Builder guide list item: specifically navigates to guide_config_builder.html with id={targetDocPageId} and optional context params, leveraging the guide where isConfigBuilder is true.

Click on a sidebar item that corresponds to a guide in the list: updates guideId in the current page URL via history.pushState, triggers selection/highlighting of the corresponding guide in guides-list, and scrolls it into view without leaving guides.html.

Click on a sidebar item that is intended for direct navigation: navigates to the corresponding guide page, using the sidebar item's targetPageId to form the destination URL and optionally attaching context parameters.

Click on a 'Clear goal' action (e.g., button or link in the goal filter area): clears the goal URL parameter, resets the goal-filter-form input, and instructs guides-list to remove goal-based filtering/sorting and revert to the default sort mode.

Browser back/forward navigation: url-state-manager listens for popstate events, re-reads URL parameters, and updates goal, guideId, and sort state, which in turn reconfigures guides-list and guides-sidebar without re-calling getGuidesOverviewPage.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters to drive all user-visible state. Relevant parameters include:

- goal (string):
  - Represents the user's free-text objective for choosing a guide.
  - On initial load, url-state-manager reads goal from the URL. If present, it sets the goal-filter-form input value and signals guides-list to compute a relevance score for each guide in getGuidesOverviewPage.guides based on matches in the guide.title and guide.summary fields.
  - When the user submits the goal-filter-form, the component updates the goal parameter via history.pushState without reloading the page.
  - Clearing the goal removes it from the URL (e.g., updating to a URL without the goal parameter) and resets guides-list to show all guides using the current sort mode.

- guideId (string):
  - Represents the currently selected or focused guide within guides.html.
  - On load, if guideId is present, url-state-manager stores it in view state. guides-list uses this to mark the matching guide (where guide.targetDocPageId === guideId) as selected and may scroll it into view.
  - guides-sidebar also uses guideId to highlight the corresponding sidebar item whose targetPageId matches guideId.
  - Clicking a sidebar item that corresponds to a guide in the list updates guideId in the URL via history.pushState, synchronizing selection between sidebar and main list.
  - When navigating away to a specific guide page, guideId is not required to be preserved, but the implementation can optionally pass it along as fromGuideId in the destination URL if needed.

- sort (string):
  - Represents the sort mode for the guide list (e.g., "recommended" vs. "alphabetical"). The specific values are treated as UI-level constants, not data-derived values.
  - On load, url-state-manager reads sort; if absent, a default (e.g., recommended) is assumed in internal state but does not need to be written into the URL until the user changes it.
  - Changing the sort mode via a control in guides-list or a dedicated sort component updates the sort parameter using history.pushState.
  - guides-list uses sort along with goal to order guides: for example, in recommended mode it can rank Config Builder (via isConfigBuilder) higher when appropriate, and in alphabetical mode it sorts guides by their title property.

- Other behaviour:
  - No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used; all restorable state is derived from or reflected in URL parameters and the dynamic data returned from getGuidesOverviewPage.
  - getGuidesOverviewPage is called once per full page load. Changes to URL parameters via history.pushState (goal, guideId, sort) reuse the already-fetched data and do not trigger additional calls to getGuidesOverviewPage.
  - On browser back/forward (popstate), url-state-manager re-applies the URL parameters to the in-memory state, causing guides-list and guides-sidebar to re-render appropriately without re-fetching data.

components.functionality: Initializes the Guides overview page by calling getGuidesOverviewPage, handling loading and error states, and passing docPage, sidebarItems, and guides data to child components.
event_handlers: onLoad
onError

components.functionality: Parses and manages URL parameters (goal, guideId, sort) on initial load and on history changes, exposes the current state to other components, and updates the URL via history.pushState or history.replaceState when components request state changes.
event_handlers: onLoad
onPopState
onGoalChangeRequest
onGuideSelectionChangeRequest
onSortChangeRequest

components.functionality: Displays the main page title and description using the docPage object returned from getGuidesOverviewPage, ensuring the header reflects the current documentation metadata for the Guides overview.

Displays the list of sidebarItems from getGuidesOverviewPage, highlights the item corresponding to the currently selected guide (based on guideId URL parameter) and especially the item whose targetPageId matches the guide with isConfigBuilder = true, and supports navigation or in-page selection when items are clicked.
event_handlers: onClickSidebarItem

components.functionality: Provides a text input and submit action that let users express their evaluation-related goal, initializes its value from the goal URL parameter, and on submit requests url-state-manager to update the goal parameter to drive guide filtering and sorting.
event_handlers: onChange
onSubmit
onClear

components.functionality: Renders the collection of guides from getGuidesOverviewPage.guides as selectable entries, applies filtering/sorting based on current goal and sort state, marks the guide whose targetDocPageId matches guideId as selected, and emits navigation events when a guide entry is clicked.
event_handlers: onClickGuide
onSortChange

components.functionality: Locates the guide in getGuidesOverviewPage.guides where isConfigBuilder is true and presents a dedicated quick-access entry or banner within the main content, synchronized with the sidebar highlight and using the same navigation behavior when clicked.
event_handlers: onClickConfigGuide

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a three-column documentation layout under a fixed global header. The left column is the persistent documentation navigation (guides-sidebar). The central column is the main reading area, where the Guides Overview header (guides-header) appears at the top, followed by a featured config highlight card (config-builder-highlight), and then the full list of guides (guides-list) flowing vertically. The right column is a contextual helper/sidebar where the goal-based helper form (goal-filter-form) sits near the top as a sticky card aligned with the main header. System-level components (guides-page-loader and url-state-manager) do not appear as standard content: the loader temporarily overlays the three-column area during data fetching, while the URL state manager is purely behavioral and occupies no visual space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a three-column documentation layout under a fixed global header. The left column is the persistent documentation navigation (guides-sidebar). The central column is the main reading area, where the Guides Overview header (guides-header) appears at the top, followed by a featured config highlight card (config-builder-highlight), and then the full list of guides (guides-list) flowing vertically. The right column is a contextual helper/sidebar where the goal-based helper form (goal-filter-form) sits near the top as a sticky card aligned with the main header. System-level components (guides-page-loader and url-state-manager) do not appear as standard content: the loader temporarily overlays the three-column area during data fetching, while the URL state manager is purely behavioral and occupies no visual space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the full-width global header, the page content is divided into three vertical regions: the left sidebar occupies roughly 20–22% of the available width, the central content column 52–56%, and the right helper sidebar 22–26%, mirroring a 12-column grid split of about 3/6/3. The central column is the widest and holds all primary reading content, with text constrained to a comfortable max-width for readability. Vertical spacing follows the 8px base scale: 24–32px gaps between major blocks (header, highlight, list), 16–24px internal padding within cards and sidebars, and 8–16px between related textual elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: The main content (guides-header, config-builder-highlight, guides-list) uses generous line-height and white space, prioritizing readability: comfortable margins around paragraphs, ample breathing room between guide cards, and no more than 70–80 characters per line. Navigation and helper elements (guides-sidebar and goal-filter-form) are denser but still airy: nav items and form controls are vertically stacked with 8–12px gaps, and 12–16px internal paddings, making them easily scannable without feeling crowded. The loader uses a minimalist overlay with a single centered indicator, keeping perceived density low even during loading states.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the global header (logo and search) and then drops into the three-column region, where the eye naturally lands on the central Guides Overview header (guides-header). From there, attention moves down through the intro text into the featured configuration highlight (config-builder-highlight), then continues into the guides list (guides-list) for detailed exploration. In parallel, the left guide navigation (guides-sidebar) anchors the user's sense of location in the documentation, while the right helper column (goal-filter-form) offers a lateral path to filter or choose the right guide. During loading, the loader overlay momentarily becomes the primary focus, fading back to reveal the three-column layout once data is ready. Overall, the flow is left-to-right for orientation (sidebar → main → helper), then top-to-bottom within each column.

layout.overall_description: Below a fixed, full-width global header, the page resolves into a three-column documentation layout stretching the full viewport height. On the left, a light, fixed-width sidebar lists documentation sections and guides, starting just beneath the header and running to the bottom of the page; it feels like a stable spine for navigation. In the center, a broad content column begins with a small, colored 'Guides' label, a large H1 title, and a concise subtitle and intro paragraph—spaced generously and left-aligned for easy scanning. Directly beneath the introduction, a subtle featured card highlights the configuration-builder guide, spanning the full width of the central column. Below that, the main guides list flows as a vertical stack of cards or rows, each separated by comfortable vertical gaps so that each guide can be scanned as a distinct unit. On the right, a narrower helper sidebar aligns its top edge with the start of the central header; inside it, a card-like goal filter form gives users a way to choose the right guide based on their evaluation goals. This helper card stays visible as the user scrolls, creating a stable right-hand reference. Throughout, the layout is balanced and airy; typography is neutral and modern, with subdued brand accents marking active navigation and key contextual labels. When data is loading, a translucent layer softens the three-column layout and a centered loading indicator briefly takes focus before receding to reveal the fully interactive page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guides-page-loader appears only during data fetching and overlays the entire documentation canvas beneath the global header. Visually, it is a semi-transparent layer that covers the left sidebar, central content column, and right helper column from just below the fixed header down to the bottom of the viewport. At its center—horizontally aligned to the middle of the central content area and vertically centered within the visible viewport—it shows a minimal loading indicator and/or short status text. The overlay spans 100% of the content width and height, with no internal padding beyond the central placement of the indicator, ensuring that underlying content is dimly visible but clearly inactive. Once loading completes, the overlay fades away completely, restoring full interaction with the three-column layout beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed-position overlay within the viewport below the global header, covering the full scrollable area while active. It is layered above all other components via a higher z-index and does not affect the underlying layout’s spatial dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is non-visual and occupies no physical space in the layout. Conceptually it sits at the page or app root level, orchestrating synchronization between the URL (query parameters, hash, etc.) and the state of visible components such as the guides-sidebar active item, goal-filter-form selections, and the currently visible segment of the guides-list. It does not render any boxes, text, or controls and therefore does not alter spacing, alignment, or the three-column structure in any way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral; it has no rendered DOM footprint affecting layout. It is effectively ‘positionless’ from the user’s perspective, updating state in response to navigation and scroll without occupying space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guides-header sits at the very top of the central content column, directly beneath the global header, with a generous top margin of roughly 32–40px to create a clear separation. It spans the full width of the central column, which itself occupies about 52–56% of the total page width and is centered between the left and right sidebars. Within this header block, the small category label (e.g., 'Guides') appears at the top left in a subtle brand color, followed by the main page title (H1) below it with 8–12px vertical spacing. A short subtitle or supporting line of text sits under the H1 with another 8–12px gap, and a multi-line introductory paragraph follows with a slightly larger 16–24px gap to emphasize it as the final part of the intro cluster. Text is left-aligned and constrained to a comfortable reading width (approximately 640–720px), even if the central column can be wider, leaving soft margins on either side. Below the intro paragraph there is a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px before the next central component (config-builder-highlight), preserving the airy, documentation-style rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the central content column. On large screens it stays horizontally aligned between the left and right columns; it does not stick as the user scrolls, allowing the focus to transition from header down into the guides-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guides-sidebar forms the entire left column of the layout, beginning just below the global header and extending to the bottom of the viewport. It occupies roughly 20–22% of the page width and is anchored flush to the left edge of the window. Inside, content is arranged vertically: at the top, a compact set of horizontal or pill-style tabs (Documentation / API Reference / Blog) sits with 16–24px top padding and 16px side padding, spanning nearly the full sidebar width. Below this, labeled nav sections (e.g., 'Welcome', 'Guides') stack vertically with 16–24px gaps between section groups. Each section title appears in a slightly lighter or smaller typographic style, with individual nav items listed beneath and indented by about 8–16px. Each nav item has a tap/click target height of roughly 32–40px, with 8–12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding, creating a moderately dense yet comfortable list. The active item is highlighted via a pill or background strip that extends the full clickable width, aligning its left edge with the indentation of other items. The sidebar background is slightly differentiated from the main content (e.g., a very light gray or subtle border on the right) to clearly define the column boundary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Configured as a sticky/fixed column: it remains vertically fixed relative to the viewport starting just beneath the global header, allowing the navigation items to stay in view as the user scrolls the central and right columns. If sidebar content exceeds the viewport height, it becomes internally scrollable while still maintaining its fixed horizontal position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The goal-filter-form lives in the right-hand column as a prominent helper card aligned with the top of the guides-header. The right column occupies about 22–26% of the page width and begins just below the global header, with a small horizontal gap (24–32px) from the central content column. The form itself is enclosed in a card with a subtle border or shadow, 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, and gently rounded corners, spanning nearly the full width of the right column with 16px side margins from the column edges. Inside the card, a short title such as 'Find the right guide' appears at the top, followed by concise helper text and then the form controls (e.g., dropdowns, checkboxes, or segmented buttons) stacked vertically. Controls are arranged with 8–12px gaps between them, and each field uses comfortable horizontal padding reflecting the broader design system. The card’s top edge is visually aligned with the baseline of the main page title or intro text in the center column so that both columns feel synchronized. Below the form, any additional helper notes or links are set with slightly smaller text and extra spacing before the bottom edge of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a sticky element within the right column: as the user scrolls, the goal-filter-form remains pinned near the top of the viewport (with a small offset below the global header), ensuring that the helper is always visible while the central guides-list scrolls. On smaller viewports where the three-column layout collapses, it would stack below or above the guides-list, but in the primary desktop layout it stays in the right sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guides-list occupies the main vertical portion of the central content column, beginning directly below the config-builder-highlight card with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans the full usable width of the central column (approximately 52–56% of page width), maintaining the same max text width as the intro for readability. Inside this section, guides are presented as a vertical stack of cards or list rows. Each guide entry occupies the full width of the column, with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between adjacent entries. Titles of guides are left-aligned, typically in a bold or semibold style, followed by one or two lines of description text beneath with 4–8px separation. Optional metadata such as tags or difficulty indicators appear either inline under the description or aligned to the right within the same card, using lighter typography to maintain clarity. The overall list is moderately spacious, making each guide scannable without feeling cramped, and may be grouped under subheadings (e.g., 'Getting started', 'Advanced') with 24–32px gaps and slightly larger section titles to visually segment the list. The top of the guides-list aligns vertically with the bottom of the featured config-builder-highlight so that content flows smoothly downward.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally as part of the central column. It does not stick or float; as the user scrolls down the page, new guide entries slide up from below while the left navigation and right goal-filter-form remain relatively fixed, supporting orientation while users move through the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The config-builder-highlight is a featured card placed in the central column between the introductory content of the guides-header and the main guides-list. It begins immediately beneath the intro paragraph of the header, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap, and spans nearly the full width of the central column, aligned flush with the left edge of the header text. The card has a subtle accent treatment to stand out: a slightly tinted background (using a soft version of the brand color) or a thin colored border on the left edge, combined with 16–24px internal padding and modest corner rounding. Within the card, a compact label such as 'Recommended' or an icon appears at the top, followed by a bold card title referencing the configuration builder guide, and a one- or two-line explanation below that. The text is left-aligned, and a primary or secondary inline link (e.g., 'Open configuration builder guide') is placed at the bottom or right side with enough spacing (8–12px) from the description. This card visually bridges the high-level overview and the detailed list by drawing the eye to a particularly important guide without overwhelming the rest of the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block element in the central content flow. It scrolls with the rest of the main column; there is no sticky behavior. Its vertical placement between the header and list is fixed so that it consistently appears in the first screenful or just below, depending on viewport height.

name: Guide: Config Builder
title: Config Builder Guide
description: Interactive documentation page for constructing evaluation configurations by selecting task types, models, metrics, and evaluation settings, then generating reusable JSON or code snippets.

core_features: Initialize and display the current Config Builder state (task type, model variants, evaluation settings, selected metrics, and last generated config) using getConfigBuilderState.
Allow users to select and change the evaluation task type via a dropdown that updates the backend builder state using updateConfigBuilderTaskType and keeps the URL parameters in sync.

Provide a models or variants editor where users can add, remove, and edit candidate and baseline model rows (including model identifiers and temperatures) and persist those changes via setConfigBuilderModels.
Expose an evaluation settings panel where users can set global options such as max_samples and update selected metrics, propagating changes to the backend via updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings.

Render a metrics selection area using the available metrics returned by getConfigBuilderState, allowing users to enable or disable metrics and reflecting these selections through updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings.
Generate an evaluation configuration snippet in JSON or another supported code format from the current builder state when the user triggers generation, using generateConfigFromBuilder.
Display the generated configuration snippet in a read-only code block, with controls to copy the snippet content using copyCodeSnippet.

Offer navigation from the generated configuration to the API reference page via a link such as api_reference.html with appropriate URL parameters (for example, including the generated snippet identifier and source page identifier).
user_workflows: Workflow: Load and review current builder state. Step 1: User navigates to guide_config_builder.html, optionally with URL parameters indicating task type, max samples, metrics, or models. Step 2: On mount, the page calls getConfigBuilderState to retrieve the current ConfigBuilderState, associated ModelVariant entries, and available Metric entries. Step 3: The page merges any URL parameters into the returned state by invoking updateConfigBuilderTaskType and updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings as needed and by preparing a models payload for setConfigBuilderModels if a models URL parameter is present. Step 4: The UI components (task type selector, models editor, evaluation settings panel, metrics selector, and config output panel) render based on this synchronized state.

Workflow: Configure task type and models. Step 1: User opens the task type dropdown and selects a task type option. Step 2: The task-type selector component calls updateConfigBuilderTaskType with the chosen task type; on success, the returned taskType is reflected in the UI and the taskType URL parameter is updated. Step 3: User edits model variants in the models table (adding rows, editing model IDs, changing roles such as candidate or baseline, and adjusting temperatures). Step 4: When the user confirms or blurs a row, the models editor aggregates all current variants into a models array and calls setConfigBuilderModels. Step 5: The models URL parameter is updated with a serialized representation of the current ModelVariant collection so the configuration can be deep-linked.

Workflow: Adjust evaluation settings and metrics. Step 1: User edits the max_samples input field in the evaluation settings panel. Step 2: User selects or deselects metrics via the metrics selector checkboxes, which are sourced from availableMetrics and mapped to metric IDs. Step 3: The panel collects the current maxSamples value and selectedMetricIds array and calls updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings. Step 4: On success, the local state is updated with the latest maxSamples and selectedMetricIds, and the maxSamples and metrics URL parameters are updated (metrics as a delimiter-separated list of metric IDs).

Workflow: Generate configuration and copy code. Step 1: After configuring task type, models, and evaluation settings, the user activates the Generate config control. Step 2: The page calls generateConfigFromBuilder without additional parameters. Step 3: The response’s generatedConfigSnippet is stored in the client state and rendered inside the code output component, and the ConfigBuilderState.generatedConfigSnippetId is updated if provided by the backend; the URL may be updated with a snippetId parameter reflecting this identifier. Step 4: User clicks the Copy config button within the code output area. Step 5: The button handler calls copyCodeSnippet using the generatedConfigSnippet.id; on success, the returned code text is placed onto the clipboard by the UI environment and may optionally be used to refresh the displayed code content to ensure it matches the latest backend version.

Workflow: Navigate to API reference with generated config. Step 1: After a configuration is generated, a link labeled for using the configuration with the API becomes enabled. Step 2: The link’s href is constructed to navigate to api_reference.html with query parameters that can include the generated config snippet identifier and a source indicator (for example, api_reference.html?source=config_builder&snippetId=<generatedConfigSnippet.id>). Step 3: When the user activates this link, the browser navigates to api_reference.html, where the target page can independently read the snippetId parameter and use its own interfaces to load or display the configuration.
interactions: On initial page load or when URL parameters change (for example via browser navigation), the root component reads the current URL parameters, calls getConfigBuilderState, and then conditionally calls updateConfigBuilderTaskType, updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings, and setConfigBuilderModels so backend state reflects the URL-driven configuration.
When the user changes the task type in the dropdown, an onChange handler invokes updateConfigBuilderTaskType with the new value and then updates the taskType URL parameter without reloading the page.

When the user adds, edits, or removes a model row in the models editor, an onModelsChange handler aggregates the full set of model variants and calls setConfigBuilderModels, then updates a models URL parameter containing a serialized form of the current ModelVariant list.

When the user edits max_samples or selects or deselects metrics, an onSettingsChange handler composes the new maxSamples and selectedMetricIds and calls updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings, then updates maxSamples and metrics URL parameters.

When the user triggers Generate config, an onClick handler calls generateConfigFromBuilder and, on success, updates the in-memory representation of the generated configuration snippet, updates ConfigBuilderState.generatedConfigSnippetId if provided, and sets or replaces a snippetId URL parameter.

When the user presses the Copy config button, an onClick handler calls copyCodeSnippet with the current codeSnippetId (taken from the generatedConfigSnippet.id or the ConfigBuilderState.generatedConfigSnippetId) and uses the returned code string to perform clipboard operations in the UI layer.

When the user clicks the navigation link to the API reference, a standard anchor navigation occurs to api_reference.html with query parameters derived from the current builder state, enabling the API reference page to utilize the generated configuration.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters to mirror and control the Config Builder state. A taskType URL parameter, if present, is applied by calling updateConfigBuilderTaskType and is updated whenever the task type selector changes. A maxSamples URL parameter is parsed into a numeric value and applied via updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings, and it is updated each time the user edits the max_samples field. A metrics URL parameter stores a delimited list of selectedMetricIds and is synchronized whenever the metrics selector changes, again via updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings. A models URL parameter holds a serialized representation of the current ModelVariant collection; on load, if this parameter is present, the root component deserializes it and calls setConfigBuilderModels; after any models edit, the parameter is regenerated from the updated ModelVariant data returned by setConfigBuilderModels. A snippetId URL parameter optionally reflects the latest generated configuration snippet identifier; it is set after generateConfigFromBuilder completes and can also be included in outgoing navigation links. The page does not rely on localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms; instead, it treats the backend state returned by getConfigBuilderState as authoritative and uses URL parameters purely as a way to initialize and share configurations, with all mutations flowing through the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Orchestrates initial data loading, URL parameter synchronization, and distribution of ConfigBuilderState, ModelVariant, Metric, and generated config snippet data to child components.
event_handlers: onMount -> parse URL parameters (taskType, maxSamples, metrics, models, snippetId)
onMount -> getConfigBuilderState() to retrieve configBuilderState, modelVariants, availableMetrics
onMount (after getConfigBuilderState) -> if taskType URL parameter differs from configBuilderState.taskType then call updateConfigBuilderTaskType(taskType)
onMount (after getConfigBuilderState) -> if maxSamples or metrics URL parameters are present then call updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings(maxSamples, selectedMetricIds)
onMount (after getConfigBuilderState) -> if models URL parameter is present then deserialize and call setConfigBuilderModels(models)

onURLChange (browser navigation) -> re-run URL parsing and reconcile with backend state using getConfigBuilderState, updateConfigBuilderTaskType, updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings, and setConfigBuilderModels as needed
onChildStateChange -> update URL search parameters to reflect current taskType, maxSamples, metrics, models, and snippetId without reloading the page

components.functionality: Displays the current task type from ConfigBuilderState and allows the user to select a different task type, updating the builder state and URL accordingly.
event_handlers: onChange(taskTypeSelect) -> updateConfigBuilderTaskType(selectedTaskType)
onChange(taskTypeSelect) -> update URL parameter 'taskType' with selectedTaskType
onChange(taskTypeSelect) -> notify config-builder-page-container of task type change so dependent components can re-render or reset as appropriate

components.functionality: Displays the list of model variants associated with the current ConfigBuilderState, allows the user to add, edit, and remove candidate or baseline models and their temperatures, and persists the updated collection via setConfigBuilderModels.
event_handlers: onAddRow -> append a new empty model variant row in the local UI state
onRowChange(modelRow) -> update local representation of the model variants collection
onBlur(modelRow) or onSaveModels -> call setConfigBuilderModels(models) with the current full collection of model variants (including modelId, role, and temperature)
onBlur(modelRow) or onSaveModels (after setConfigBuilderModels success) -> replace local ModelVariant list with the returned modelVariants array
onBlur(modelRow) or onSaveModels (after setConfigBuilderModels success) -> serialize current ModelVariant list into a models URL parameter and update the URL
onDeleteRow(modelRow) -> remove the row from local state and call setConfigBuilderModels(models) with the remaining variants, then update the models URL parameter

components.functionality: Manages evaluation-wide settings such as max_samples and coordinates metric selection updates together with the metrics selector by calling updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings.
event_handlers: onChange(maxSamplesInput) -> update local maxSamples value and call updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings(maxSamples, selectedMetricIds)
onChange(maxSamplesInput) (after updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings success) -> update local ConfigBuilderState.maxSamples with the returned value
onChange(maxSamplesInput) -> update URL parameter 'maxSamples' with the current numeric value
onMetricsChange(selectedMetricIds from metrics-selector-panel) -> call updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings(maxSamples, selectedMetricIds)
onMetricsChange(selectedMetricIds from metrics-selector-panel) (after updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings success) -> update ConfigBuilderState.selectedMetricIds
onMetricsChange(selectedMetricIds from metrics-selector-panel) -> update URL parameter 'metrics' with a serialized list of selectedMetricIds

components.functionality: Shows a list of available metrics derived from the availableMetrics data returned by getConfigBuilderState and lets users toggle metrics on and off, feeding changes back to the evaluation-settings-form.
event_handlers: onMount -> consume availableMetrics passed from config-builder-page-container to render metric toggle controls keyed by metric IDs
onToggle(metricId) -> update local list of selectedMetricIds and notify evaluation-settings-form of the updated selection
onToggle(metricId) -> rely on evaluation-settings-form to call updateConfigBuilderEvaluationSettings and update URL parameter 'metrics'

components.functionality: Provides the Generate config control, handles the generation request, and forwards the resulting snippet and identifiers to the code output and navigation components.
event_handlers: onClick(generateConfigButton) -> call generateConfigFromBuilder() with no additional parameters

onClick(generateConfigButton) (after generateConfigFromBuilder success) -> store generatedConfigSnippet.id, generatedConfigSnippet.language, generatedConfigSnippet.snippetType, and generatedConfigSnippet.code in local state
onClick(generateConfigButton) (after generateConfigFromBuilder success) -> update ConfigBuilderState.generatedConfigSnippetId with generatedConfigSnippet.id if necessary in the local representation
onClick(generateConfigButton) (after generateConfigFromBuilder success) -> update URL parameter 'snippetId' with generatedConfigSnippet.id
onClick(generateConfigButton) (after generateConfigFromBuilder success) -> notify config-code-output and api-reference-link components to re-render with the new snippet data

components.functionality: Displays the generated configuration snippet as read-only code using the data returned from generateConfigFromBuilder or copyCodeSnippet, and exposes a control to trigger copying.
event_handlers: onSnippetUpdate(from generate-config-panel) -> render generatedConfigSnippet.code in a read-only code block and keep track of generatedConfigSnippet.id
onClick(copyButton) -> call copyCodeSnippet(codeSnippetId) using the currently tracked snippet ID (either generatedConfigSnippet.id or ConfigBuilderState.generatedConfigSnippetId)

onClick(copyButton) (after copyCodeSnippet success) -> optionally replace displayed code with the returned code text to ensure it matches backend state, and trigger any UI-level clipboard copy behavior

components.functionality: Renders a contextual link that navigates to the API reference page with query parameters referencing the current generated config snippet and indicating that the user came from the Config Builder Guide.
event_handlers: onSnippetUpdate(from generate-config-panel) -> update the href of the link to point to api_reference.html with a snippetId query parameter corresponding to generatedConfigSnippet.id and a source query parameter indicating the origin page
onRender -> disable or hide the link if no generated config snippet identifier is available in ConfigBuilderState or local CodeSnippet state
onClick(apiReferenceLink) -> perform standard browser navigation to api_reference.html with the constructed query parameters

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a classic three-column documentation layout. A fixed global header spans the full width at the top. Below it, the body is split into: a fixed-width left sidebar for global docs navigation, a fluid central column for the Config Builder guide and interactive controls, and a narrower right sidebar for the in-page table of contents (TOC). Within the central column, content follows a linear top-to-bottom flow: small page category label (e.g., 'Guides'), H1 title ('Config Builder'), short subtitle, an introductory paragraph, then a sequence of full-width interactive panels (task type, models, metrics, settings, generate config, and output). The api-reference-link appears as a subtle, inline navigational element near the end of the main flow, treated like a secondary call-to-action.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a classic three-column documentation layout. A fixed global header spans the full width at the top. Below it, the body is split into: a fixed-width left sidebar for global docs navigation, a fluid central column for the Config Builder guide and interactive controls, and a narrower right sidebar for the in-page table of contents (TOC). Within the central column, content follows a linear top-to-bottom flow: small page category label (e.g., 'Guides'), H1 title ('Config Builder'), short subtitle, an introductory paragraph, then a sequence of full-width interactive panels (task type, models, metrics, settings, generate config, and output). The api-reference-link appears as a subtle, inline navigational element near the end of the main flow, treated like a secondary call-to-action.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The viewport width is divided roughly into 20–25% for the left sidebar, 50–55% for the central content column, and 20–25% for the right sidebar. The central column is visually dominant and centered. Each interactive component within the central column typically spans 100% of that column's width, with internal padding around 16–24px. Vertical spacing between related controls inside a panel follows 8–16px, while vertical spacing between major panels uses 24–40px. The code output panel visually mirrors the 'full-width media block' pattern from the mockup, occupying the full central column width and standing out as a key endpoint of the flow.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderately spacious. The central column uses comfortable line-height and generous white space around headings and paragraphs. Interactive panels have enough padding and spacing to feel structured and scannable without appearing crowded—form controls are grouped logically with 8–12px gaps within groups and 16–24px between groups. Sidebars are denser but still readable: navigation items and TOC entries are stacked with compact vertical rhythm (~8px), but each item has enough padding to be tapped or clicked comfortably.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the top-left logo in the global header, moves to a centered search input, then to a right-aligned primary CTA, following a Z-pattern. From there, attention drops diagonally to the left sidebar to orient the user within 'Guides' and the specific 'Config Builder' page. The eye then moves into the central H1 title and introductory copy. Highlighted active nav items in the left sidebar and the colored page label reinforce context. After the intro, the user's focus moves down through the interactive panels in sequence (task type → models → metrics → evaluation settings → generate → output). The right-hand TOC provides a secondary scan path, letting users jump between sections of the builder. The code output panel at the bottom of the main flow and the subtle api-reference-link provide a clear visual endpoint and next-step option.

layout.overall_description: Below a fixed, full-width header with a logo on the left, a centered search field, and a right-aligned primary CTA, the page resolves into a three-column documentation layout. On the left, a fixed-width sidebar occupies roughly a quarter of the screen, listing top-level tabs (Documentation, API Reference, Blog) and a vertical navigation tree, with 'Guides' and the 'Config Builder' guide highlighted. In the center, a wider content column holds the main guide: a small colored 'Guides' label sits at the top, followed by a large 'Config Builder' H1, a concise subtitle, and a short introductory paragraph explaining how to construct evaluation configurations. Below this text, the central column transitions into a series of spacious, card-like panels stacked vertically, each representing a step in configuring the evaluation: choosing task type, editing models, selecting metrics, adjusting evaluation settings, generating the config, and finally viewing the resulting JSON or code. Each panel spans the full central column width with consistent padding and subtle borders or shadows, separated by comfortable vertical gaps. To the right, a narrower sidebar lists 'On this page' with links to each section of the Config Builder, indented according to heading level and kept visible via sticky positioning. The overall effect is a calm, modern, developer-friendly workspace where the main interactive flow runs down the center, framed by context on the left and structural wayfinding on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The config-builder-page-container occupies the entire central content column beneath the global header, starting approximately 24–32px below the header’s bottom edge. Horizontally, it spans about 50–55% of the viewport width, centered between the left navigation sidebar and the right TOC sidebar, with 32–40px gutters on either side. At its very top, the container includes the small page category label ('Guides') aligned to the left, followed by 8–12px of vertical space, then the H1 'Config Builder' with generous top and bottom margins (24–32px). Directly under the H1, a short subtitle appears in a slightly muted text color, with a 12–16px gap before a 2–3 line introductory paragraph. After a 24–32px gap below the intro, the main interactive panels (task-type-selector, models-table-editor, metrics-selector-panel, evaluation-settings-form, generate-config-panel, config-code-output) are stacked vertically in the order described, each spanning the full width of this container and aligned to a consistent left content column. The api-reference-link is placed near the bottom of the container, aligned to the right edge of the text content within the central column, after the code output, with about 24px of top margin separating it from the code viewer. Overall, the container behaves as the primary scrollable column, with generous vertical spacing (24–40px) between major sections and consistent internal padding for each panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow; scrolls with the page while being visually framed between a fixed header and sticky sidebars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The task-type-selector is the first interactive panel in the central column, positioned directly below the introductory paragraph with approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing. It appears as a full-width card-like block within the config-builder-page-container, aligned flush with the central column’s left and right content edges (leaving the same inner margins as the intro text). The panel has 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, with a concise section header (e.g., '1. Choose task type') at the top left, followed by 8–12px of space and a short helper description. Below that, a set of task type options (e.g., radio buttons, pills, or a dropdown) are arranged horizontally if space allows, wrapping to new lines on smaller screens, with 8–12px gaps between options. Vertically, the controls and helper text are grouped tightly enough to feel like a cohesive step, but the panel is separated from the next component (models-table-editor) by a clear 24–32px gap. This positioning makes the task-type-selector feel like the logical first step in the flow, occupying roughly 15–20% of the central column’s vertical viewport on initial load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the central column; it does not stick, but its early placement in the scroll order makes it highly visible on page load.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The models-table-editor panel is positioned immediately below the task-type-selector, separated by a vertical gap of about 24px. It also spans the full width of the central content column, maintaining the same left and right alignment as the previous section. Internally, it uses a card-like layout with 16–24px padding. A section header such as '2. Configure models' sits at the top-left of the panel, with 8–12px of space before a short explanatory line. Below this, a table-like editor fills most of the panel’s width, with columns for model name, provider, parameters, and any advanced options. Rows are arranged with 8–12px vertical row height plus internal padding, and there is a clear horizontal rule or subtle row separation. Controls like 'Add model' or 'Duplicate row' are aligned to the top-right or bottom-right inside the panel with 16–24px of space from the panel’s edges. This component typically occupies more vertical space than the task-type-selector (roughly 20–30% of the central viewport height), but remains comfortably separated from the following metrics-selector-panel by a 24–32px gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow; the table content scrolls with the page and does not lock or stick, though the header row remains visually fixed within the panel using standard table styling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The evaluation-settings-form is positioned beneath the metrics-selector-panel (which sits between models-table-editor and this form), separated from the metrics panel by 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the central column, using a card-style container with 16–24px internal padding. At the top-left, a section heading such as '4. Evaluation settings' aligns with the left edge of the text column, followed by a short line of helper text. Inside the panel, settings fields (e.g., number of samples, max tokens, temperature, concurrency, timeouts) are arranged in a responsive grid of two columns where horizontal space allows, falling back to a single column on narrower viewports. Each field has 8–12px vertical spacing from the next, and 16–24px spacing between logical groups. The bottom of the panel aligns to the same baseline grid as other components, ensuring that there is a consistent 24–32px gap between this form and the generate-config-panel below it. The overall impression is a structured, medium-density form that fits neatly into the vertical narrative of the builder.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the central column; fields and labels scroll naturally with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metrics-selector-panel sits directly between the models-table-editor and the evaluation-settings-form, separated from both by consistent 24–32px vertical gaps. It spans the full width of the central column, matching the alignment and padding of the other card-like panels. A heading such as '3. Select metrics' is aligned to the top-left of the panel, with 8–12px of space below it for a concise description. Within the panel, metrics are presented either as grouped checkboxes/pills or as a list with toggles, arranged in one or two columns depending on available width. Each metric entry has a clear label and optionally a one-line description beneath, with 8–12px spacing between entries. Metric groups (e.g., 'Quality', 'Safety', 'Latency') are separated by 16–24px of vertical space and may have subtle group labels in a smaller, muted font. The panel’s height is moderate to tall, depending on the number of metrics, but it remains visually balanced by generous internal spacing and a clear bottom margin before the next section. Its position emphasizes that metrics selection is a central step, sitting roughly in the middle of the builder flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow; it scrolls naturally and does not use sticky behavior, keeping the focus on sequential progression.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The generate-config-panel is placed directly below the evaluation-settings-form, with about 24–32px of vertical space between them, and directly above the config-code-output, from which it is separated by a slightly smaller gap (about 16–24px) to emphasize their close relationship. It spans the full width of the central column, but is visually more compact than the data-entry panels. Internally, it has 16–24px padding, with a left-aligned headline like '5. Generate configuration' and a brief explanation. The primary action button (e.g., 'Generate config') is placed prominently near the bottom-right of the panel content, aligned horizontally with the headline’s baseline or slightly below, following the Z-pattern within the panel itself (text on the left, primary action on the right). Secondary controls such as a small dropdown for output format (JSON, Python, CLI) may sit to the left of the button with 8–12px spacing. This panel feels like a transition between input and output: it’s visually linked upwards to the forms above and downwards to the code output beneath. On initial view of the lower half of the page, it occupies roughly 10–15% of the visible central column height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; does not fix in place, but its proximity to the config-code-output creates a tight functional grouping as the trigger for the output below.
