component_layouts.layout_narrative: The config-code-output sits immediately below the generate-config-panel, with a relatively tight 16–24px vertical gap to emphasize cause-and-effect between the 'Generate' action and the displayed configuration. It is treated like the primary 'media block' of the page, spanning the full width of the central column and extending vertically enough to show multiple lines of code (typically 200–320px tall by default, adjustable if resizable). The code viewer is contained within a card-like frame with a subtle border or shadow, 16–24px internal padding, and a code-focused monospace font. A small header bar at the top of the viewer may include a label like 'Generated config' on the left and tabs or a dropdown for format (JSON / Python / YAML) on the right, aligned to the panel's inner edges. The code area itself is scrollable within the panel if content exceeds the default height, ensuring the overall page scroll remains smooth. This section is visually dense with information but balanced by generous space above and below. It reads as the culmination of the builder flow, occupying a strong focal position towards the bottom of the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page, but the inner code area is scrollable if content overflows; it does not stick to the viewport edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The api-reference-link is a subtle, tertiary navigation element positioned near the end of the central content column, just below the config-code-output. It is separated from the code viewer by approximately 24px of vertical space so it reads as a follow-up action rather than part of the output itself. The link is right-aligned within the central column’s text bounds, sitting on a single line that aligns horizontally with the right edge of the heading text and buttons above. Its styling is understated (standard link color, maybe a small inline arrow) and its font size matches body text or is slightly smaller. There is generous whitespace below (32–40px) before the end of the page content, ensuring the link doesn’t feel cramped at the bottom. In the overall visual flow, it serves as a gentle next step, guiding users toward the full API reference once they’re comfortable with the interactive builder.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the main content; no sticky behavior. It scrolls out of view as the user moves past the bottom of the page.

name: JavaScript SDK
title: JavaScript SDK
description: Documentation page that loads JavaScript SDK methods and code examples, lets users configure a batch evaluation example (including concurrency), and copy the resulting code. It also links to the underlying REST API reference.

core_features: Load JavaScript SDK documentation content (page metadata, SDK methods, and code snippets) using the getJavascriptSdkPage interface.
Display a selectable list of JavaScript SDK methods and show detailed information for the currently selected method.
Render SDK-related code snippets for the selected method, including a configurable batch evaluation example backed by the configureRunBatchEvaluationExample interface.
Allow users to adjust batch evaluation options (such as concurrency) via form controls and immediately update the associated JavaScript example snippet through configureRunBatchEvaluationExample.
Provide a copy action for any displayed code snippet by calling the copyCodeSnippet interface and sending the code to the clipboard.
Offer navigation from SDK methods to the REST API reference page (api_reference.html) by constructing links with URL parameters derived from the currently selected method and page data.
user_workflows: View JavaScript SDK methods and examples: 1) User navigates to sdk_javascript.html (optionally with URL parameters). 2) The page calls getJavascriptSdkPage to retrieve the DocPage, SdkMethod list, and CodeSnippets. 3) The page uses URL parameters (if present) to determine the active SDK method and primary snippet. 4) The user sees method tabs, selects a method, and the method detail and code snippet components update accordingly.

Configure and preview a batch evaluation example: 1) User selects the SDK method that is associated with the batch evaluation example (identified via its exampleSnippetId, which corresponds to the configurable CodeSnippet). 2) The page reads any existing concurrency URL parameter. 3) If a concurrency value is present, the page calls configureRunBatchEvaluationExample with the snippet ID and the concurrency value to obtain an updated CodeSnippet. 4) The updated snippet is rendered. 5) The user changes the concurrency value in the configuration form, triggering another call to configureRunBatchEvaluationExample; the snippet is updated again and the new concurrency is reflected in the URL.

Copy the configured JavaScript code example: 1) After adjusting configuration (such as concurrency) and seeing the updated code snippet, the user clicks the "Copy" button on that snippet. 2) The page calls copyCodeSnippet with the snippet ID. 3) If the response indicates success, the client copies the returned code string to the clipboard and may show a transient success message. 4) If not successful, the client shows an error message based on the returned message field.

Navigate from the JavaScript SDK to the REST API reference: 1) With an SDK method selected, the user clicks a "View REST API reference" link. 2) The page constructs a URL to api_reference.html, including query parameters derived from the current DocPage and SdkMethod (for example, page ID and method ID). 3) The browser navigates to api_reference.html with those parameters, where the API reference page can resolve and show the related REST endpoint.

Deep-link to a specific method and configuration: 1) A user receives or bookmarks a URL like sdk_javascript.html with query parameters (for example, methodId and concurrency). 2) On load, the page parses the query parameters to pre-select the specified SDK method and concurrency value. 3) The page calls getJavascriptSdkPage, then, once data is available, applies the methodId to choose the active SdkMethod and determines the associated CodeSnippet. 4) If a concurrency value is present, the page calls configureRunBatchEvaluationExample for that snippet, so the rendered example matches the shared URL. 5) When the user changes tabs or configuration, the URL is updated so it can be shared again.
interactions: On initial page load, the page parses the current URL query parameters (for example, methodId, snippetId, concurrency) and calls getJavascriptSdkPage. After data loads, the parsed parameters are used to set the active SDK method and snippet.

When the user clicks on a method tab, the active method is set to the corresponding SdkMethod, the view updates to show its details and any associated snippets, and the URL query parameter for methodId is updated to match.

When the user focuses or changes the concurrency input in the batch evaluation configuration form, the component validates the input in the client and, on change or submit, calls configureRunBatchEvaluationExample with the current snippet ID and the new concurrency value. The returned updated CodeSnippet is bound back into the code snippet display, and the concurrency URL parameter is updated.

When the user clicks the copy button on any code snippet, the component calls copyCodeSnippet with that snippet's ID. If success is true, the returned code is placed onto the clipboard. A transient success or error indication is shown using only in-memory state (no persistent storage).

When the user clicks an SDK-to-API-reference link, the browser navigates to api_reference.html with query parameters derived from the current DocPage and SdkMethod (for example, docPageId and sdkMethodId), enabling the API Reference page to show the relevant REST endpoint for that SDK method.

When the browser's back or forward navigation changes the URL (for example, previous methodId or concurrency), a URL state sync component listens to the change, re-evaluates the active method and configuration, and, if necessary, re-invokes configureRunBatchEvaluationExample to keep the visible snippet consistent with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies entirely on URL query parameters and in-memory component state, without using localStorage or other client persistence. On load and on every URL change, the page parses query parameters such as methodId (selected SDK method), snippetId (selected CodeSnippet, typically the exampleSnippetId of the active SdkMethod), and concurrency (current concurrency configuration value for the batch evaluation example). After getJavascriptSdkPage returns the DocPage, SdkMethod array, and CodeSnippet array, the page resolves: (1) activeMethodId: if methodId is present and matches a SdkMethod.id, it is used; otherwise, the first available SdkMethod (by returned order) is selected. (2) activeSnippetId: if snippetId is present and matches a CodeSnippet.id, it is used; otherwise, if the active SdkMethod has an exampleSnippetId, that ID is used; otherwise, a method-specific default selection rule is applied (for example, the first CodeSnippet that belongs to this DocPage and is marked as an SDK usage snippet). (3) concurrency: if present and valid, it is used as the input value for the batch evaluation configuration; if it is valid and the active snippet is the one controlled by configureRunBatchEvaluationExample, the page calls configureRunBatchEvaluationExample with that snippet ID and concurrency to obtain an updated CodeSnippet and then replaces the corresponding item in the in-memory CodeSnippet array. Any user action that changes the selected method, snippet, or concurrency updates the URL via history.pushState or history.replaceState so that the URL always reflects the current view. Navigation to api_reference.html is implemented purely as a URL change where parameters like fromDocPageId and sdkMethodId are appended based on the current DocPage.id and SdkMethod.id.

components.functionality: Initializes the JavaScript SDK page by parsing URL parameters, calling getJavascriptSdkPage, and seeding the in-memory state with the DocPage metadata, SdkMethod list, and CodeSnippets. It then determines the active method and snippet based on the URL and available data.
event_handlers: onInit
onGetJavascriptSdkPageSuccess
onGetJavascriptSdkPageError

components.functionality: Manages synchronization between the browser URL and the in-memory page state for selected method, selected snippet, and concurrency configuration. It reads methodId, snippetId, and concurrency from the URL on load and on popstate, and writes these values back into the URL when they change in the UI.
event_handlers: onUrlChange
onActiveMethodChange
onActiveSnippetChange
onConcurrencyChange

components.functionality: Displays the JavaScript SDK page title and description using DocPage data, and can expose high-level metadata such as the documentation category. It does not fetch data itself but consumes the loaded DocPage from the javascript-sdk-loader.

Renders a tab or list selector for all available SdkMethods returned by getJavascriptSdkPage. When the user selects a method, it updates the active method in shared state, triggers a URL update for methodId through sdk-url-state-sync, and informs downstream components (method detail and code snippets) to re-render.
event_handlers: onClickMethodTab

components.functionality: Shows detailed information for the currently selected SdkMethod, including its name and description. It also exposes a navigational link to the REST API reference page (api_reference.html), constructing a URL with query parameters based on the current DocPage and SdkMethod so that the API reference can resolve the corresponding REST endpoint.
event_handlers: onClickApiReferenceLink

components.functionality: Provides controls for configuring the JavaScript batch evaluation example associated with a specific CodeSnippet (for example, the snippet referenced by the active SdkMethod.exampleSnippetId). It presents a concurrency input bound to the current concurrency value. On initialization, if a concurrency value exists in the URL and a matching snippet is found, it calls configureRunBatchEvaluationExample with the snippet ID and concurrency to obtain an updated CodeSnippet. When the user changes the concurrency value and confirms the change (for example, blur or submit), it calls configureRunBatchEvaluationExample again, updates the in-memory CodeSnippet with the returned updatedCodeSnippet, and triggers sdk-url-state-sync to update the concurrency query parameter. This component does not modify code text locally; all code updates come from the configureRunBatchEvaluationExample interface so that copyCodeSnippet returns the correctly updated code.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl
onChangeConcurrencyInput
onSubmitConcurrency
onConfigureRunBatchEvaluationExampleSuccess
onConfigureRunBatchEvaluationExampleError

components.functionality: Displays the currently selected CodeSnippet for the active SDK method. It reads the snippet's language, snippetType, code, hasCopyButton, and isEditable fields from the loaded CodeSnippet data. For the batch evaluation example snippet that is updated via configureRunBatchEvaluationExample, the component re-renders whenever the snippet's code changes in memory. If hasCopyButton is true and the user clicks copy, it calls copyCodeSnippet with the snippet ID and, on success, copies the returned code value to the clipboard. Any transient status messages are managed in component state only.
event_handlers: onSelectSnippet
onClickCopy
onCopyCodeSnippetSuccess
onCopyCodeSnippetError

components.functionality: Determines which CodeSnippets correspond to the currently active SdkMethod. It uses the SdkMethod.exampleSnippetId to find the primary example snippet and filters the CodeSnippet list from getJavascriptSdkPage by docPageId and other attributes (such as snippetType) to assemble a list of relevant snippets for the method. It exposes the primary snippet ID to both sdk-code-snippet-block and batch-evaluation-example-configurator and updates the snippetId URL parameter via sdk-url-state-sync whenever the primary snippet changes.
event_handlers: onActiveMethodChange
onResolvePrimarySnippet
onPrimarySnippetChange

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: All page-specific components (header, method tabs, method detail, configurator, and code snippet) live in the central content column, stacked vertically in a clear narrative: page header at the top, then method-selection tabs, followed by the selected method’s textual detail, and finally a full-width interactive example block containing the batch-evaluation configurator on the left and the live code snippet on the right. Non-visual components (loader, URL state sync, snippet mapper) execute behind the scenes, aligned logically with this order but not occupying visual space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All page-specific components (header, method tabs, method detail, configurator, and code snippet) live in the central content column, stacked vertically in a clear narrative: page header at the top, then method-selection tabs, followed by the selected method’s textual detail, and finally a full-width interactive example block containing the batch-evaluation configurator on the left and the live code snippet on the right. Non-visual components (loader, URL state sync, snippet mapper) execute behind the scenes, aligned logically with this order but not occupying visual space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page continues the three-column documentation layout: left sidebar ~22% width, central content column ~56% width, right sidebar TOC ~22% width on a typical desktop viewport. Within the central column, all textual content (header + method detail) spans nearly the full content width, with generous left/right padding (around 24px) to maintain a readable line length. The interactive example block (configurator + code snippet) occupies the full central column width, internally split into a 40% (left) configurator panel and 60% (right) code panel, resembling a side-by-side playground. Vertical spacing follows the mockup: ~32–40px between major blocks; 16–24px between related sub-elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography is low-to-medium density: generous line-height, clear paragraph spacing, and breathing room around headings and code. The header and method detail read like calm documentation text with comfortable margins, while the configurator and tabs are a bit denser, reflecting their functional, UI-heavy role but still respecting 8–12px internal padding and clear separation between control groups. Navigation (tabs and any inline links) remains scannable and never cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s gaze starts at the global header (logo → centered search → top-right CTA), then drops into the central column, beginning with the context label and JavaScript SDK page title. From there, the eyes move down through the intro text to the method tabs, where they choose a method. After a tab is selected, attention flows down into the method detail text and then into the visually prominent interactive block: first to the left-side configurator controls, then across to the right-side code snippet where changes are reflected. The right sidebar (On this page) mirrors the headings found in the method detail, enabling a secondary left-to-right scanning pattern between content and TOC. Active states (selected tab, current TOC item) subtly highlight the user’s current context.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a familiar three-column documentation shell. A fixed global header stretches across the top, with a compact height and ample horizontal padding, containing the logo on the far left, a wide, centered search field, and a primary call-to-action button on the right. Beneath this, the viewport is divided into three vertical regions: a fixed-width left sidebar for global and section navigation, a wider central column for the JavaScript SDK documentation content, and a narrower right sidebar that lists the in-page headings. Within the central column, the JavaScript SDK page header appears first: a small purple category label (e.g., “SDKs”), a large, bold H1 title “JavaScript SDK,” a single-line subtitle clarifying what this page covers, and a short introductory paragraph that explains how the SDK relates to evaluations and the REST API. Below this, a horizontal row of method tabs lets users switch between SDK methods. Under the active tab, the method’s detail section presents an overview, parameters, and notes laid out in a clean, text-first style. Anchored directly beneath this narrative, a full-width interactive example block takes the place of the mockup’s screenshot: a light, card-like container with subtle border and padding that spans the central column. Inside the card, the batch evaluation configurator sits on the left as a vertical stack of control groups (model selection, dataset, concurrency, etc.), while on the right a code panel shows a JavaScript snippet reflecting the current configuration, with a copy button in the top-right of the code block. As users scroll, the central column’s content flows linearly downward, while the left sidebar stays pinned for section switching and the right sidebar’s TOC remains visible, highlighting the current heading drawn from the method detail. Non-visual data and mapping components work invisibly to load methods, sync URL state with the chosen method, and keep the snippet aligned with the configurator without disrupting this clean, predictable layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is logically positioned at the very top of the central content column, just below the global header and above the visible JavaScript SDK page header, but it does not render any visible UI. It occupies no measurable height or width in the layout; instead, it runs before visible components render, fetching the list of JavaScript SDK methods and example metadata that will populate the method tabs, method detail content, configurator defaults, and code snippet. Conceptually, imagine it as an invisible layer that spans the full width of the central column at the top of the page, ensuring that by the time the user sees the header and tabs, all necessary data is already available.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, occupies zero visual space; executes at the top of the central content column lifecycle before rendering user-facing components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component is also non-visual and sits conceptually alongside the method tabs at the top portion of the central column. It does not have a visible footprint, but you can think of it as overlaying the method tabs horizontally: as the user selects a method tab or modifies certain key parameters (such as chosen method or example variant), this component keeps the URL query parameters and/or hash in sync. It spans the same horizontal region as the tabs, just beneath the page header, but introduces no additional spacing or visible elements between the header and tabs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height layer logically bound to the method tabs; listens and updates URL state without affecting layout or scroll position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The SDK page header is the first visible block in the central content column, positioned directly under the global header with a generous top margin of about 32–40px to separate it from the fixed site chrome. It spans nearly the full width of the central column (about 90–95% of that column’s width), with 24px horizontal padding on each side to keep the text comfortably centered and the line length readable. At the very top of this block, a small, uppercase category label (e.g., “SDKs” or “Guides”) appears in a subtle brand accent color, aligned to the left. About 8–12px below that, the main H1 title “JavaScript SDK” is set in large, bold type, occupying roughly 1–2 lines and extending across most of the column width. Another 8–12px below, a single-line subtitle (H2-style or lead text) is set in medium-weight, smaller typography, slightly softer in color. Finally, 16–24px below the subtitle, one or two short paragraphs of introductory text span the same width, with generous line-height and clear paragraph spacing. The bottom of the header block leaves about 32px of space before the method tabs start, preserving a clear break between introductory context and interactive navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; scrolls with the page, sitting just below the fixed global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The method tabs component sits directly below the SDK page header, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the central content column, with tab labels aligned horizontally from left to right. Each tab is a pill or underline-style button with 12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding, and they are arranged in a single row that hugs the left edge of the content column. The active tab uses the brand accent color and a bolder label to stand out, while inactive tabs are muted but still clearly legible. There is about 8–12px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the tabs and the start of the method detail content, providing a visual separation between navigation and content. On narrower viewports, the tabs may wrap to a second line or become horizontally scrollable, but they remain visually anchored directly above the method detail section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily static, scrolling with the content; may optionally become sticky within the central column on tall pages, pinning to the top of the central area once scrolled into place (while remaining below the global header) to keep method switching readily accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The method detail component appears immediately beneath the method tabs, with a modest 8–12px gap so it feels connected to the selected tab. It occupies the same full width as the header within the central column (about 90–95% of the column width with consistent side padding). The content is arranged vertically: a method-specific H2 (e.g., “runBatchEvaluation”) at the top, followed by a short descriptive paragraph, then subsections like “Parameters,” “Response,” and “Usage notes.” Each subsection has a heading (H3) and either a short paragraph, bullet list, or small inline code examples. Vertical spacing between these subsections is around 16–24px, keeping the content readable and scannable without feeling cramped. The main section and subsection headings align to the left and feed into the right sidebar’s TOC, so the user can see which part of the method detail they’re in as they scroll. At the bottom of the method detail block, there is a slightly larger gap (around 32px) before the interactive example begins, creating a clear transition from conceptual explanation to hands-on example.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content that scrolls normally within the central column; its headings are mirrored and highlighted in the right sidebar’s sticky TOC as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The batch evaluation example configurator lives inside a full-width interactive card that sits directly below the method detail section in the central column. The card itself spans nearly the entire central column width (about 95% of the column, aligned with the text above), with 16–24px internal padding on all sides and a subtle border or shadow that echoes the mockup’s media block. Within this card, the configurator occupies the left side of an internal two-column layout: roughly 40% of the card’s width on desktop. It is anchored to the left edge with a vertical stack of control groups—each group separated by 12–16px of vertical spacing. Controls might include dropdowns for SDK method variants, numeric inputs for concurrency, toggles for options like streaming, and perhaps dataset selection. Each control has a short label above it and uses 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding to remain touch-friendly but compact. The configurator column stretches from the top of the card nearly to the bottom, with its content aligned to the top so the first controls are immediately adjacent to the card’s top edge. On narrower screens, this configurator column shifts to sit above the code snippet, spanning 100% of the card width, preserving comfortable vertical spacing (16–24px) between the last control and the code panel below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the interactive card; scrolls with the central content. Within the card, the configurator participates in a responsive internal two-column layout that collapses to a single column on small viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The code snippet block shares the same interactive card container as the batch evaluation configurator but occupies the right side of the card on desktop, taking up roughly 60% of the card’s width. It is aligned horizontally with the configurator: the top of the code panel sits at the same vertical height as the top of the configurator’s first control, creating a balanced side-by-side layout. Inside its area, the snippet is displayed in a monospaced code block that spans nearly the full width of the right column, with 16–24px internal padding and a subtle background contrast (slightly darker than the card background) to differentiate it. A small toolbar at the top-right of the code block contains a copy button (and optionally a language label like “JavaScript”), with 8–12px of padding and minimal iconography. The code lines wrap naturally, but the line length remains reasonable because of the right column’s width constraint. There is about 16px of vertical spacing between this toolbar and the code body, and 12–16px of padding beneath the last line of code before the edge of the code panel. On narrow viewports, the code snippet block drops below the configurator, stretching to 100% of the card width while maintaining its internal paddings and visual hierarchy. As users manipulate the configurator on the left, the code on the right updates, reinforcing the direct relationship between these two halves of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the interactive card, updating content dynamically in response to configurator changes. It participates in the same responsive behavior as the configurator: side-by-side on larger screens and stacked below on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The method snippet mapper is an invisible logic layer that conceptually sits between the method detail + configurator and the code snippet block. It has no visible UI and therefore occupies no explicit space in the layout. You can imagine it as a transparent connector that horizontally bridges the left-side configurator area and the right-side code snippet area inside the interactive card: it listens to the selected SDK method (from the method tabs and method detail context) and the current configuration options, then generates the appropriate JavaScript snippet to display. It does not alter spacing or alignment; it simply ensures the content in the code snippet remains in sync with the rest of the page state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; logically lives within the interactive example block, mediating state between method selection/configuration and the code block without affecting layout or scroll behavior.

name: Examples Gallery
title: Examples Gallery
description: Browse and filter end-to-end evaluation examples by metric and dataset size, and open detailed documentation for each example.

core_features: Load the Examples Gallery page metadata, current filter state, available metrics, and initial evaluation examples using getExamplesGalleryPage.
Display a filter form with a metric dropdown and a minimum dataset size input, pre-populated from the current ExamplesGalleryFilterState and URL parameters when present.
Update the server-side filter state (selected metric and minimum dataset size) via updateExamplesGalleryFilters when the user applies filters.
Fetch and render the evaluation examples that match the current filter state using getFilteredEvaluationExamples after filters are applied.
Allow navigation to an Example Detail page by clicking any example card in the filtered list, passing the exampleId via URL parameters.
Synchronize filter state with URL query parameters (metricId, minSize) so that links from other pages can pre-filter the gallery and the current filtered view can be bookmarked or shared.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open gallery from header navigation
1. User clicks the "Examples" link in the header navigation.
2. Browser navigates to examples.html without query parameters (or with any existing ones).
3. On page load, the client calls getExamplesGalleryPage.
4. The page shell receives docPage, filterState, availableMetrics, and examples.
5. The filter form initializes its controls from filterState (which may be influenced by URL parameters on the server side).
6. The examples list component renders the examples returned by getExamplesGalleryPage.
7. User can scroll and click any example card to open its Example Detail page.

Workflow: Open gallery with preselected metric and dataset size
1. User clicks a contextual link (for example from a metrics concept page or the index page) that points to examples.html with metricId and/or minSize query parameters.
2. Browser loads examples.html?metricId=...&minSize=....
3. On page load, the client calls getExamplesGalleryPage.
4. The backend uses the URL parameters (via server routing) to determine the initial ExamplesGalleryFilterState returned in filterState.
5. The filter form reads filterState.selectedMetricId and filterState.minDatasetSize to preselect the metric dropdown and the minimum dataset size input.
6. The examples list renders the examples collection returned from getExamplesGalleryPage, which is already filtered according to the initial filterState.
7. User may adjust filters and reapply if desired.

Workflow: Apply or change filters inside the gallery
1. User changes the metric selection in the metric dropdown and/or updates the minimum dataset size input in the filter form.
2. User clicks the "Apply filters" control in the filter form.
3. The filter form validates the inputs (e.g., ensures the dataset size input is numeric or empty).
4. On successful validation, the client calls updateExamplesGalleryFilters with the current metric dropdown value as selectedMetricId and the parsed numeric input as minDatasetSize (or null/undefined if cleared).
5. When updateExamplesGalleryFilters resolves, the client receives an updated filterState.
6. The client updates the browser URL query parameters to reflect the new filterState (metricId and minSize), without reloading the page.
7. The client calls getFilteredEvaluationExamples to fetch the examples that match the updated filterState.
8. The examples list component re-renders with the new examples collection.
9. User can repeat steps 1–8 as needed.

Workflow: Open an example detail from the filtered gallery
1. After filters are applied, the examples list displays a set of example cards in a defined order.
2. User identifies a specific example card (e.g., the third item currently visible) and clicks it.
3. The click handler reads the example's id from the bound EvaluationExample object.
4. Browser navigates to example_detail.html?exampleId={id} according to the outgoing navigation configuration.
5. The Example Detail page uses exampleId to load the detailed content relevant to that evaluation example.

Workflow: Share or bookmark a filtered gallery view
1. User adjusts filters and clicks "Apply filters".
2. After updateExamplesGalleryFilters and getFilteredEvaluationExamples complete, the filter form logic updates the URL to include metricId and minSize that match the active filterState.
3. User copies the current URL from the browser address bar or bookmarks it.
4. When this URL is opened in the future or by another user, the page reloads.
5. The client again calls getExamplesGalleryPage on page load; the server takes the query parameters into account when computing filterState.
6. The gallery opens already filtered to the same metric and minimum dataset size that were active when the URL was shared.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: Browser navigation to examples.html with or without query parameters.
- Behavior: The page shell component calls getExamplesGalleryPage with no arguments.
- Result: The page receives docPage metadata, current filterState, availableMetrics, and an initial examples list. The filter form and examples list components render based on these values.

Interaction: Metric dropdown change
- Trigger: User selects a different metric in the metric dropdown in the filter form.
- Behavior: The filter form updates its local control state for the selected metric but does not immediately call any interface.
- Result: The new selection is considered only when the user clicks "Apply filters".

Interaction: Minimum dataset size input change
- Trigger: User types in the minimum dataset size input.
- Behavior: The filter form updates its local control state for the dataset size (as text), allowing partial input while typing.
- Result: The new value is parsed and validated only when the user clicks "Apply filters".

Interaction: Apply filters
- Trigger: User clicks the "Apply filters" control (e.g., a button) in the filter form.
- Behavior:
  1. The filter form validates the current metric selection and dataset size input.
  2. If validation passes, the form calls updateExamplesGalleryFilters(selectedMetricId, minDatasetSize).
  3. After receiving the updated filterState, the page updates the URL query parameters metricId and minSize to match filterState.selectedMetricId and filterState.minDatasetSize.
  4. The page then calls getFilteredEvaluationExamples.
  5. The examples list re-renders with the returned examples.
- Result: The gallery shows examples consistent with the new filter selections, and the URL reflects those filters.

Interaction: Clear metric filter (if user deselects metric)
- Trigger: User clears the metric selection in the dropdown (e.g., selects a "no metric" option, if available in the UI layer).
- Behavior: On "Apply filters", updateExamplesGalleryFilters is called with selectedMetricId set to an empty or null value, and minDatasetSize set based on input.
- Result: FilterState.selectedMetricId becomes unset; results reflect only the dataset size filter (or no filters if minDatasetSize is also unset). The URL metricId query parameter is removed or emptied accordingly.

Interaction: Clear minimum dataset size (if user clears input)
- Trigger: User clears the dataset size input (leaves it empty) and clicks "Apply filters".
- Behavior: The dataset size input is interpreted as no minimum; updateExamplesGalleryFilters is called with minDatasetSize unset or null.
- Result: FilterState.minDatasetSize becomes unset; results reflect only the metric filter (or no filters if metric is also unset). The URL minSize query parameter is removed or emptied accordingly.

Interaction: Click on example card
- Trigger: User clicks anywhere on an example card in the examples list.
- Behavior: The examples list component reads the bound EvaluationExample.id of the clicked card.
- Result: The browser navigates to example_detail.html?exampleId={id} in accordance with the outgoing navigation configuration.

Interaction: Reload page with active filters
- Trigger: User reloads the browser while on a filtered gallery URL (metricId and/or minSize present).
- Behavior: The browser reloads examples.html with the same query parameters; the client again calls getExamplesGalleryPage.
- Result: The returned filterState and examples correspond to the parameters; the UI restores the same filtered view without any additional user actions.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The authoritative filter state for the Examples Gallery is maintained server-side as an ExamplesGalleryFilterState and is exposed through the assigned interfaces. URL query parameters act as an external representation of this state for deep linking and navigation, but the page never persists state in localStorage or other client-side storage.

State sources and flow:
- On page load, the browser navigates to examples.html, possibly with metricId and minSize query parameters. The client immediately calls getExamplesGalleryPage with no arguments. The server interprets any URL parameters (via routing and controller logic outside this page) and returns a filterState whose selectedMetricId and minDatasetSize reflect those parameters when valid.
- The filter form initializes its controls from filterState. This ensures that both direct navigations (no query parameters) and deep links (with metricId/minSize) are represented consistently in the UI.

State updates when user applies filters:
- When the user clicks "Apply filters", the filter form parses its current control values into a selectedMetricId (string or unset) and minDatasetSize (number or unset).
- The client calls updateExamplesGalleryFilters(selectedMetricId, minDatasetSize). The server updates the ExamplesGalleryFilterState and returns the new filterState.
- The client then updates the browser URL's query string so that:
  - metricId matches filterState.selectedMetricId when it is set; otherwise the metricId parameter is removed or empty.
  - minSize matches filterState.minDatasetSize when it is set; otherwise the minSize parameter is removed or empty.
- After the filterState is updated, the client calls getFilteredEvaluationExamples. This call uses the server-maintained ExamplesGalleryFilterState to determine which EvaluationExample records to return.

Consistency rules:
- The examples list must always be rendered from data returned by either getExamplesGalleryPage (initial load) or getFilteredEvaluationExamples (after filters are updated). The UI does not maintain its own filtered subset of examples.
- The filter form's displayed values always derive from the most recent filterState returned from either getExamplesGalleryPage or updateExamplesGalleryFilters.
- URL query parameters are updated only after updateExamplesGalleryFilters succeeds, to avoid inconsistencies between URL and actual server filter state.
- If URL parameters are manually edited by the user and the page is reloaded, getExamplesGalleryPage determines the initial filterState based on those parameters; no additional client-side reconciliation is required beyond rendering the new state.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading for the Examples Gallery page, initializes state from getExamplesGalleryPage on load, and orchestrates communication between the filter form and the examples list. It also ensures that the DocPage metadata is available for any layout components (title, description) and that filterState is kept in sync between the server and UI.
event_handlers: onInit: On page load, call getExamplesGalleryPage(). Bind the returned docPage, filterState, availableMetrics, and examples to child components.

onFilterStateUpdated: Receive updated filterState from the filter form after updateExamplesGalleryFilters has completed; update the filterState used by the page shell and pass it down to the filter form and examples list.
onExamplesRefreshed: After calling getFilteredEvaluationExamples(), replace the examples collection passed to the examples list.
onUrlQuerySync: After filterState changes, update the browser URL query parameters (metricId and minSize) to match the new filterState without a full page reload.

components.functionality: Provides controls for selecting a metric and specifying a minimum dataset size, displays the current state of these filters, and, when submitted, updates the server-side ExamplesGalleryFilterState via updateExamplesGalleryFilters and triggers a refresh of the examples list.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize input controls (metric dropdown selection and dataset size input) from the bound ExamplesGalleryFilterState.selectedMetricId and ExamplesGalleryFilterState.minDatasetSize.
onMetricChange: When the user selects a metric in the dropdown, update the filter form’s internal selectedMetricId value; no interface call is made yet.
onMinDatasetSizeChange: When the user edits the dataset size input, update the filter form’s internal textual value; validation is deferred until form submission.

onSubmit: When the user activates the "Apply filters" control:
          1) Validate and parse the metric selection and dataset size 
             (e.g., convert the dataset size input to a number if non-empty).
          2) Call updateExamplesGalleryFilters(selectedMetricId, minDatasetSize).
          3) Receive the updated filterState from the response and emit a filterStateUpdated event to the page shell.
          4) After the page shell has synchronized the URL query parameters, trigger the page shell to call getFilteredEvaluationExamples().

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose a metric to filter examples by. It lists all available metrics received from the server and highlights the currently selected metric based on the filter state.
event_handlers: onInit: Populate the dropdown options from the availableMetrics collection provided by the page shell and set the initial selected option using ExamplesGalleryFilterState.selectedMetricId if present.
onChange: When the selected option changes, notify the parent filter form with the chosen metric id so that it can update its internal selectedMetricId.

components.functionality: Captures the minimum dataset size threshold for filtering examples. It displays the current threshold and allows clearing or updating it.
event_handlers: onInit: Set the initial input value from ExamplesGalleryFilterState.minDatasetSize if defined; otherwise leave it empty.
onChange: As the user types, update the parent filter form’s internal representation of the minimum dataset size input (as a string), without invoking any interfaces.
onBlur: Optionally validate basic numeric format and notify the parent filter form of any validation state changes (the full validation still occurs on form submission).

components.functionality: Displays the current collection of EvaluationExample records as selectable cards or rows. Reflects the current filter state indirectly by rendering whatever examples are provided by getExamplesGalleryPage or getFilteredEvaluationExamples.
event_handlers: onInit: Render the initial examples collection passed from the page shell after getExamplesGalleryPage has completed.
onExamplesUpdated: When the page shell provides a new examples collection (after getFilteredEvaluationExamples), re-render the list with the updated examples.
onCardClick: When a user clicks an example card, read the bound EvaluationExample.id and navigate the browser to example_detail.html?exampleId={id}.

components.functionality: Manages simple list state behaviors such as showing a loading indicator while examples are being refreshed and handling the case when no examples are returned for the current filter state. It does not perform pagination logic unless provided by higher-level routing, but ensures the UI reflects the current state of the examples collection.
event_handlers: onLoadingStart: Triggered by the page shell when getExamplesGalleryPage or getFilteredEvaluationExamples is in progress; displays a loading state and hides the current examples list if necessary.
onLoadingEnd: Triggered when the examples collection is updated; hides the loading state and shows the examples list or empty state.

onEmptyResults: When the examples collection is empty after a fetch, display an empty state message indicating that no examples match the current filters (without suggesting any specific metric or dataset size).

components.functionality: Keeps the filter-related URL query parameters (metricId and minSize) in sync with the current ExamplesGalleryFilterState and ensures that navigation into the page with these parameters results in a corresponding filter state on load.
event_handlers: onInit: On initial page load, read the current URL query parameters (metricId, minSize) for informational purposes; the actual filterState used by the UI comes from getExamplesGalleryPage. No interfaces are called directly from this component.

onFilterStateChanged: When the page shell receives a new filterState from updateExamplesGalleryFilters, update the browser's URL query string so that metricId and minSize reflect filterState.selectedMetricId and filterState.minDatasetSize, respectively, using the History API or a full navigation according to implementation.

onExternalNavigation: When navigation occurs from other pages with metricId and minSize in the URL, this component does not override server behavior but allows the routing to proceed; onInit logic simply reflects whatever filterState getExamplesGalleryPage returns.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the documentation layout pattern: a fixed global header at the top, then a three-column body with a fixed-width left navigation sidebar, a fluid central content column, and a narrower right sidebar for in-page navigation. Inside the central column for the Examples Gallery page, content flows linearly from top to bottom: a small page category label (e.g., 'Examples'), then the large H1 'Examples Gallery', a short subtitle, a one- to two-paragraph introduction, followed by the filter form. Below the filter form, the examples grid occupies most of the central column, with pagination or an empty state shown directly beneath it. Each subsection is visually separated with consistent vertical spacing to maintain a clear reading rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the documentation layout pattern: a fixed global header at the top, then a three-column body with a fixed-width left navigation sidebar, a fluid central content column, and a narrower right sidebar for in-page navigation. Inside the central column for the Examples Gallery page, content flows linearly from top to bottom: a small page category label (e.g., 'Examples'), then the large H1 'Examples Gallery', a short subtitle, a one- to two-paragraph introduction, followed by the filter form. Below the filter form, the examples grid occupies most of the central column, with pagination or an empty state shown directly beneath it. Each subsection is visually separated with consistent vertical spacing to maintain a clear reading rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain the mockup’s approximate horizontal proportions: 20–25% width for the left sidebar, 50–55% for the central content, and 20–25% for the right sidebar on desktop. The global header spans the full width with modest height (~64px) and comfortable padding. Within the central column, textual header elements (label, H1, subtitle, intro) use generous vertical gaps (24–32px between types of blocks, 12–16px between related lines), with a larger 32–40px gap between the intro and the filter form, and between the filter form and the examples grid. The filter controls themselves use a horizontal layout with each control taking roughly 25–40% of the row on wide screens, leaving breathing room between them and to the right edge.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density moderately spacious, matching developer docs. Typography has generous line-height and margins, making the intro and any explanatory text easy to read. The filter form is slightly denser, with controls placed on a single row where possible but still separated by 16–24px gaps. The examples grid is visually light, with clear card separation (24px+ gutters) and enough padding inside each card so individual examples feel scannable rather than cramped. Pagination controls or empty state messaging have extra vertical breathing room to clearly distinguish them from the grid above.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow the Z-pattern of the mockup: the eye starts at the top-left logo in the global header, moves to the centered search bar, then to the top-right CTA. From there it drops down to the left sidebar to find the 'Examples Gallery' section, then moves into the central column where the bold H1 and subtitle anchor attention. After reading the intro, the eye naturally moves down to the horizontally laid-out filter form, then continues down into the examples grid to browse cards. When scanning for structure or specific subsections, the user’s gaze can shift to the right sidebar where an in-page navigation (e.g., links to 'Filtering options', 'Example types', 'Large-scale datasets') is aligned vertically and kept visible as they scroll, reinforcing a secondary left-to-right flow across the three columns.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a modern, three-column documentation layout beneath a fixed global header. At the very top, a slim header bar spans the full viewport width, with the product logo on the left, a prominent but unobtrusive search input centered, and a subtle primary action button on the right, all sitting on a clean light background. Directly below, the main canvas is divided into three vertical regions: a left navigation sidebar occupying about one-fifth of the width, a central content column taking just over half, and a right sidebar using the remaining space. The left sidebar lists docs sections and links in a vertically stacked, moderately dense list, with the current 'Examples Gallery' area highlighted. In the central column, there is generous white space above and around a small colored category label and the large 'Examples Gallery' H1, followed by a concise subtitle and an explanatory paragraph or two. Beneath this text, a full-width filter bar sits as a horizontal strip of controls aligned to the left of the content column. Below the filter bar, the examples appear in a clean grid of cards, each separated by comfortable gutters. At the bottom of this grid, a centered pagination control or an empty state message appears depending on results. Running alongside this content, the right sidebar presents an 'On this page' list or similar in-page navigation, with main section titles in slightly stronger text and indented sub-items beneath, remaining visible as the user scrolls. The overall feel is spacious, structured, and optimized for developers to quickly understand, filter, and explore evaluation examples without visual clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell occupies the entire viewport and establishes the global documentation frame. At the very top, a fixed-position global header spans 100% of the width, approximately 64px tall, with 16–24px horizontal padding. Within this header, the product logo is aligned left, a search field is centered, and a primary CTA button is aligned right, forming the recognizable Z-pattern focal points. Below the header, the remaining vertical space is divided into three columns on desktop: a left sidebar taking about 22% of the width, a central content column taking about 54%, and a right sidebar taking about 24%. All three columns start immediately beneath the header with a consistent top margin (24–32px) from the underside of the header. The left sidebar is fixed-width (e.g., ~260–300px) and vertically scrolls with the page, presenting documentation navigation: section labels (Welcome, Guides, Examples, etc.) in small uppercase or subtle text, with nested link items indented beneath each label. The central content column is fluid, centered in the remaining space, and houses all Examples Gallery-specific content (label, H1, subtitle, intro, filters, examples grid, pagination). The right sidebar sits flush against the right edge of the main content area; its in-page navigation list is set to be sticky within the viewport so that when the user scrolls, the top of this list remains anchored around 80–96px from the very top (just below the header), while the central column scrolls normally. Vertical spacing throughout the shell uses 24–40px gaps between major blocks, ensuring comfortable breathing room and a consistent baseline grid tied to 8px increments.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The global header is fixed to the top of the viewport, always visible. The left and right sidebars scroll with the page content, while the right in-page navigation within the sidebar uses a sticky behavior, remaining visible as the central content scrolls. The shell enforces a three-column responsive layout that collapses to a single column on smaller screens, stacking left nav above central content and optionally moving the right sidebar content below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter form is positioned within the central content column, directly beneath the introductory text for the Examples Gallery. After the page label, H1, subtitle, and one or two paragraphs of description, there is a larger vertical gap of about 32–40px, and then the filter form spans nearly the full width of the central column. It appears as a horizontal band of controls aligned to the left, with clear but subtle grouping. Each filter control (such as the metric dropdown and the minimum dataset size input) is arranged side by side in a single row on desktop. The row itself is approximately 48–56px tall for the controls, with 16–24px of vertical padding above and below the fields to visually separate this 'filter bar' from the content above and the examples grid below. Horizontal spacing between controls is consistent (16–24px gutters), and the right edge of the filter form aligns with the right edge of the central text column, leaving a clean vertical line. The controls within the form have labels positioned directly above each input in smaller, muted text, with about 4–8px of space between label and field. On narrower screens, the filter form gracefully wraps: controls that do not fit on one row stack into two rows, maintaining 12–16px vertical spacing between rows while preserving the clear separation from the examples grid beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter form scrolls with the central content. It remains positioned between the intro text and the examples list, without any fixed or sticky behavior by default. In responsive layouts, it adapts from a horizontal row of controls to a multi-row or vertical stack as the central column width shrinks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metric dropdown sits within the filter form as the leftmost primary control on desktop. It occupies roughly 35–40% of the filter row’s usable width, with a minimum width of around 240–280px so that the metric names can be fully readable. The control is left-aligned with the main text column edge. A small label such as 'Metric' appears directly above the dropdown in a neutral, slightly lighter text style, separated by about 4–6px. The dropdown field itself is about 40–44px tall, with 8–12px of vertical padding inside and 16–20px horizontal padding, matching the mockup’s button/input patterns. To its right, there is a horizontal gap of about 16–24px before the next filter control (e.g., the minimum dataset size input). Vertically, the baseline of its input aligns with that of the neighboring numeric input so that all controls form a clean horizontal line. In terms of visual weight, the dropdown has a subtle border or field outline and uses the brand accent color only for active or focused states, preserving the overall minimalist feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The dropdown is a standard inline form control within the filter form. It does not have independent fixed or sticky behavior; its spatial positioning is determined by the filter form’s layout. On smaller viewports, if the row wraps, the metric dropdown remains the first control, occupying the full available width on its row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The minimum dataset size input is positioned immediately to the right of the metric dropdown within the same horizontal filter row on desktop. It is aligned on the same vertical axis so that the tops of their labels and the top edge of their inputs form neat horizontal lines. The control occupies about 20–30% of the filter row’s width, with a typical width of 160–220px, leaving some flexible whitespace to its right to prevent the row from feeling crowded. A small label such as 'Min dataset size' appears above the numeric field in the same style and size as the metric label, separated by roughly 4–6px. The numeric input field itself is about 40–44px tall, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding, matching the styling of the metric dropdown for consistency. There is about 16–24px of space between this input and any subsequent controls or the right edge of the form. On narrower screens, when the filter form wraps, this numeric input may move below the metric dropdown, spanning close to the full width of the central column with 12–16px vertical spacing from the control above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The numeric input is a standard inline control within the filter form. It moves with the form during scrolling and participates in the filter form’s responsive wrapping behavior, stacking beneath the metric dropdown on smaller widths as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The examples list occupies the main content area beneath the filter form in the central column and is visually the heaviest block on the page after the heading. After the filter form, there is a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px, and then the examples list begins, spanning the full available width of the central column (minus standard inner padding if used). The list is rendered as a responsive grid of example cards: on wide viewports, 2–3 columns per row depending on the central column width, with horizontal gutters of approximately 24px and vertical gutters of 24–32px between rows. Each card is a rectangular block with 16–24px internal padding, a clear title at the top, a brief description, and metadata such as metric type and dataset size arranged in a compact, scannable layout. The leftmost edge of the grid aligns with the main text column, and the rightmost edge aligns with the same vertical line as the filter form above, maintaining a clean vertical rhythm. The top of the first row of cards is visually connected to the filter area via spacing, yet distinct enough to signal a new section of interaction (browsing examples versus changing filters). As the user scrolls, rows of cards move up and off-screen in a predictable, linear fashion, keeping the experience calm and oriented around scanning vertically.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The examples list behaves as a standard scrollable content block within the central column. It expands vertically based on the number of example cards. The grid responds to available width, reducing the number of columns when the central column becomes narrower (e.g., 2 columns on medium screens, 1 column on small screens), but always maintaining consistent gaps between cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination or empty state handler sits directly beneath the examples list within the central content column, acting as the closing element of the main body content. When there are multiple pages of results, a horizontal pagination control appears about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the last row of example cards. The pagination control is center-aligned within the central column, with page numbers, previous/next arrows, and possibly a 'Rows per page' selector arranged in a linear row. There is ample whitespace around the pagination (16–24px above and below) so it is clearly separated from the card grid while still read as part of the same section. When there are no results, instead of cards and pagination, an empty state block appears starting in the same position where the grid would normally begin. This block is centered horizontally in the central column and occupies roughly 50–60% of the column width, with a simple icon or illustration at the top, a bold 'No examples match your filters' heading beneath it, and a short explanatory paragraph followed by a secondary action (e.g., a 'Clear filters' link or button). There is generous padding (24–32px) inside this block and increased vertical spacing (about 48px from the filter form above) to make the empty state feel intentional and easy to understand.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component’s spatial footprint is tightly coupled to the examples list: when there are results, it remains a compact footer-like control at the bottom of the list; when there are no results, it expands to occupy the vertical region where cards would have been. It scrolls normally with the central content and does not employ sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL query sync component is non-visual and does not occupy any physical space in the layout. Conceptually, it is attached to the central content column and specifically to the filter form and examples list. As the user changes filter values (metric dropdown, minimum dataset size input, and any other filter controls), this component updates the URL query parameters behind the scenes, and conversely, when the page loads or the URL changes, it initializes the filter controls and the examples list from those parameters. There are no visible UI elements, margins, or borders associated with this component; it affects behavior only, not spatial arrangement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component has no spatial footprint. It overlays the page logically rather than visually, synchronizing state between the filter form, the examples list, and the URL without altering the document flow or layout.

name: Example Detail
title: Example Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected evaluation example, including task configuration, dataset metadata, REST API endpoint path, and reproducible code snippets, based on the exampleId URL parameter.

core_features: Load and display evaluation example details using the exampleId URL parameter via getEvaluationExampleDetail (title, summary, task type, metric IDs, dataset ID and size, endpoint path, and related code snippets).
Provide a REST API endpoint section that shows the example’s endpointPath and exposes a copy icon that calls copyEvaluationExampleEndpointPath to copy the endpoint string.
Render one or more code snippets (from codeSnippets in getEvaluationExampleDetail) in various languages, each with a copy button that calls copyCodeSnippet.
Show basic dataset information (datasetId and datasetSize) associated with the evaluation example to help users understand the scale of the evaluation.
Offer navigation back to the examples.html page via a simple link or breadcrumb without additional API calls.
user_workflows:
Viewing an evaluation example detail:
1. User navigates to example_detail.html?exampleId={EVALUATION_EXAMPLE_ID} from examples.html or search_results.html.
2. On page load, the example-detail-loader component reads exampleId from the URL.
3. example-detail-loader calls getEvaluationExampleDetail with evaluationExampleId set to exampleId.
4. When the response resolves, the page populates the example-summary-header, dataset-summary-panel, endpoint-section, and code-snippets-section with the returned evaluationExample, exampleDetailPage, and codeSnippets data.
5. If exampleId is missing or getEvaluationExampleDetail fails, the loader shows an error state instead of the detail components.

Copying the REST API endpoint path:
1. After the example details are loaded, the endpoint-section displays the endpointPath from evaluationExample.
2. User clicks the copy icon/button in endpoint-section.
3. The endpoint-section triggers copyEvaluationExampleEndpointPath with evaluationExampleId set to the current exampleId from the URL.
4. On a successful response (success=true), the UI copies endpointPath from the response to the clipboard and optionally shows a transient "copied" status.
5. On failure, the UI shows a non-blocking error message using the message field.

Copying a code snippet:
1. After getEvaluationExampleDetail completes, the code-snippets-section lists all codeSnippets returned for this example.
2. User locates a snippet (e.g., by language label or section label within the component) and clicks its copy button/icon.
3. The corresponding snippet component calls copyCodeSnippet with the snippet’s id as codeSnippetId.
4. On success, the UI copies the returned code string to the clipboard and optionally shows a transient "copied" state for that snippet.
5. On failure, the snippet shows an inline error message using the message field while leaving the snippet content visible.

Navigating back to the examples gallery:
1. The breadcrumb-navigation component renders a "Back to examples" control that links to examples.html.
2. User clicks the control.
3. The browser navigates to examples.html without additional API calls or state transfer; any filtering or selection state there is handled by that page.
interactions: On page load: Read exampleId from the query string of example_detail.html. If present, trigger getEvaluationExampleDetail; if absent, show a missing-parameter error state.
On URL change (for single-page app scenarios): If exampleId changes, re-trigger getEvaluationExampleDetail with the new ID and reset all dependent components to a loading state until data is fetched.

On successful getEvaluationExampleDetail response: Bind evaluationExample data to the example-summary-header, dataset-summary-panel, and endpoint-section components; bind codeSnippets array to the code-snippets-section component.
On failed getEvaluationExampleDetail response: Display an error message component or state, hide or disable detail components, and provide the user with a link back to examples.html.

Click on copy endpoint icon: In endpoint-section, call copyEvaluationExampleEndpointPath(evaluationExampleId=current exampleId). On success, use endpointPath from the response for clipboard copy and set a temporary "copied" flag in component state.

Click on code snippet copy button: In a code-snippets-section child snippet, call copyCodeSnippet(codeSnippetId=current snippet id). On success, use code from the response for clipboard copy and set a temporary "copied" flag for that snippet.
Click on "Back to examples" link or breadcrumb: Navigate to examples.html using a standard hyperlink, clearing the current example_detail.html view.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the exampleId URL query parameter. When example_detail.html loads, the page reads exampleId from the query string (e.g., example_detail.html?exampleId={ID}). If exampleId is defined, getEvaluationExampleDetail is called with evaluationExampleId=exampleId. The response populates in-memory state for evaluationExample, exampleDetailPage, and codeSnippets. All rendered components derive their data from this in-memory state rather than any hardcoded values. If exampleId changes (for example in a client-side router), the page re-invokes getEvaluationExampleDetail with the new ID and replaces the previous state. No data is persisted in localStorage or other persistent client storage; the URL parameter is the single source of truth for which example is displayed. Copy operations use the IDs from this state (evaluationExample.id and CodeSnippet.id values) when calling copyEvaluationExampleEndpointPath and copyCodeSnippet; they do not assume or embed any static endpoint paths or code content.

components.functionality: Reads the exampleId URL parameter, invokes getEvaluationExampleDetail, manages loading and error states, and provides the resulting evaluationExample, exampleDetailPage, and codeSnippets data to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Extract exampleId from the query string and, if present, call getEvaluationExampleDetail({ evaluationExampleId: exampleId }).
onUrlChange: Detect changes to the exampleId query parameter and re-trigger getEvaluationExampleDetail with the new ID, resetting loading and error states.
onLoadSuccess: Store the returned evaluationExample, exampleDetailPage, and codeSnippets in memory and notify dependent components to render.
onLoadError: Capture and expose error information so the UI can render an error message and optionally a link back to examples.html.

components.functionality: Displays a simple navigation control to return from the example detail page to the examples gallery, using only standard navigation (no APIs).
event_handlers: onClickBackToExamples: Navigate the browser to examples.html when the user activates the "Back to examples" breadcrumb or link.

components.functionality: Shows the high-level information about the evaluation example, including title, summary, taskType, metricIds, datasetId, and datasetSize, using data returned from getEvaluationExampleDetail.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When evaluationExample from example-detail-loader updates, re-render the title, summary, taskType, metricIds list, datasetId, and datasetSize.
onMissingData: If evaluationExample is undefined due to an error or missing exampleId, render a non-blocking message and rely on the loader’s error state.

components.functionality: Highlights dataset-related fields for the evaluation example, such as datasetId and datasetSize, to help users understand which dataset and how many samples the example uses.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When evaluationExample changes, update the dataset summary display to show the current datasetId and datasetSize values.

onNoDataset: If evaluationExample.datasetId is not present, display a state indicating that no dataset is associated with this example while still showing any available datasetSize information if provided.

components.functionality: Displays the REST API endpointPath associated with the evaluation example and exposes a copy icon that uses copyEvaluationExampleEndpointPath to obtain the exact endpoint string for clipboard copying.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When evaluationExample updates, display its endpointPath value as read-only text for the user.
onClickCopyEndpoint: When the user clicks the endpoint copy icon/button, call copyEvaluationExampleEndpointPath({ evaluationExampleId: current evaluationExample.id or exampleId from the URL }).

onCopyEndpointSuccess: When copyEvaluationExampleEndpointPath returns success=true, use the returned endpointPath for clipboard copying and set a temporary copied state flag so the UI can indicate success.
onCopyEndpointError: When copyEvaluationExampleEndpointPath returns success=false or errors, display a non-blocking error using the message field and leave the endpointPath visible.

components.functionality: Renders the list of CodeSnippet entries associated with the evaluation example (different languages or usage patterns) and provides per-snippet copy actions through copyCodeSnippet.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When codeSnippets from example-detail-loader update, render a list of snippet items, each bound to its CodeSnippet properties (such as language and code).
onSelectSnippet: When the user interacts with snippet selectors (e.g., tabs or list items), update internal state to reflect the currently focused snippet, without altering the URL.
onClickCopySnippet: For a selected snippet or for each snippet’s copy control, call copyCodeSnippet({ codeSnippetId: snippet.id }).
onCopySnippetSuccess: When copyCodeSnippet returns success=true, use the returned code string for clipboard copying and mark only that snippet’s UI as temporarily "copied".
onCopySnippetError: When copyCodeSnippet returns success=false or errors, display an inline error message near the affected snippet using the message field while keeping the snippet content visible.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the same three-column documentation layout as the mockup: a fixed global header across the top, a left sidebar for primary site navigation, a central column for the Example Detail content, and a right sidebar for the in-page table of contents. Within the central column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: first the breadcrumb-navigation as a lightweight contextual locator, then the example-summary-header as the main H1 block (including category label, title, subtitle, and introductory copy), followed by a sequence of sections stacked vertically: dataset-summary-panel, endpoint-section, and finally code-snippets-section. The example-detail-loader overlays or replaces this central column content while data is loading, without affecting the structural positions of the other components.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the same three-column documentation layout as the mockup: a fixed global header across the top, a left sidebar for primary site navigation, a central column for the Example Detail content, and a right sidebar for the in-page table of contents. Within the central column, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: first the breadcrumb-navigation as a lightweight contextual locator, then the example-summary-header as the main H1 block (including category label, title, subtitle, and introductory copy), followed by a sequence of sections stacked vertically: dataset-summary-panel, endpoint-section, and finally code-snippets-section. The example-detail-loader overlays or replaces this central column content while data is loading, without affecting the structural positions of the other components.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The width distribution mirrors the mockup: roughly 22% of the viewport width for the left navigation sidebar, about 54% for the central content column, and about 24% for the right in-page TOC sidebar on a typical desktop viewport. All page-specific components listed here live exclusively in the central column and span nearly 100% of that column’s width, with 16–24px internal padding where they appear as cards or panels. Vertical spacing follows the base 8px system: 16–24px between tightly related items (e.g., breadcrumb and header), 24–32px between adjacent sections (e.g., header to dataset-summary-panel), and 40–48px between major conceptual blocks (e.g., data sections versus code section) to keep the hierarchy clear.
layout_strategies.content_density: The central column maintains a moderately spacious, reading-optimized density. Text within example-summary-header uses generous line-height and 24–32px spacing between the title, subtitle, and intro paragraph. The dataset-summary-panel, endpoint-section, and code-snippets-section use a slightly denser layout than the hero header but still leave ample breathing room: 16–24px padding inside each panel and 12–16px between related internal items. Navigation elements like breadcrumb text and any in-section labels are compact but not cramped, consistent with a DevTools documentation feel. Code blocks and endpoint paths are given enough vertical padding to feel substantial but not so much that they dominate the viewport.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the mockup’s Z-pattern at the global level (logo to search to header CTA), then drops into a left-to-right emphasis on the three-column layout. Once on this page, the eye first lands at the top-left of the central column on the breadcrumb-navigation, then moves down to the dominant example-summary-header. From there, the user naturally scrolls downward through dataset-summary-panel, endpoint-section, and finally code-snippets-section. In parallel, once the initial context is understood, the right sidebar’s TOC draws attention as a secondary guide, mapping to the central sections (Dataset, Endpoint, Code) to enable quick scanning and jumping. The loader, when visible, momentarily becomes the primary focal element by dimming or masking the central column, then disappears to reveal the main linear flow.

layout.overall_description: The Example Detail page sits within a standardized documentation shell: a fixed-height global header stretches across the top with a logo on the far left, a centered search input, and a subtle call-to-action button on the right, all on a light background with generous horizontal padding. Below, the viewport is divided into three vertical regions. On the left, a fixed-width sidebar (about one-fifth of the page) houses the documentation navigation tree in a vertical stack. On the right, a slimmer sidebar lists an in-page “On this page” table of contents with indented entries. Between them, the central content column sits slightly wider than either sidebar and acts as the main reading area. At the top of this central column, aligned with the left margin, a breadcrumb trail subtly anchors the user’s location. Directly beneath, the Example Detail header block occupies a prominent area, with a small colored category label above a large, clean H1-like title, a muted subtitle, and a short introductory paragraph, all with comfortable spacing.
