Scrolling down, the dataset summary appears as a full-width card-like panel, followed by the REST API endpoint section and then a larger code snippets section stacked vertically, each separated by consistent vertical gaps. The right TOC column mirrors these main sections with links, guiding the reader’s eye horizontally between the central content and right-hand navigation. During data loading, a minimal loader overlays or replaces the central content column, keeping the overall three-column structure intact while signaling progress in a restrained, modern way.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader is confined to the central content column, visually centered both horizontally and vertically within the portion of the column currently in view. It does not occupy the global header or the left/right sidebars; instead, it sits on top of the central stack where the breadcrumb and detail sections will render. When active, it either dimly overlays the central column with a translucent layer or displays a centered skeleton state that roughly matches the shape of the example-summary-header and first section below it. The loader’s content (spinner or skeleton rows) occupies roughly the middle third of the central column’s height on first paint, with at least 40–64px empty space above and below to maintain the same airy feel as the mockup. The side margins line up exactly with the central column’s text column, ensuring the loader feels like part of the main content rather than a full-page interrupt.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loader is positioned relatively to the central content wrapper but appears as an overlay above all central components using a semi-transparent backdrop or skeleton placeholders. It is not fixed to the viewport; instead, it scrolls with the main column if the page is taller than the viewport. When loading completes, it collapses and is removed from the document flow, revealing the breadcrumb-navigation at the top of the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-navigation sits at the very top of the central content column, immediately below the global header and aligned flush with the left edge of the text column. It spans the full usable width of the central column but uses a compact inline breadcrumb trail aligned to the left, leaving generous empty space to the right so it doesn’t compete with the main title. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom edge of the global header and the breadcrumb, echoing the mockup’s comfortable buffer before the first content element. Below the breadcrumb, a 16–24px vertical space separates it from the example-summary-header, clearly distinguishing the compact navigational meta from the more visually dominant page title block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The breadcrumb is a static element within the normal flow of the central column. It does not stick to the top on scroll; once the user scrolls past it, it moves out of view as the main sections scroll upward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The example-summary-header occupies the topmost major block of the central content column, directly beneath the breadcrumb-navigation, and spans nearly the full width of this column with standard inner padding matching other large headers on the site. At the top of this block, a small, colored category label (e.g., “Examples” or the relevant section) appears in the top-left, sitting about 4–8px above the main title. Below it, a large H1-style title stretches up to roughly 70–80% of the central column’s width, left-aligned, with ample line-height and white space above and below. A short subtitle in a slightly smaller, muted font appears directly under the title with around 8–12px spacing, followed by a multi-line introductory paragraph with 16–24px spacing below the subtitle. Any meta information (such as example ID, last updated, or tags) can sit in a horizontal row below the paragraph or tucked to its right, but within the same block, maintaining a 12–16px gap from the intro text. Vertically, this header block takes up roughly the top quarter to third of the initial viewport in a typical desktop view, creating a strong but uncluttered focal area before the user reaches the detailed panels below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This header is a standard, non-sticky block in the central column’s normal flow. It anchors the scroll position visually but does not remain pinned; as the user scrolls down, it moves upward and off-screen, while the right-hand TOC can highlight its corresponding section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dataset-summary-panel sits directly below the example-summary-header, separated from it by a generous vertical gap of about 24–32px, signaling a new major section. It spans the full width of the central content column, but its content is wrapped inside a subtle card-like container with 16–24px internal padding on all sides, a faint border or background tint, and slightly rounded corners to match the rest of the documentation UI. A section title such as “Dataset” or “Dataset Summary” appears at the top-left of the panel, aligned with the header’s text alignment, and may be mirrored as a top-level heading in the right-hand TOC. Beneath this title, dataset metadata (e.g., dataset name, size, task type, source) is arranged in either a two-column grid or a vertical list, with 8–16px spacing between rows, keeping the information scannable but not dense. The panel’s bottom edge is followed by a 24–32px vertical gap before the endpoint-section begins, maintaining the same rhythm of section separation seen in the mockup’s content blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The dataset panel is a static block in the vertical flow. It scrolls naturally with the central column and lines up with a corresponding entry in the right-hand TOC, which may highlight when this panel is in view. No sticky behavior; it simply occupies a solid section of the main reading stream.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The endpoint-section is positioned immediately below the dataset-summary-panel, with a vertical gap of around 24–32px to clearly mark it as the next main section. Like the dataset panel, it spans the full width of the central column’s content area, using a similar or slightly lighter card treatment to visually connect it in the hierarchy. At the top, a clear section heading such as “REST API Endpoint” or “Endpoint” is left-aligned and sized similarly to other section headings, making it a strong anchor for the corresponding TOC item. Below the heading, there is a brief descriptive sentence explaining the endpoint’s purpose, followed by the endpoint path itself in a prominent monospace code-styled block or pill (e.g., `POST /v1/examples/{exampleId}/run`). This code-styled element occupies roughly 60–80% of the column width and is padded with 8–12px inside, with 12–16px spacing above and below to keep it legible. Any supplementary details such as HTTP method, required headers, or path parameters can be listed below in a lightly structured format with 8–12px vertical spacing. A 24–32px gap below the last row of endpoint details separates this section from the subsequent code-snippets-section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The endpoint-section follows the normal vertical scroll behavior. It acts as a key anchor for mid-page navigation and will likely correspond to a primary entry in the right sidebar TOC, but it itself is not sticky or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The code-snippets-section appears as the last major content block of the central column, directly beneath the endpoint-section with a larger vertical gap of about 32–40px to emphasize it as a substantial, action-oriented section. It spans the full available width of the central column and visually reads as a large, structured panel. At the top-left, a section heading such as “Code snippets” or “Run this example” aligns with the other section titles and is treated as a primary heading level in the right-hand TOC. Immediately under the heading, there may be a brief one- or two-line explanation about how to use the snippets, followed by a horizontal language selector (e.g., tabs for Python, JavaScript, cURL) aligned left and occupying roughly the first 50–60% of the column. Below the tabs, the active code block occupies the majority of the section’s vertical space. This code block stretches close to the full column width with 16–24px padding around the code content, a subtle contrasting background, and mono-space font, mirroring typical modern documentation code sections. Vertical spacing inside the section is orderly: 8–12px between heading and description, 12–16px between description and tabs, and 16–24px between tabs and the code block. The bottom of this section ends the main content stream, with a generous 40–48px of whitespace below if there is no further content, ensuring the page doesn’t feel cramped at the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The code snippets section is a static end-of-page block in this view. It scrolls naturally and may be the largest single section by height. It aligns with a primary entry in the right-hand TOC, and as the user scrolls through the often tall code block, the TOC remains visible in the right sidebar to aid navigation back to earlier sections.

name: Playground
title: Playground
description: Interactive playground page where developers can configure and run sample evaluations in the browser by selecting a task type, entering a prompt, choosing metrics and weights, setting sample size and evaluation name, selecting a model, and executing an evaluation run.

core_features: Load Playground configuration using getPlaygroundPageConfig to retrieve the DocPage metadata, available task types, available metrics, and default model IDs.
Preselect the Playground task type based on the optional taskType URL parameter when present and valid; otherwise fall back to a default from availableTaskTypes.
Allow users to select or change the evaluation task type (for example text classification or text generation) from the availableTaskTypes list.
Provide a prompt input area where users can enter the instruction or example text that will be evaluated.
Display available metrics from availableMetrics, allow users to enable or disable individual metrics, and maintain a selected metricIds list for the evaluation run.
Expose dedicated numeric inputs for accuracyWeight and latencyWeight that are only enabled when the corresponding metrics are selected, while allowing other metrics to be enabled without weights.
Allow users to configure sampleSize and evaluationName fields required for the Playground evaluation run.
Provide a model selector populated from defaultModelIds so users can choose which modelId to use in the run.
Validate the configured evaluation (required fields, numeric constraints, presence of at least one metric) before allowing the user to run it.
Trigger createPlaygroundEvaluationRun with the current configuration (taskType, prompt, accuracyWeight, latencyWeight, metricIds, sampleSize, evaluationName, modelId) when the user submits the form.
Display the returned PlaygroundEvaluationRun object including id, status, and key configuration fields so users can see the outcome of their Playground run.
user_workflows: Initial load and configuration retrieval: (1) User navigates to playground.html, optionally with a taskType URL parameter. (2) The page-init component calls getPlaygroundPageConfig with no arguments. (3) The response docPage is used for page metadata and the availableTaskTypes, availableMetrics, and defaultModelIds are stored in page state. (4) The initial task type is resolved: if the URL taskType value is found in availableTaskTypes it is used; otherwise the first availableTaskTypes entry is selected. (5) The task-type selector, metrics configuration panel, prompt input, model selector, and run configuration fields are initialized based on this state.

Selecting or changing task type: (1) User opens the task-type selector dropdown. (2) User chooses a task type from options derived from availableTaskTypes. (3) The selection updates the current pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.taskType in state. (4) The URL is updated using history.replaceState to set or update the taskType query parameter to the selected value. (5) Optional: task-type dependent hints or placeholder texts for the prompt field are refreshed using the new task type (without hardcoded values).

Configuring prompt and basic run settings: (1) User enters or edits text in the prompt input; the value is bound to the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.prompt field. (2) User enters a value into the sampleSize input; the numeric value is bound to PlaygroundEvaluationRun.sampleSize. (3) User enters a string into the evaluationName input; the value is bound to PlaygroundEvaluationRun.evaluationName. (4) Validation runs on change to ensure fields required by createPlaygroundEvaluationRun are not empty and that sampleSize is a valid number within acceptable constraints defined by the implementation.

Selecting metrics and weights: (1) The metrics configuration panel renders one row per Metric returned in availableMetrics. (2) For each metric, the user can toggle a checkbox to include or exclude it from the evaluation; selected metric IDs are added to or removed from the pending metricIds array in state. (3) If a Metric.id matches the identifier used for accuracy, a numeric input bound to accuracyWeight is displayed and enabled when that metric is selected and disabled (and cleared) when it is not. (4) Similarly, if a Metric.id matches the identifier used for latency, a numeric input bound to latencyWeight is displayed and enabled when that metric is selected and disabled (and cleared) when it is not. (5) For all other metrics, no weight input is shown; they only affect the metricIds list. (6) On any change, the form-level validation checks that any non-empty accuracyWeight or latencyWeight values are valid numbers and can apply additional rules defined by the implementation.

Choosing a model: (1) Model selector is populated from the defaultModelIds array returned by getPlaygroundPageConfig. (2) Initial selection is the first entry from defaultModelIds unless the implementation chooses a different default. (3) User may open the selector and choose a different model; the selected value is bound to the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.modelId field. (4) If defaultModelIds is empty, the selector displays an appropriate empty state and validation requires the user to provide a valid modelId in any alternative way defined by the implementation.

Running an evaluation: (1) When the user clicks the Run evaluation button within the run configuration form, the form is validated: required fields (taskType, prompt, sampleSize, evaluationName), at least one selected metric in metricIds, and any additional constraints are checked. (2) If validation fails, the form prevents submission and surfaces errors near the relevant fields (implementation-defined); createPlaygroundEvaluationRun is not called. (3) If validation passes, the button transitions into a loading or disabled state and createPlaygroundEvaluationRun is called with the current taskType, prompt, accuracyWeight, latencyWeight, metricIds, sampleSize, evaluationName, and modelId from state. (4) On success, the returned playgroundEvaluationRun object is stored in page state and passed to the run-status component to be displayed. (5) The Run evaluation button can be re-enabled so the user can run another evaluation with modified inputs if desired.

Inspecting the latest run result: (1) After createPlaygroundEvaluationRun returns, the run-status component receives the playgroundEvaluationRun object. (2) The component displays fields such as id, taskType, evaluationName, modelId, sampleSize, metricIds, and status. (3) If status indicates that the run is still in progress or has completed or failed, the component shows this clearly based on the status values provided. (4) If the user modifies the configuration and runs another evaluation, the run-status component updates to show the latest returned playgroundEvaluationRun.

Task type driven URL sharing: (1) As the user changes the task type, the taskType URL parameter is kept in sync via the URL-state-sync component using history.replaceState. (2) If the user shares the URL with this taskType parameter to another user or opens it in a new tab, on load the page-init component will again use that taskType parameter to preselect the task type, giving a shareable starting configuration.
interactions: Click on header navigation link labeled for the Playground navigates to playground.html, optionally carrying a taskType query parameter when linked from index.html or examples.html.
On page load, the page-init component automatically invokes getPlaygroundPageConfig; there is no user action required for this interaction.
Selecting a task type from the task-type selector dropdown updates internal state and triggers the URL-state-sync component to update the taskType query parameter.
Typing into the prompt textarea updates the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.prompt field in state on each change or blur event.
Modifying the sample size input updates the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.sampleSize field in state and triggers validation on change or blur.
Editing the evaluation name input updates the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.evaluationName field in state.
Toggling a metric checkbox inside the metrics configuration panel adds or removes that metric’s id from the pending metricIds array bound to PlaygroundEvaluationRun.metricIds.
Typing into the accuracy weight input updates the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.accuracyWeight field in state; the input becomes disabled and cleared when the accuracy metric is not selected.
Typing into the latency weight input updates the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.latencyWeight field in state; the input becomes disabled and cleared when the latency metric is not selected.
Opening and changing the model in the model selector updates the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.modelId field in state.
Clicking the Run evaluation button triggers the submission handler of the run configuration form: it performs validation and then calls createPlaygroundEvaluationRun if valid.
Receiving a successful response from createPlaygroundEvaluationRun updates the run-status component with the returned playgroundEvaluationRun, which is then rendered to the user.

If createPlaygroundEvaluationRun fails (for example due to validation or network issues), the form submission handler displays an error state in the UI and re-enables the Run evaluation button so the user can try again.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of external state is the taskType URL query parameter on playground.html. On initial load, the page-init component reads window.location.search to extract the taskType parameter, if present. After getPlaygroundPageConfig returns, the component checks if the extracted taskType string is present in the availableTaskTypes array. If it is, that value becomes the initial task type used in the page’s internal state for the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.taskType. If the URL parameter is absent or does not match any availableTaskTypes value, the initial task type falls back to an implementation-defined default, such as the first element of availableTaskTypes. The URL-state-sync component listens for changes to the selected task type in internal state. Whenever the user changes the task type, the component updates the browser URL using history.replaceState (or a similar mechanism) so that the taskType query parameter always reflects the currently selected task type without reloading the page. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the URL preserves the selected task type. No other page state is persisted to the URL; other configuration such as prompt, metrics, weights, sample size, evaluation name, and model selection are held in memory only and reset on a full page reload. LocalStorage or other client-side storage mechanisms are not used; all persistence beyond the current session is via the URL parameter for task type and the backend’s persistence of PlaygroundEvaluationRun instances created by createPlaygroundEvaluationRun.

components.functionality: On initial render, this component initializes the Playground by reading the taskType URL parameter, invoking getPlaygroundPageConfig, and seeding the internal page state (including default task type, metrics list, and model options) used by all other components.
event_handlers: onMount: Read the taskType query parameter from window.location.search and store it as a pending initialTaskType value.
onMount: Call getPlaygroundPageConfig with no arguments and wait for its response.
onConfigLoaded: Store the returned docPage metadata for potential use by the documentation shell; store availableTaskTypes, availableMetrics (as Metric entities), and defaultModelIds in page state.

onConfigLoaded: Resolve the effective initial task type by checking if the URL-derived initialTaskType is present in availableTaskTypes; if not, select a default from availableTaskTypes and set this as the current PlaygroundEvaluationRun.taskType value in state.
onConfigError: If getPlaygroundPageConfig fails, update page state to reflect an error state that disables dependent components until resolved.

components.functionality: Provides a dropdown allowing users to select the evaluation task type from the availableTaskTypes list, updating the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.taskType and synchronizing it with the URL taskType parameter.
event_handlers: onChange: When the user selects a new task type option, update the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.taskType field in state to this value.
onChange: Notify the playground-url-state-sync component so it can update the taskType URL parameter via history.replaceState.
onInit: Use the initially resolved task type (from the page-init component) to set the initial selected value of the dropdown.

components.functionality: Renders a text input or textarea where users can enter the prompt or instruction that will be evaluated, bound to the PlaygroundEvaluationRun.prompt field.
event_handlers: onChange: Update the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.prompt field in state whenever the user edits the text.
onBlur: Trigger field-level validation ensuring the prompt is non-empty before allowing submission.

components.functionality: Displays all available metrics derived from the availableMetrics list and allows users to select which metrics to include and to specify weights for accuracy and latency where applicable, maintaining the metricIds, accuracyWeight, and latencyWeight fields on the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.
event_handlers: onInit: Render one metric entry per Metric entity from availableMetrics, each with a checkbox bound to the presence of that Metric.id in PlaygroundEvaluationRun.metricIds.
onMetricToggle: When a metric checkbox is checked, add that Metric.id to the PlaygroundEvaluationRun.metricIds array; when unchecked, remove it.
onMetricToggle: If the toggled metric matches the identifier used for accuracy, enable or disable the accuracy weight input accordingly and clear accuracyWeight when disabled.
onMetricToggle: If the toggled metric matches the identifier used for latency, enable or disable the latency weight input accordingly and clear latencyWeight when disabled.

onAccuracyWeightChange: When the user edits the accuracy weight input, update PlaygroundEvaluationRun.accuracyWeight with the parsed numeric value (or an empty value if cleared) and trigger validation.
onLatencyWeightChange: When the user edits the latency weight input, update PlaygroundEvaluationRun.latencyWeight with the parsed numeric value (or an empty value if cleared) and trigger validation.
onValidationRequest: Provide feedback to the run-config-form component about whether metric selection and associated weights are valid (for example, numeric and within acceptable ranges).

components.functionality: Provides a selector populated from defaultModelIds allowing users to choose which modelId will be used for the Playground evaluation run.
event_handlers: onInit: Populate the selector options from the defaultModelIds array provided by getPlaygroundPageConfig; select a default option in accordance with the implementation’s rules (for example, the first entry when available).
onChange: When the user selects a model option, update the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.modelId field in state.
onBlur: Validate that a modelId is selected when the evaluation run requires it.

components.functionality: Aggregates task type, prompt, metrics configuration, sample size, evaluation name, and model selection into a single form. Handles validation and, on submission, calls createPlaygroundEvaluationRun with the current configuration and propagates the returned PlaygroundEvaluationRun to the status panel.
event_handlers: onSampleSizeChange: Update the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.sampleSize field in state as the user edits the sample size input, parsing the value as a number.
onEvaluationNameChange: Update the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun.evaluationName field in state as the user edits the evaluation name input.

onValidate: When any bound input changes or when the form is about to submit, run cross-field validation to ensure required fields (taskType, prompt, sampleSize, evaluationName, at least one metric in metricIds) are present and that numeric fields like sampleSize, accuracyWeight, and latencyWeight are valid.

onSubmit: If validation passes, disable the Run evaluation button and call createPlaygroundEvaluationRun, passing the current values of taskType, prompt, accuracyWeight, latencyWeight, metricIds, sampleSize, evaluationName, and modelId from the pending PlaygroundEvaluationRun state.

onSubmitSuccess: When createPlaygroundEvaluationRun returns successfully, update an in-memory representation of the latest PlaygroundEvaluationRun with the returned object and notify the evaluation-run-status-panel component so it can render the new run; re-enable the Run evaluation button.

onSubmitError: If createPlaygroundEvaluationRun returns an error or the network request fails, display an error message associated with the form, keep or restore the current field values for user correction, and re-enable the Run evaluation button.

components.functionality: Displays the most recently created PlaygroundEvaluationRun returned by createPlaygroundEvaluationRun, including its id, taskType, evaluationName, modelId, sampleSize, metricIds, status, and createdAt so users can understand the outcome of their Playground run.
event_handlers: onRunUpdated: When the run-config-form component provides a new playgroundEvaluationRun object after a successful submission, replace any previously displayed run data with this new object.
onRender: Based on PlaygroundEvaluationRun.status, render status-specific text or indicators (for example, treating distinct values like running, completed, and failed distinctly in the UI).

components.functionality: Keeps the taskType URL query parameter synchronized with the currently selected task type in state so the Playground configuration is shareable and reproducible via URL.
event_handlers: onTaskTypeChange: Listen for changes to the current PlaygroundEvaluationRun.taskType value and, when it changes, update the browser URL using history.replaceState so that the taskType query parameter matches the new value without causing a page reload.

onUrlChangeExternal: Optionally listen for browser navigation events (such as back/forward) and, when the URL’s taskType parameter changes externally, update the in-memory current task type to match, reusing the same validation used on initial load to ensure the new value exists in availableTaskTypes before applying it.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the documentation layout from the mockup: a fixed global header across the top, and beneath it a three-column grid with a fixed-width left sidebar for product documentation navigation, a fluid central column for the Playground content, and a narrower right sidebar for in-page navigation (On this page). Within the central column, content flows top-to-bottom: a small category label (for example, Guides), the H1 title Playground, a short subtitle and one-paragraph introduction explaining the interactive evaluation experience, followed by a single, visually grouped Playground configuration area. Inside this configuration area, the controls are ordered from conceptual high-level to low-level: task-type-selector and model-selector at the top, then prompt-input, then metrics-config-panel, then run-config-form, and finally the evaluation-run-status-panel as the output area. All components respect the linear vertical flow, with any horizontal pairing (such as task-type and model selectors) treated as a sub-grid inside the central column card, collapsing to a single column on smaller viewports.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the documentation layout from the mockup: a fixed global header across the top, and beneath it a three-column grid with a fixed-width left sidebar for product documentation navigation, a fluid central column for the Playground content, and a narrower right sidebar for in-page navigation (On this page). Within the central column, content flows top-to-bottom: a small category label (for example, Guides), the H1 title Playground, a short subtitle and one-paragraph introduction explaining the interactive evaluation experience, followed by a single, visually grouped Playground configuration area. Inside this configuration area, the controls are ordered from conceptual high-level to low-level: task-type-selector and model-selector at the top, then prompt-input, then metrics-config-panel, then run-config-form, and finally the evaluation-run-status-panel as the output area. All components respect the linear vertical flow, with any horizontal pairing (such as task-type and model selectors) treated as a sub-grid inside the central column card, collapsing to a single column on smaller viewports.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The viewport width is divided approximately into 22% for the left sidebar, 56% for the central content, and 22% for the right sidebar on desktop. The global header spans 100% width with modest vertical padding (around 16–24px). Within the central column, the main Playground configuration area occupies roughly 90–100% of the central column width, centered with generous side gutters that align with the typographic grid. Inside this area, horizontally paired controls (task-type-selector and model-selector) each take about 48% of the central column width with a small 4% gap between them, dropping to full-width stacks on tablet and mobile. Vertical spacing between major components (title block, configuration group, run status) sits in the 24–40px range, with internal padding of 16–24px inside card-like panels such as the metrics-config-panel and the evaluation-run-status-panel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderately spacious, prioritizing scanability and readability. Text elements in the central column use generous line-height and comfortable paragraph spacing, similar to modern developer documentation. Form controls and selectors have enough padding (around 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal) so they feel touch-friendly and not cramped, but are still tight enough to keep the full configuration visible above the fold on a typical laptop screen. Panels like metrics-config-panel and evaluation-run-status-panel use internal section spacing in the 16–24px range, with clear separation between groups of fields or result blocks. Navigation lists in the sidebars are more compact but still use 8–12px vertical spacing between items to support quick scanning without visual noise.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the global header in a Z-pattern: from the logo at top-left, across to a centered search field, then to any primary call-to-action at the top-right. From there, attention drops diagonally to the active section in the left sidebar (Playground highlighted under the appropriate section), then into the central H1 Playground and its brief explanation. Below the intro, the eye moves into the configuration controls, first across the top row where task-type-selector and model-selector are aligned side by side, then down the column through prompt-input, metrics-config-panel, and run-config-form. The primary Run evaluation action in the run-config-form becomes the short-term focal point. After a run is triggered, attention flows downward into the evaluation-run-status-panel, which visually reads as the full-width output area akin to the screenshot/media block in the original mockup. The right-hand On this page sidebar subtly supports a secondary left-to-right scanning flow by listing anchors for sections like Configuration, Metrics, and Run status, but remains visually lighter than the central content.

layout.overall_description: Below a slim, fixed global header that stretches across the top with the logo on the left, a centered search input, and a subtle primary action on the right, the page is divided into three vertical regions. On the left, a fixed-width navigation sidebar occupies roughly the left fifth of the viewport, listing top-level tabs like Documentation and API Reference, followed by grouped links where the current section for Playground is highlighted. On the right, a narrower sidebar of similar width lists On this page anchors with slightly indented sub-items, tracking the main sections of the Playground document. The central column, taking up a little more than half of the width, starts with a small colored category label, the large H1 Playground, a short one-line subtitle, and a concise introductory paragraph, all with generous breathing room above and between them. Directly beneath this text, a single, card-like Playground configuration area spans most of the central column width, with a soft border or subtle background and 16–24px padding. At the top of this card, task-type-selector and model-selector are arranged side by side as two equal-width blocks on desktop, aligning their top edges and leaving a slim gap between them, but stacking into a single column on narrower screens. Beneath them, the prompt-input stretches full width, followed by the metrics-config-panel as a more structured, possibly multi-column block, and then the run-config-form, which culminates in a right-aligned Run evaluation button that visually anchors the configuration section. Below this, the evaluation-run-status-panel fills the width of the card as a taller, scrollable-looking panel showing logs, scores, or sample outputs, occupying the same visual role as the large screenshot in the mockup. The entire center column maintains moderate vertical spacing and consistent alignment, so the experience reads as a clean, modern, developer-focused playground embedded within a familiar documentation shell of navigation and in-page TOC.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component defines the Playground page within the existing documentation shell. It sits in the central content column directly beneath the global header, aligning its left edge with the main text alignment of other documentation pages. At the top of the central column, playground-page-init renders a small uppercase category label (for example, Guides) in the brand accent color, followed by the H1 title Playground, a short subtitle line, and a brief introductory paragraph describing the interactive evaluation playground. Vertical spacing between these elements is generous (around 16–24px between label, title, subtitle, and paragraph, and 32–40px between the intro paragraph and the first interactive control). Below this introductory block, playground-page-init establishes a full-width, card-like container that visually groups the rest of the Playground components within the central column: task-type-selector, model-selector, prompt-input, metrics-config-panel, run-config-form, and evaluation-run-status-panel. This container respects the central column width (about 56% of the viewport on desktop), with 16–24px internal padding and subtle separation from the surrounding page via a light border or background tone, mirroring the media block treatment seen in the mockup. The left and right documentation sidebars remain outside and on either side of this central arrangement, unchanged, so the overall page still feels like part of the broader docs system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the central column, participating in normal document flow under the fixed global header and between the left and right sidebars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the card-like Playground configuration area established by playground-page-init, the task-type-selector is positioned at the top-left of the first configuration row, directly below the intro text with a top margin of about 32–40px. On desktop and larger tablets, it occupies roughly the left 48% of the central column’s inner width, forming a two-column sub-grid with the model-selector to its right and leaving a small 4% horizontal gap between them. The component itself is a horizontally oriented control (such as pills or a dropdown block) with a label above it aligned to the left, using 8–12px vertical spacing between label and control. It has comfortable horizontal padding (16–24px) and moderate height so that individual task options are easily clickable. On smaller viewports where the main column narrows, the task-type-selector shifts to occupy 100% of the configuration card width, stacking above the model-selector with 16–24px vertical spacing, while still maintaining alignment with the central column margins and the other input fields below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the configuration card; participates in a responsive two-column sub-grid on wide screens and collapses into a single-column layout on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prompt-input component is positioned directly below the first configuration row that contains task-type-selector and model-selector, with a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px to clearly separate it as the next step in the workflow. It spans the full inner width of the configuration card within the central column, aligning its left and right edges with other form elements for a clean vertical rhythm. A left-aligned label and optional helper text sit above the input box with about 8–12px of vertical spacing, and the input area itself is a multi-line text field tall enough (roughly 200–280px) to suggest comfortable prompt authoring without feeling cramped. The entire prompt-input section uses 16–24px internal padding consistent with other interactive elements and is vertically generous so that, even when filled with text, it maintains the spacious documentation feel. On narrower screens, it remains a full-width block directly under the stacked task-type and model selectors, preserving the linear top-to-bottom reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the central column’s configuration card, remaining in normal vertical flow beneath the top selector row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metrics-config-panel sits immediately below the prompt-input, separated by around 24–32px of vertical space that signals a new conceptual section. It occupies the full inner width of the central configuration card and is visually framed as a structured sub-panel, potentially with a slightly stronger border or section heading to indicate that this area is dedicated to selecting metrics and weights. Inside this panel, metrics may be arranged in a denser sub-grid (for example, two columns of metric toggles or weight sliders), but the outer panel respects the same padding system as the rest of the card: 16–24px on all sides, with 12–16px vertical spacing between rows of metric controls. The label or heading for the metrics section is left-aligned to match other labels and sits at the top of the panel. This panel’s height grows with the number of metrics but it always spans roughly 100% of the configuration card width, keeping a clear, stacked alignment with prompt-input above and run-config-form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width sub-panel in the main configuration card, expanding vertically as needed while maintaining fixed alignment and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Model-selector shares the top configuration row with task-type-selector inside the central configuration card. On desktop, it is positioned to the right of task-type-selector, aligned along the same top baseline, and occupies roughly the right 48% of the card’s inner width, leaving a small gap between the two controls. It presents a labeled dropdown or selection control that allows choosing an underlying model, with the label left-aligned above the control and 8–12px of vertical spacing between them. The control’s height and padding mirror the task-type-selector so the two form a visually balanced pair. On narrower viewports (tablet and mobile), the model-selector stacks directly under the task-type-selector, both at full width, with a 16–24px vertical margin to preserve clarity and maintain the page’s vertical reading flow. The component’s right edge visually aligns with other full-width elements like prompt-input and metrics-config-panel below, preserving a consistent central column gutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; participates in the same responsive two-column-to-single-column behavior as task-type-selector within the configuration card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The run-config-form appears directly below the metrics-config-panel with a vertical margin of about 24–32px, acting as the final configuration step before launching an evaluation. It spans the full width of the central configuration card and contains a small set of fields such as evaluation name and sample size. These fields may be arranged in a compact horizontal row on larger screens (for example, name taking about 60% and sample size about 20–30%, with the remainder reserved for the action button) but always remain visually grouped and aligned to the left, following the central column’s typographic grid. The primary Run evaluation button is placed either at the right end of this row or right-aligned beneath the fields, creating a strong visual anchor at the bottom-right of the form area, consistent with the Z-pattern and making it the natural place for the eye to land. Internal spacing between fields and the button follows the 8–16px scale, keeping this section slightly denser than the large prompt input but still comfortably spaced. On smaller viewports, the fields and the button stack vertically with 12–16px gaps, preserving clarity while fitting within the narrower central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the configuration card; fields may form a responsive internal row that collapses to a vertical stack on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The evaluation-run-status-panel is located directly under the run-config-form, with a slightly larger vertical margin (around 32–40px) to emphasize the transition from configuration to results. It spans almost the entire width of the central column’s configuration area, visually mirroring the large media block (screenshot) from the original mockup. The panel appears as a distinct card or section with a light border or subtle background tint, 16–24px internal padding, and possibly a simple heading such as Latest run at the top left. Its height grows based on the amount of output (for example, logs, metric tables, or sample-level results), but it maintains generous internal spacing between rows or blocks of information (12–16px), ensuring that even complex output feels readable rather than cramped. When the results grow taller than the viewport, the user scrolls the entire central column, and this panel moves in concert with the rest of the content, remaining anchored at the bottom of the main Playground card and aligned with the central column gutters. On smaller screens, it remains full-width and directly follows the run-config-form, preserving the straightforward top-down narrative of configure then inspect results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, occupying a full-width block beneath the configuration components and scrolling with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes at the page level and does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it is attached to the entire Playground configuration area, listening to changes in task-type-selector, prompt-input, metrics-config-panel, model-selector, and run-config-form, and synchronizing this state with the page URL. Since it has no visual footprint, it consumes no width or height in the layout and does not affect spacing or alignment between the visible components. Its presence is purely logical, enabling deep linking and state restoration while leaving the three-column documentation layout and the central column’s vertical flow unchanged.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it does not occupy any physical space in the layout and has no effect on flow or positioning.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays documentation search results for a query and lets users navigate to the matching documentation pages.

core_features: Read the search query from the URL parameter `q` and initialize the page state with this query.
Invoke the `searchDocs` interface with the current query to retrieve structured search results.
Display the current query in an editable search input so users can refine and resubmit their search.
Render a list of search results returned by `searchDocs`, showing each result’s documentation page name, section/category labels, and a short content snippet.
Allow users to click any search result to navigate to the corresponding documentation page, using data returned in each result (e.g., `docPageId` or `docPageName`).
Handle loading, empty, and error states for the search operation without using static or hardcoded data tied to documentation content.
Keep the search state synchronized with the URL by updating the `q` parameter when the user performs a new search, so results can be shared and revisited via the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arriving from global search on another page
1. User types a query into the global search input in the header on any page (e.g., `index.html`).
2. User submits the form in the header; the site navigates to `search_results.html?q=<encoded-query>`.
3. The Search Results page reads `q` from the URL and passes it as `query` to the `searchDocs` interface.
4. While waiting for the response, the page shows a loading state and pre-fills the search input with the query from `q`.
5. When `searchDocs` returns, the page displays the results list, including section labels and snippets.
6. User scans results and clicks one; the browser navigates to the corresponding doc page URL derived from that result’s `docPageId`/`docPageName`.
7. The user reads the selected documentation page and can later return/back to the Search Results page with the same `q` preserved in the URL.

Workflow 2: Refining a search from within the Search Results page
1. User is on `search_results.html` with an existing `q` parameter and sees a list of results.
2. User edits the query in the page’s search input (e.g., adding terms or changing phrasing).
3. User submits the query via the on-page search form.
4. The page updates the URL to `search_results.html?q=<new-encoded-query>` using a full navigation or history push, without storing state in `localStorage`.
5. The page re-reads `q` from the URL, triggers `searchDocs` with the new query, and shows a loading state.
6. Once `searchDocs` resolves, the page replaces the old result list with the new results.
7. User can repeat this process, with each refinement reflected in the URL.

Workflow 3: Handling no-results and error cases
1. User navigates to `search_results.html?q=<query>` where either the query is very narrow or invalid.
2. The page calls `searchDocs` with the query from `q`.
3. `searchDocs` returns successfully but with an empty `results` array.
4. The page hides the results list component and displays an empty-state message that references the current query and suggests refining it (message text is generic UI copy, not tied to any static documentation data).
5. User modifies the query in the search input and resubmits, causing an updated `q` and a new `searchDocs` call.
6. If the `searchDocs` call fails (e.g., network error), the page shows an error message and keeps the query input available so the user can retry the search (re-triggering `searchDocs` when they resubmit).
interactions: Typing in the search input: Updates an in-component query buffer but does not immediately call `searchDocs` until the form is submitted, preventing excessive requests.

Submitting the search form: Prevents default browser form submission behavior if necessary, constructs a new URL with the `q` parameter set to the current input value, and navigates to that URL so the page lifecycle (including the `searchDocs` call) always reflects the URL state.

Initial page load or URL change: On page initialization (or when the URL changes), the page reads the `q` parameter. If `q` is present and non-empty, it triggers a `searchDocs({ query: q })` call and enters a loading state until results arrive.

Clicking a search result: Each result row or link is clickable. A click navigates to the corresponding documentation page URL computed from that result’s identifying fields (e.g., `docPageId` or `docPageName` mapped to a filename/path by the site’s routing logic). Standard browser behaviors such as opening in a new tab with modifier keys are preserved by rendering real links.
Handling loading state: When `searchDocs` is in progress, a loading indicator is shown and the previous results list is either hidden or visually marked as stale to avoid confusion.

Handling empty results: If `searchDocs` returns with an empty `results` array, the results list is not rendered, and an empty-state component appears instead, referencing the active `q` value so users understand that zero results correspond to their current query.
Handling errors: If the `searchDocs` call fails, the page shows an error component and optionally a retry action that re-invokes `searchDocs` with the current `q` without changing the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of state for this page is the search query contained in the URL parameter `q`. On every page load, the page reads `q` from `window.location` (or equivalent router). If `q` is undefined or an empty string, the page initializes with an empty search input and does not call `searchDocs` until the user submits a query. If `q` is present and non-empty, the page immediately calls `searchDocs` with `query` set to that value. The response from `searchDocs` is treated as the authoritative state for the current search: the `query` in the response is displayed as the current query; `results` is used to render the results list; and `searchResultId` can be stored in in-memory state for internal tracking but is not written to the URL. Whenever the user submits a new query via the search form, the page updates the `q` parameter in the URL to match the submitted value and then re-runs the initialization logic (read `q`, call `searchDocs`). All rendered content depends on the combination of (a) the current `q` from the URL and (b) the latest `searchDocs` response. No search-related state is persisted in `localStorage` or any other client-side storage; refreshes and shares of the URL rely solely on the `q` parameter to reconstruct the search.

components.functionality: Displays and manages the search input at the top of the page, synchronizing the query with the URL and initiating new searches.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the `q` parameter from the page URL and set the input field’s value to this query (or to an empty string if `q` is missing).
onChange: Update the component’s internal query buffer whenever the user types in the search input, without calling `searchDocs` yet.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission behavior if necessary, then construct a new URL for `search_results.html` with `q` set to the current input value, URL-encoded. Navigate to that URL (or update history). This navigation triggers the page-level initialization that will call `searchDocs` using the updated `q`.

components.functionality: Coordinates the execution of the `searchDocs` interface based on the `q` URL parameter and manages loading, success, empty, and error states before delegating rendering to subcomponents.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the `q` parameter from the URL. If `q` is present and non-empty, set internal state to `loading` and call `searchDocs` with `{ query: q }`. If `q` is absent or empty, set state to `idle` and skip the call.

onSearchDocsSuccess: Receive the response object from `searchDocs`, capturing `searchResultId`, `query`, and `results`. Set internal state to `success` and pass the data to the list/empty components for rendering.
onSearchDocsError: If the `searchDocs` call fails, set internal state to `error` and provide error details to the error component, while preserving the current `q` so the user can retry.
onRetry: Re-invoke `searchDocs` with the current query (from the URL or the last successful response) when the user clicks a retry action, without changing the URL.

components.functionality: Shows a loading state when a search request is in progress so users understand that results are being fetched.
event_handlers: onStateChange: Listen to state updates from `search-results-container` and render the loading indicator whenever the state is `loading`; hide it for all other states.

components.functionality: Displays an error message if the `searchDocs` call fails and exposes a control to retry the search.
event_handlers: onStateChange: When the `search-results-container` state is `error`, render a generic error message and show the current query (from URL `q`) so the user knows what failed.
onClickRetry: When the user clicks a retry control, emit an event consumed by `search-results-container` to call `searchDocs` again with the current query; the URL `q` remains unchanged.

components.functionality: Shows an informative message when a search completes successfully but returns no results for the current query.
event_handlers: onSearchDocsSuccess: If the `results` array in the `searchDocs` response is empty, render the empty-state UI and include the active query (from the response `query` or URL `q`) in the message. If `results` is non-empty, hide the empty-state component.

components.functionality: Renders the list of search results returned by `searchDocs`, including labels derived from `sectionType`/`category` and the snippet for each result.
event_handlers: onSearchDocsSuccess: Receive the `results` array from the `searchDocs` response when the orchestrator state is `success` and `results` is non-empty. For each result object, render a child `search-result-item` component, passing `entryId`, `docPageId`, `docPageName`, `category`, `sectionType`, and `snippet`.
onStateChange: Hide the list when in `loading`, `error`, or `idle` with no query; show the list only when there are results to display.

components.functionality: Represents a single row in the search results: clickable title, section label, and snippet, all derived from a single `searchDocs` result entry.
event_handlers: onRender: Convert the `sectionType` (e.g., `javascript_sdk`, `api_reference`) into a human-readable label algorithmically (for example, by replacing underscores with spaces and capitalizing words) without relying on static per-section data. Similarly, use `category` as a secondary label if needed.

onClick: When the user clicks the result’s main link, navigate to the corresponding documentation page. The URL is derived from the result’s identifying data (`docPageId` and/or `docPageName`) using the site’s routing logic (outside this component). This navigation is what triggers transitions to pages such as the JavaScript SDK docs, API reference pages, quickstarts, concepts, or examples, depending on which doc page the result refers to.

onAuxClick: Support standard browser behavior for auxiliary clicks (e.g., middle-click or Ctrl/Meta-click) by ensuring the main label is a standard anchor element whose `href` is the computed doc page URL, allowing opening results in new tabs or windows.

components.functionality: Provides a brief summary above the result list, such as indicating which query is being shown and how many results were returned.
event_handlers: onSearchDocsSuccess: When the `searchDocs` response arrives, compute the number of results from the `results` array length and display it along with the response `query` (or URL `q`) as summary text. If there are zero results, this component can delegate to or be hidden in favor of the `search-empty-state` component.
onStateChange: Hide the summary bar when there is no active query (`q` missing) or when in `error` state.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the documentation shell from the mockup: a fixed global header at the very top, a left documentation navigation sidebar, a central main column for the search results, and a right sidebar for in-page/contextual navigation. Within the central column, the search query header (search form) replaces the H1+intro stack as the primary focal element. Directly beneath it, the search results summary sits like a compact subheader, followed by the search results container which conditionally shows loading, error, empty, or results list states. Each result item is vertically stacked within the list, in a linear top-to-bottom reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the documentation shell from the mockup: a fixed global header at the very top, a left documentation navigation sidebar, a central main column for the search results, and a right sidebar for in-page/contextual navigation. Within the central column, the search query header (search form) replaces the H1+intro stack as the primary focal element. Directly beneath it, the search results summary sits like a compact subheader, followed by the search results container which conditionally shows loading, error, empty, or results list states. Each result item is vertically stacked within the list, in a linear top-to-bottom reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global grid follows the mockup: roughly 20–25% width for the left sidebar, 50–55% for the central content column, and 20–25% for the right sidebar on desktop. Inside the central column, the search-query-header spans nearly the full column width with generous top and bottom padding. The search-results-summary is slightly tighter but still full-width within the column. The search-results-container (and thus loading/error/empty/results content) occupies the remaining vertical space in the main column and expands with content. Right and left sidebars remain narrower and mostly vertical lists, preserving the 12-column-inspired tripartite layout.
layout_strategies.content_density: The central content keeps a moderately spacious, reading-friendly density: comfortable line heights, noticeable vertical spacing (~16–24px) between the search header, summary, and results states. Result items are moderately dense but with enough padding (12–16px) and vertical separation (~12–16px) to scan comfortably. Sidebars maintain slightly higher density than the center column but still include padding and group spacing for scannability, matching the documentation feel from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s Z-pattern and subsequent column handoff. The eye moves from the logo in the top-left of the global header to the centered global search/brand area, then to any right-side header actions. From there, focus drops into the left sidebar to confirm the user is in the Documentation section, then into the central search-query-header as the main interactive focus. After the search is seen or edited, attention naturally goes down to the search-results-summary and then into the search-results-list. As users scroll through results, the right sidebar offers a secondary horizontal shift in attention for quick jumps (e.g., to grouped result sections or related docs), reinforcing a left-to-center-to-right scanning pattern.

layout.overall_description: The page sits inside a modern documentation shell: a fixed, slim global header spans the full width at the top with the product logo on the left, a centered or slightly left-aligned global search, and a right-aligned CTA, all on a light background with subtle brand accents. Below this, the main viewport is divided into three vertical columns. On the left, a fixed-width navigation sidebar lists documentation sections and nested links in a vertically stacked, lightly padded list. In the center, a wider content column holds the search experience: first, a prominent search-query-header with the query input aligned to the left and spanning most of the column, sitting in generous whitespace. Directly under it, a concise search-results-summary reads like a secondary header bar. Beneath that, the search-results-container fills the remaining vertical space, swapping between loading, error, empty, or a scrolling list of search-result-item blocks, each full-width within the column and separated by consistent vertical gaps. On the right, a narrower sidebar lists in-page navigation or grouped search anchors in a compact but readable vertical list, likely sticky within the viewport. The overall effect is clean, calm, and structured: the central column is clearly the primary reading and interaction area, the sidebars provide orientation and navigation, and the spacing ensures the search results feel easy to scan rather than crowded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-query-header is positioned near the top of the central content column, just below the global header and any standard page category label (if used) and aligned with the main column’s left edge. It spans roughly 80–90% of the central column’s width, leaving a slim margin on the right for breathing room. Vertically, there is a generous gap (about 24–32px) between the global header and this search header, matching the mockup’s space before an H1. The search form itself is laid out horizontally within this area: the input field dominates the width, with any supplementary controls (e.g., clear, filters) aligned inline to the right inside the same row. The total height of this block is moderate but feels substantial because of the internal padding (about 12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) and the whitespace above and below. It sits directly above the search-results-summary, with a vertical gap of about 16–24px, creating a clear hierarchy of query first, then results context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column’s normal document flow; it scrolls offscreen as the user scrolls down through long lists of results, relying on the fixed global header for persistent orientation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-container occupies the full usable width of the central content column directly beneath the search-results-summary, extending downward as far as needed based on content. It is effectively the main vertical stack that holds the status and result components. The container aligns flush with the left edge of the central column and matches its internal content width (roughly 50–55% of the full page). There is a modest vertical gap (around 16px) between the summary bar above and the top of this container. Within this area, only one of the status views (loading, error, empty) or the search-results-list is visible at any given time, each centered relative to the container’s width. The container itself does not show heavy borders; separation is achieved via spacing and typography consistent with the documentation aesthetic. As the user scrolls, this entire block scrolls as a single column of content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally with the document; it becomes the main scrollable content region in the central column, while sidebars may remain partially or fully visible depending on screen height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the search-loading-indicator appears inside the search-results-container, immediately below the search-results-summary area. It is horizontally centered within the central column, occupying roughly 30–40% of the column width for any spinner and label, with ample whitespace to the left and right. Vertically, it sits about 24px below the summary bar, leaving clear separation. The indicator itself is compact, using subtle brand accents (e.g., purple) and neutral accompanying text. It does not stretch full width; instead, it forms a small focal point in the upper portion of the content area, surrounded by open space so the loading state feels deliberate and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically within the search-results-container during search operations, but otherwise follows normal scroll behavior with no fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-error-message replaces other content inside the search-results-container when an error occurs, positioned directly beneath the search-results-summary. It spans a majority of the central column’s width (about 70–80%), with equal left and right margins so it feels centered but still aligned to the overall column grid. The message is presented as a lightly emphasized block: a single or two-line error title with optional body text below, arranged in a vertical stack with 8–12px spacing between lines. There is generous padding inside the error block (around 12–16px) to distinguish it from the background, and vertical breathing room (about 24px) above and below so it does not visually collide with adjacent elements. Any retry button or link sits below the text, left-aligned within the same block, maintaining the minimalist style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed within the search-results-container in place of other views, scrolling with the main content column and not fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-empty-state appears inside the search-results-container when no results are found, occupying a central, moderately sized area beneath the search-results-summary. It is horizontally centered and typically constrained to about 50–60% of the central column width, with plenty of whitespace around it. The content is arranged vertically: a concise headline at the top, a short explanatory sentence or two, and optional guidance links or actions, each separated by 8–12px. This block is placed with generous top margin (about 32–40px from the summary) so it feels like an intentional, calm message rather than just a missing list. The visual treatment is light—subtle iconography or a faint border at most—aligning with the minimalist documentation style and avoiding heavy illustrations.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shown exclusively when there are zero matching results, contained within the central column’s normal flow and scrolling out of view as the user navigates away or scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-list fills the width of the search-results-container directly under the search-results-summary, aligned with the left edge of the central column and spanning nearly its full width (roughly 90–100% of the inner column). There is a small vertical gap (around 16px) between the summary bar and the first result item. The list is a vertical stack of search-result-item components, each separated by consistent vertical spacing (about 12–16px). The list itself has no heavy border; instead, it reads as a column of cards or rows floating in the central column’s white space. As results grow, this list becomes the main scrollable content, with sidebars remaining static or sticky around it, maintaining the documentation-like reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard vertical list inside the central content column, scrolling naturally with the page; no fixed or virtualized behaviors are implied at the layout level.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each search-result-item is a full-width row or card within the search-results-list, aligned with the central column’s left edge and extending to about 100% of the list’s inner width, minus standard horizontal padding (16–24px). Vertically, each item has internal padding of roughly 12–16px and a subtle bottom border or shadow to separate it from the next item, preserving a clean but scannable list. The internal content is arranged top-to-bottom: a title at the top (aligned left), followed by a brief snippet or description and optional metadata (such as doc section or tags) below, each layer separated by 4–8px. The overall height of each item is moderate, and consistent spacing of 12–16px between items ensures a rhythm that feels readable, not cramped. On hover, a minimal background change or border accent aligns with the modern DevTools aesthetic without breaking minimalism.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeated vertically within the search-results-list; items do not have independent fixed behavior and move together as the list scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-summary sits immediately below the search-query-header in the central column, acting like a subtle subheader for the results. It is left-aligned with the central column’s content and spans nearly the full width (about 90–100%), with internal horizontal padding of 12–16px if rendered as a bar. The height is compact—roughly one to two lines of text, such as “24 results for ‘batch evaluation’,” possibly with a small right-aligned control like a sort dropdown. Vertical spacing above (from the search-query-header) is about 16–24px, and spacing below (to the results container’s content) is about 12–16px, making it feel visually attached to both the query and the list. Typography is slightly smaller or lighter than the search input label but still clear, signaling its role as contextual metadata rather than the main headline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; it scrolls away with the results, acting as the top anchor of the results section without sticky behavior.

name: About
title: About
description: Functional specification for the About page, which loads and displays dynamic background information about the AI evaluation platform and links users to key documentation sections using the getAboutPageContent interface.

core_features: Load the About page metadata and main content by calling getAboutPageContent on initial page load, and bind the returned docPage, content, and keySectionDocPageIds to child components.
Render the returned content (markdown or HTML) as structured documentation, including headings and anchors that can be navigated via in-page links and URL parameters.
Display the About page title and description using the docPage fields provided by getAboutPageContent, ensuring the visible heading is fully data-driven.

Generate a list of navigable key sections based on keySectionDocPageIds, emitting navigation events with the selected documentation page ID so the global router can navigate to the corresponding pages (such as getting started or support).

Provide call-to-action links for new users that are derived from keySectionDocPageIds (e.g., the first ID used as a primary onboarding action, the second as a help/support-oriented action), without hardcoded link labels or URLs.

Support deep-linking into specific subsections of the About content using URL parameters (e.g., a section parameter that matches an anchor or heading ID in the content) and synchronize scroll position and highlighting accordingly.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Reading About information: (1) User opens about.html (optionally with URL parameters). (2) The page initializes and the about-page-loader component calls getAboutPageContent. (3) Once data is returned, the about-header component displays docPage.name and docPage.description. (4) The about-main-content component renders the content field as markdown/HTML. (5) The user scrolls through the page or clicks in-page anchors generated from headings to move between sections.

Workflow 2 – Navigating to key sections (e.g., Getting Started, Support): (1) User opens about.html and getAboutPageContent is executed. (2) The about-key-sections-list and about-cta-bar components receive keySectionDocPageIds. (3) Each key section entry is rendered as a clickable item whose label is derived from the content or externally provided mappings, not from hardcoded text. (4) When the user clicks a key section link (for example to start onboarding or to access help), the component emits an event containing the selected target docPageId. (5) The app-level router consumes this event and navigates to the corresponding page (such as the getting-started or support documentation) by constructing the correct URL for that docPageId.

Workflow 3 – Deep-linking to a subsection of the About content: (1) User opens about.html with a section URL parameter whose value matches an anchor or heading ID contained in the dynamic content. (2) The about-page-loader calls getAboutPageContent. (3) After the content is rendered, the about-main-content component checks the section URL parameter and searches the rendered content for a matching anchor/section ID. (4) If a match is found, the component scrolls the page to that section and optionally highlights it. (5) If the user clicks another in-page anchor, the component updates the URL (query parameter and/or hash) to reflect the new active section, keeping all state in the URL.

Workflow 4 – Returning from key sections to About with context: (1) User navigates away from About to another documentation page (such as a getting-started or support page) via a link emitted from the About components, with a ref parameter appended that encodes that the user came from the About page. (2) When the user navigates back to about.html (via browser back or an explicit link), the ref parameter may still be present. (3) The about-page-loader does not require this parameter, but the about-main-content and about-cta-bar components can read it to adjust emphasis (for example, emphasizing different key sections) while still relying only on dynamic data from getAboutPageContent.
interactions: Initial load: When about.html is loaded, the about-page-loader component triggers an onInit handler that calls getAboutPageContent. While the request is pending, it exposes a loading state that child components can use to render loading indicators instead of content.

Header interaction: The about-header component may expose anchors or small navigation links based on headings parsed from the content; when a user clicks one, onAnchorClick updates the URL (by changing the section parameter or hash) and instructs the about-main-content component to scroll to the corresponding section.

Scrolling and in-page navigation: The about-main-content component handles user scrolling and anchor link clicks. Clicking an internal anchor triggers onInternalLinkClick, which updates the current section state and URL (without using local storage) and scrolls into view.

Key section list click: In the about-key-sections-list component, clicking a list item triggers onKeySectionClick, emitting the associated target docPageId. The higher-level router listens for this event and navigates to the appropriate documentation page (for example, a quickstart or support page) by constructing a URL based on the docPageId.

Primary CTA click: In the about-cta-bar component, clicking the primary CTA triggers onPrimaryCtaClick, emitting the selected key docPageId (derived from keySectionDocPageIds, such as the first element). The router then navigates to the corresponding page, potentially including a ref parameter in the target URL to indicate the user came from the About page.

Secondary CTA click: In the about-cta-bar component, clicking the secondary CTA triggers onSecondaryCtaClick, emitting another key docPageId (such as the second element of keySectionDocPageIds, typically used for help or support access). The router then navigates to that page.

URL-driven state updates: When the URL changes (for example, the section query parameter changes through user interaction or navigation history), a global listener or the about-main-content component itself reacts, recalculates the active section based on the parameter value, and scrolls/highlights the relevant part of the content.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page does not require any URL parameters to function, but it supports URL-based state to control navigation and context. All state is kept in the URL and in-memory component state; no localStorage or other persistent client storage is used. Optional parameters include: (1) section: a string whose value is expected to match an anchor or heading ID within the dynamically loaded content. After getAboutPageContent returns and the content is rendered, about-main-content reads section and scrolls to the corresponding section. If section is missing or does not match any anchor, the component displays the content from the top. When the user clicks an in-page anchor, the component updates the section parameter (and optionally the hash) to match the selected section, so that reloading the page or sharing the URL restores the same position. (2) ref: a string used purely as contextual information for navigation (for example, indicating that the user arrived from a particular part of the site). The About page reads this parameter but does not rely on specific values; it may pass the ref value along when emitting navigation events (e.g., appended to CTA target URLs) so that downstream pages can adjust behavior. (3) Any unrecognized parameters are ignored by the About components. The page’s core data (docPage, content, keySectionDocPageIds) always comes exclusively from getAboutPageContent, and any navigation to other pages (e.g., a getting-started or support document) uses the docPageIds emitted from About components rather than hardcoded paths or IDs.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page by calling getAboutPageContent once on page load, manages loading and error states, and provides the resulting docPage, content, and keySectionDocPageIds to other components as reactive inputs.
event_handlers: onInit
onLoadSuccess
onLoadError
