components.functionality: Displays the About page heading and short description using the docPage data returned by getAboutPageContent. It can also provide quick in-page navigation links derived from headings parsed out of the main content when available.
event_handlers: onAnchorClick

components.functionality: Renders the main About content returned by getAboutPageContent.content as markdown or HTML, parses headings to create anchors, and synchronizes scroll position and active section with the section URL parameter and internal anchor clicks.
event_handlers: onInternalLinkClick
onSectionParamChange
onContentRendered

components.functionality: Shows a list of key documentation sections based on keySectionDocPageIds. Each item is rendered with a label derived from dynamic data (for example, from the content or from higher-level mappings) and, when clicked, emits an event containing the selected docPageId so the router can navigate to that documentation page.
event_handlers: onKeySectionClick

components.functionality: Provides prominent call-to-action links for new or blocked users, using the dynamically provided keySectionDocPageIds. Typically, the first ID in keySectionDocPageIds is used as a primary onboarding CTA and the second as a secondary support/help CTA. The component does not hardcode link text or URLs; labels and final navigation URLs are derived from dynamic data and router logic.
event_handlers: onPrimaryCtaClick
onSecondaryCtaClick

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page adopts the documentation template from the mockup: a fixed global header across the top, a three-column body beneath (left product/docs navigation, central article content, right contextual navigation). Within the central column, the About-specific components are ordered top-to-bottom as: about-header (category label, H1, subtitle, intro), about-main-content (richer background and details), then about-cta-bar (primary actions) at the bottom of the article. The right column replaces the usual in-page TOC with about-key-sections-list, following the same vertical list and indentation pattern used for the TOC. The about-page-loader overlays the central and right columns while data is loading, mimicking skeleton versions of the header, content, and key sections list.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page adopts the documentation template from the mockup: a fixed global header across the top, a three-column body beneath (left product/docs navigation, central article content, right contextual navigation). Within the central column, the About-specific components are ordered top-to-bottom as: about-header (category label, H1, subtitle, intro), about-main-content (richer background and details), then about-cta-bar (primary actions) at the bottom of the article. The right column replaces the usual in-page TOC with about-key-sections-list, following the same vertical list and indentation pattern used for the TOC. The about-page-loader overlays the central and right columns while data is loading, mimicking skeleton versions of the header, content, and key sections list.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page body beneath the global header maintains roughly a 20–25% / 50–55% / 20–25% width split for left sidebar, central content, and right sidebar respectively, with a consistent horizontal gutter (around 24–32px) between columns. The about-header, about-main-content, and about-cta-bar each span the full width of the central column. The about-key-sections-list occupies the majority of the right sidebar width with 16–24px internal padding, sitting within the same vertical scroll region as the main content. The about-page-loader, when active, stretches over the combined central and right columns, leaving the fixed global header and left navigation visible but visually de-emphasized.
layout_strategies.content_density: The central column is optimized for comfortable reading: relatively low density, with generous line height and 24–40px vertical spacing between logical blocks (label, title, subtitle, intro, sections). Paragraphs and headings inside about-main-content are well spaced (16–24px between paragraphs, 32–48px between sections). The about-key-sections-list is more compact but still breathable, with 8–12px between navigation rows and 16–24px padding around the group. The about-cta-bar has moderate density, with enough padding (16–24px vertically, 24–32px horizontally) so buttons and text feel substantial but not bulky. The loader uses lightly shaded skeleton bars with ample spacing to preserve the minimalist, calm feel even during loading.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the global header: logo on the top-left, search centered, top-right CTA. From there, the user’s eye drops diagonally to the left sidebar to confirm they are in the Documentation section and that the About page is selected. Attention then moves horizontally into the central column, landing first on the small category label and the large H1 in about-header, then down through subtitle and intro. After the intro and as users begin scrolling into about-main-content, peripheral awareness shifts to the right column, where about-key-sections-list offers quick access to core doc areas, mirroring the TOC pattern from the mockup. As users approach the end of the central content, focus is drawn to the horizontally spanning about-cta-bar, which aligns with the overall flow by summarizing next steps and providing clear primary actions without disrupting the established reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: Below the fixed global header, the About page is laid out as a three-column documentation view framed by generous white space. On the far left, a fixed-width sidebar lists the main product areas and documentation sections; it anchors the experience but visually recedes once the user focuses on the content. The central column, occupying just over half of the viewport, holds the About-specific story: at its top, a compact category label and a large, confident H1 introduce the page, followed by a shorter subtitle and a relaxed introductory paragraph with ample breathing room. As the user scrolls, this column flows into deeper narrative sections and occasional media blocks that extend the full central width, all separated by consistent vertical spacing. On the right, a narrower sidebar hosts the key documentation sections in a vertically stacked list, starting near the top of the column and gently sticking below the global header as the user scrolls, mimicking an in-page TOC while instead pointing to core docs. Throughout the experience, the columns are clearly separated with comfortable gutters, and the typography and spacing keep the layout calm and legible. At the bottom of the central column, a full-width CTA bar appears like a subtle card across the content area, offering clear next actions related to getting started with evaluations or exploring specific guides. When data is loading, a soft overlay spans the central and right columns, replacing real content with skeleton blocks aligned to the header, content sections, and key sections list while leaving the global header and left navigation visible, giving users a clear sense of structure even before the content appears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-loader sits above the main content area as a full-overlay layer that covers both the central content column and the right sidebar, while leaving the global header and left navigation sidebar visible but visually subdued. It is positioned directly below the global header, starting flush with the top edge of the main content region and extending down to at least the first full viewport height (and further if content height is known). Its width matches the combined width of the central column (about 50–55% of the page) and the right column (about 20–25%), aligning precisely with their outer edges and maintaining the same left gutter relative to the left navigation. Inside the overlay, skeleton elements are vertically stacked to mirror the About layout: a small, narrow bar in the top-left of the central region approximates the category label; below it, a wider bar simulates the H1; one or two medium-width bars mimic the subtitle and intro paragraph; and a taller block spans most of the central column width where the first main content section would appear. To the right, within the sidebar area, shorter stacked skeleton rows mirror the key sections list. The overlay uses a semi-opaque white or very light tint over the base background, with low-contrast skeleton shapes, ensuring the structure is visible but not visually heavy. It scrolls with the page content, remaining fixed relative to the central/right scrollable region until loading completes, at which point it fades out to reveal the real about-header, about-main-content, and about-key-sections-list beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay positioned relative to the main content area (central + right columns), scrolling with the page but sitting at a higher z-index than the content it covers; appears only while data is loading and then is removed or faded out.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-header is anchored at the top of the central content column and defines the starting point of the About page narrative. It is positioned directly below the global header, with a vertical gap of roughly 32–40px to create a comfortable transition from global navigation into page content. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the central column, which occupies approximately 50–55% of the overall viewport and is centered between the left navigation and the right sidebar with 24–32px gutters on each side. Within this block, the elements follow a tight vertical rhythm aligned to the left: at the very top, a small, all-caps or subtly colored category label (e.g., “About” or “Overview”) sits with minimal height, separated from the H1 by about 8–12px. Below that, the main page title (H1) is large and bold, using most of the central column width but leaving generous margins on either side for readability. An 8–12px gap follows before a shorter subtitle in a lighter weight or slightly smaller size, usually one or two lines, aligning with the left edge of the H1. Another 12–16px gap leads into a brief introductory paragraph spanning a comfortable line length (roughly 60–80 characters per line). The combined height of the header block, including all text and internal spacing, typically occupies the upper third of the first viewport on desktop, leaving enough room below for the start of the main content or a hint of the next section to be visible. No borders are used; instead, whitespace and typographic hierarchy distinguish the header as the primary focal region in the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored at the top of the central content column; scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-main-content component sits immediately below the about-header within the central column, separated from the intro paragraph by a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px to clearly signal the transition from introduction to deeper content. It spans the full width of the central column, maintaining the same left alignment and line length established in the header. The content inside is arranged in a linear top-to-bottom flow, structured into logical sections describing the AI evaluation platform (e.g., mission, architecture, core concepts, usage scenarios). Each section begins with a heading that is visually subordinate to the H1 but clearly distinguished from body text, followed by one or more paragraphs, bullet lists, or code/diagram blocks. Vertical spacing between headings and their associated paragraphs is around 8–12px, between paragraphs is around 12–16px, and between major sections (e.g., from one H2 block to the next) is in the 32–48px range, with 48–64px for very distinct concept shifts. Any media (such as diagrams or screenshots) within this component align to the left edge of the text and typically extend to 90–100% of the central column width, with 24–32px vertical spacing above and below them so they feel integrated but not crowded. As users scroll, this component forms the main vertical reading track, occupying most of the vertical canvas, while the right sidebar remains visible in the periphery. At the end of the about-main-content, a generous vertical margin of 48–64px separates the last section from the about-cta-bar, ensuring the CTA area feels like a distinct but related conclusion rather than an extension of the narrative text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content region forming the main vertical flow of the central column; content height grows with the richness of the About specification and scrolls under any sticky right-sidebar elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-key-sections-list occupies the right sidebar column and visually echoes the in-page TOC style from the mockup, adapted to list key documentation areas instead of headings. Horizontally, it sits in the rightmost 20–25% of the viewport, separated from the central column by a gutter of approximately 24–32px. The list itself is contained within a subtly differentiated block (either a light background tint or simply a compact card-style grouping) with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Vertically, its top edge aligns roughly with the lower part of the about-header—either aligned with the first baseline of the H1 or slightly below the intro paragraph—so that it becomes visible as soon as the user starts to engage with the page but does not compete directly with the page title. At the top of the list, a small title like “Key sections” or “Explore docs” appears in a slightly stronger weight than the list items, separated below by 8–12px. Under this heading, key doc areas (e.g., “Getting Started”, “Evaluation Runs”, “API Reference”, “Examples”) are arranged in a vertical list with 8–12px between items. If sections are grouped, group labels appear in a lighter or smaller style with a 12–16px gap above them and child links indented by 8–16px, mirroring the indentation hierarchy used for the mockup’s TOC. Active or recommended links can be highlighted with a subtle accent color and a pill or underline, but still remain visually secondary to the central H1 and main content. The overall height of the list is designed to fit comfortably within a typical viewport height on desktop; if it exceeds this, it scrolls within the page rather than within its own internal scroll container, keeping behavior consistent with the rest of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right sidebar: it remains fixed relative to the viewport once it reaches a position just below the global header, so it stays visible as the user scrolls through the central about-main-content, and then scrolls offscreen only when reaching the bottom of the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-cta-bar is placed in the central column near the bottom of the About page, serving as a conclusive, action-oriented section. It is positioned directly below the last block of about-main-content, separated by a generous vertical margin of approximately 48–64px so it reads as a distinct, summarizing strip rather than a continuation of the narrative. Horizontally, the CTA bar spans the full width of the central column, aligning with the same left and right edges as the header and main content. The bar appears as a low-profile card: a subtle background tint or faint border, with rounded corners consistent with the site’s design language, and internal padding of about 16–24px vertically and 24–32px horizontally. Inside, layout follows a horizontal arrangement on desktop: on the left, a short, strong heading such as “Ready to start evaluating?” and one or two lines of supporting text stack vertically with 4–8px between them; on the right, primary and secondary CTAs (e.g., a filled “Get started guide” button and a ghost “View API reference” link) are right-aligned and grouped with 8–16px horizontal spacing. On narrower viewports, this can naturally stack, placing the heading and copy on top and CTAs below, still within the same padded card. The bar’s height is modest, typically 72–120px depending on text length, ensuring it stands out without feeling heavy. It appears roughly within the lower third of the full page scroll, becoming the last major element in the central column before the footer or end-of-page space, acting as the final stop in the visual journey from understanding to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored at the bottom of the central column’s content flow; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the About content and remains in place as the final section, without sticky behavior.

name: Support
title: Support
description: Provides dynamically loaded support channels and troubleshooting guidance, with deep links into related documentation pages and the playground.

core_features: Load Support page metadata, available support channels, and troubleshooting sections via getSupportPageContent on page initialization.
Display a list of support channels (email, issue tracker, knowledge base, etc.) with interaction behavior based on the channel type and details returned by getSupportPageContent.
Display a searchable, expandable list of troubleshooting sections covering evaluations, APIs, SDKs, metrics, and playground issues, based on troubleshootingSections returned by getSupportPageContent.

Provide navigation from each troubleshooting section into relevant documentation using relatedDocPageIds and the site’s routing, including direct links to api_reference.html, guides.html, concepts_metrics.html, and playground.html according to the type of issue.
Allow deep linking into a specific support channel or troubleshooting section using URL parameters, and keep URL parameters in sync with user interactions (no localStorage).
Expose a structured area where other pages can link users back to a specific troubleshooting section (e.g., from an error state elsewhere in the docs) by setting URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View general support options
1. User navigates to support.html (e.g., via the footer link).
2. On load, the page calls getSupportPageContent and stores docPage, supportChannels, and troubleshootingSections in page state.
3. The Support title is set from docPage.name and description text from docPage.description.
4. The Support Channels list renders one entry per supportChannels item.
5. If the URL contains a channel parameter, the corresponding channel is pre-selected; otherwise the first channel in supportChannels is selected by default.
6. When the user clicks a support channel, the Support Channel Detail component displays the associated details and executes the appropriate behavior (e.g., navigation to a URL, opening an email client, or copying information). The URL is updated to reflect the newly selected channel.

Workflow: Troubleshoot a specific problem
1. User scrolls to (or is automatically scrolled to) the Troubleshooting section of the page.
2. If the URL contains a section parameter, the Troubleshooting List component expands the matching troubleshootingSection based on a deterministic section identifier derived from the section title.
3. The user can enter a keyword into a troubleshooting search input; the Troubleshooting List filters troubleshootingSections in-memory using the search term, and the query parameter in the URL is updated.
4. The user clicks on a troubleshooting section summary to expand it; the Troubleshooting Detail Panel shows the full summary plus related documentation links built from relatedDocPageIds.
5. When the user clicks a related documentation link associated with API issues, the browser navigates to api_reference.html with appropriate query parameters (e.g., source=support and a section-specific identifier). Similarly, clicking links for guides navigates to guides.html, metric-related links to concepts_metrics.html, and playground-related links to playground.html.
6. The user can navigate back (browser back button); the Support page reads the URL parameters again and restores the previously selected troubleshooting section and search query.

Workflow: Deep link from another page into a specific Support issue
1. Another page in the docs constructs a URL like support.html?section=<section_identifier>&view=troubleshooting.
2. The user is redirected or clicks this URL to open the Support page.
3. On load, Support Page Loader calls getSupportPageContent and then Support Deep Link Handler parses URL parameters.
4. The handler sets the active view to Troubleshooting, matches section_identifier to the appropriate troubleshootingSection using the same identifier generation logic used when building the URL, and expands that section in the Troubleshooting List.
5. The user reviews suggested steps and follows related documentation links or support channels as needed.

Workflow: Start in the Playground from a troubleshooting suggestion
1. User searches or browses troubleshootingSections to understand issues related to evaluation setup.
2. A troubleshooting section with guidance that suggests trying an evaluation in the Playground presents a link (e.g., “Open in Playground”) that navigates to playground.html with query parameters such as source=support and an issue identifier.
3. The user clicks this link; the browser navigates to playground.html, and the Playground page can optionally interpret the query parameters to preconfigure a sample run or highlight relevant guidance.
interactions:
Interaction: Page initialization
- Trigger: Window load / component mount.
- Behavior: Call getSupportPageContent with no parameters. When the promise resolves, store the result in internal state and render all dependent components. If URL parameters exist, apply them to select the active view, channel, and troubleshooting section.
- URL impact: None on initial fetch; afterwards, may normalize or add missing parameters (e.g., default view) without changing the underlying content.

Interaction: Select support channel
- Trigger: User clicks on a channel item in the Support Channel List.
- Behavior: Mark that channel as selected, show its details in the Support Channel Detail component, and optionally perform an immediate action depending on channel type (e.g., open a link, prepare an email, or provide a copy-to-clipboard button).
- URL impact: Update or append a channel parameter (e.g., support.html?channel=<channel_identifier>) without reloading the page.

Interaction: Filter troubleshooting sections by keyword
- Trigger: User types into the troubleshooting search input or clears it.
- Behavior: Apply client-side filtering of troubleshootingSections based on the keyword against properties like title and summary. Show only matching sections in the Troubleshooting List.
- URL impact: Update or remove a query parameter (e.g., q=<encoded_keyword>) to reflect the current filter so that the state can be restored on reload or shared.

Interaction: Expand/collapse troubleshooting section
- Trigger: User clicks on a troubleshooting section header.
- Behavior: If the section is currently collapsed, expand it and collapse any previously expanded section, and scroll it into view if necessary. If it is already expanded, collapse it.
- URL impact: When a section is expanded, set section=<section_identifier> in the URL. When no section is expanded, remove the section parameter.

Interaction: Click related documentation link from a troubleshooting section
- Trigger: User clicks a link rendered under a troubleshooting section.
- Behavior: Based on the type of issue and routing configuration, build a URL pointing to api_reference.html, guides.html, concepts_metrics.html, or playground.html. Include query parameters such as source=support, supportSection=<section_identifier>, and a related doc identifier derived from relatedDocPageIds.
- URL impact: Browser navigates away to the target page using standard link navigation; the query parameters allow the target page to understand context.

Interaction: URL parameter change (manual or via history)
- Trigger: Browser back/forward navigation or manual modification of URL parameters (section, channel, q, view).
- Behavior: A URL listener within Support Deep Link Handler parses the new URL and updates active channel selection, active troubleshooting section, and search filter to match the new parameters without re-fetching content unless necessary.
- URL impact: None (read-only in this interaction; the handler interprets existing parameters).
page_functionality.state_logic: The Support page uses only URL parameters and in-memory state (no localStorage) to manage and restore user state. On load, getSupportPageContent is called once and its result initializes the main dataset for the page. The following URL parameters are supported: view (e.g., 'channels' or 'troubleshooting') determines which primary section is focused when the page loads; channel identifies the selected support channel, using a stable identifier derived deterministically from the channel's type and label (e.g., a slug); section identifies the expanded troubleshooting section, using a deterministic identifier derived from the section title; q holds the current troubleshooting search query. When the user interacts with the page (selecting a channel, expanding a section, changing the search query), the page updates these parameters using the History API without reloading. On browser back/forward navigation, the Support Deep Link Handler re-reads the URL, then updates in-memory state to bring the UI back into sync (selected channel, expanded section, and search filter). Navigation to external documentation pages preserves context by including source and issue-identifying parameters in the target URLs, but those target pages own their own state logic.

components.functionality: Initializes the Support page by calling getSupportPageContent, storing docPage metadata, supportChannels, and troubleshootingSections in state, and passing them down to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when support.html loads; invokes getSupportPageContent() and sets loading/error states.
onLoadSuccess: Receives the response { docPage, supportChannels, troubleshootingSections } and writes them into a shared page state store.
onLoadError: Updates an error state that downstream components can react to (e.g., display a non-hardcoded error message based on the error object).

components.functionality: Renders the Support page title and description using the docPage fields returned by getSupportPageContent, ensuring they remain consistent with server-provided metadata.
event_handlers: onDocPageChange: When docPage is updated by support-page-loader, set the document title to docPage.name and display docPage.description where available.

components.functionality: Controls which primary view is emphasized (support channels vs troubleshooting) based on URL parameter view and user interaction.
event_handlers: onInitFromURL: Reads the view parameter and sets the initial active view.
onClickViewTab: When the user selects a different view, updates in-memory activeView state and writes view=<new_view> into the URL using the History API.
onURLChange: When the URL changes (e.g., via back/forward), re-reads view and updates the UI accordingly.

components.functionality: Displays all available support channels returned by getSupportPageContent.supportChannels and allows the user to select one for more details or immediate action.
event_handlers: onInitFromURL: Reads the channel parameter, matches it to a channel by computing a deterministic identifier (e.g., a slug from type and label), and marks that channel as selected if found.
onClickChannel: Sets the clicked channel as selected, updates the URL channel parameter, and notifies the support-channel-detail component of the new selection.
onSupportChannelsChange: Re-renders the list if supportChannels changes (e.g., due to a refreshed getSupportPageContent call).

components.functionality: Shows the details and actionable UI for the currently selected support channel, interpreting the channel's type and details fields to decide what actions to make available (e.g., link navigation, email composition, or copyable text).
event_handlers: onSelectedChannelChange: When notified by support-channel-list of a new selection, displays the chosen channel's label and details and configures relevant action controls.

onClickPrimaryAction: Depending on the selected channel's type and details, performs an appropriate action such as opening a URL, generating a mailto link, or copying details to the clipboard. Any URLs used are derived from the details value, not hardcoded here.
onURLChange: Reads the channel parameter and re-selects the appropriate channel if page state has been reset.

components.functionality: Provides a text input to filter troubleshootingSections based on their title and summary, enabling users to quickly find relevant issues.
event_handlers: onInitFromURL: Reads the q parameter from the URL, sets the search input value, and notifies the troubleshooting-list component to apply this filter.
onChangeQuery: When the user types or clears the input, debounces the updates, notifies troubleshooting-list with the new query, and updates the q parameter in the URL (removing it if empty).
onURLChange: When URL changes externally, updates the input value to match the q parameter and triggers a re-filter in troubleshooting-list.

components.functionality: Displays a list of troubleshooting sections, filtered by any active search query, and allows the user to expand a section to view more details.
event_handlers: onInitFromURL: Reads section and q parameters and determines which sections to display and which to expand, using a deterministic identifier generated from each section.title for comparison.
onFilterChange: Receives the current query from troubleshooting-search-filter and filters troubleshootingSections in-memory, maintaining the currently expanded section where possible.

onClickSectionHeader: Toggles expansion of the clicked section; when expanding, generates a section identifier from the section.title, updates local activeSectionId, syncs the URL section parameter, and notifies troubleshooting-detail-panel.
onURLChange: When the URL changes, re-applies the active section and filter query by computing identifiers from troubleshootingSections.

components.functionality: Shows full details for the currently selected troubleshooting section, along with dynamically constructed links to related documentation and tools (API Reference, Guides, Metrics concepts, Playground).
event_handlers: onActiveSectionChange: When notified by troubleshooting-list of a new active section, retrieves its summary and relatedDocPageIds and prepares link objects for rendering.

onClickRelatedDocLink: Builds and navigates to the appropriate URL for a related document. The target base (api_reference.html, guides.html, concepts_metrics.html, or playground.html) is chosen according to routing rules configured in the client (e.g., based on section context or external mapping), and the query string includes parameters like source=support, supportSection=<section_identifier>, and docId=<related_docPageId>.
onURLChange: When the section parameter changes, recomputes the active section using the title-derived identifier and refreshes the displayed details.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters (view, channel, section, q) so that all components stay in sync with the browser's address bar and back/forward navigation.
event_handlers: onInit: Runs on page load, parses current URLSearchParams, and broadcasts initial state (view, channel, section, q) to interested components such as support-view-switcher, support-channel-list, troubleshooting-search-filter, and troubleshooting-list.

onUpdateState: When a child component requests a state change (e.g., user selects a channel or expands a section), merges the new state into the existing URL parameters and updates the browser history using pushState or replaceState without reloading the page.
onPopState: Listens for browser popstate events (back/forward) and re-parses the URL, broadcasting updated view, channel, section, and q values back to the relevant components.

components.functionality: Ensures that navigation actions initiated from Support (e.g., clicking a troubleshooting link) respect the outgoing navigation targets defined for api_reference.html, guides.html, concepts_metrics.html, and playground.html.
event_handlers: onClickApiErrorTroubleshootingLink: Navigates to api_reference.html with query parameters marking source=support and the originating section identifier.
onClickGuideTroubleshootingLink: Navigates to guides.html with source=support and relevant identifiers.
onClickMetricsTroubleshootingLink: Navigates to concepts_metrics.html with source=support and relevant identifiers.
onClickPlaygroundLink: Navigates to playground.html with source=support and relevant identifiers, allowing the playground page to pre-interpret the context if needed.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column grid with three main vertical regions: fixed-width left sidebar, fluid central content column, and narrower right sidebar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page lives inside the existing documentation shell: fixed global header at the top, left documentation navigation sidebar, fluid central content column, and a narrower right sidebar reserved for in-page navigation or quick links. Within the central column, the Support page content follows a strict top-to-bottom flow: support-header (category label, H1, subtitle, intro) at the very top, then the support-view-switcher, then either the support-channel-* components (Channels view) or the troubleshooting-* components (Troubleshooting view), and finally the support-footer-links at the bottom. Data/state components (support-page-loader, support-deep-link-handler) visually overlay or influence this flow without breaking it.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page lives inside the existing documentation shell: fixed global header at the top, left documentation navigation sidebar, fluid central content column, and a narrower right sidebar reserved for in-page navigation or quick links. Within the central column, the Support page content follows a strict top-to-bottom flow: support-header (category label, H1, subtitle, intro) at the very top, then the support-view-switcher, then either the support-channel-* components (Channels view) or the troubleshooting-* components (Troubleshooting view), and finally the support-footer-links at the bottom. Data/state components (support-page-loader, support-deep-link-handler) visually overlay or influence this flow without breaking it.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On a typical desktop viewport, the left sidebar occupies roughly 20–22% of the width, the central content column about 56–60%, and the right sidebar about 20–22%. The global header spans 100% width with modest vertical height. All Support page components sit inside the central column, which is centered in the remaining viewport width with generous left and right padding. Within that column, most content blocks (headers, lists, detail panels) span nearly the full column width (around 80–90% of the browser width minus sidebars), with comfortable internal padding and whitespace above and below.
layout_strategies.content_density: The Support content uses a reading-optimized density similar to the mockup: generous line-height, moderate font sizes, and vertical spacing in the 16–32px range between related elements, with 32–48px spacing between larger conceptual blocks (e.g., between header and view switcher, between lists and detail panels, and above the footer links). Lists (channels and troubleshooting items) are denser than the main text but still have enough padding and row height to be easily scannable without appearing crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The primary visual flow mimics the mockup: users first notice the global header (logo on the top-left, search in the center, primary CTA on the top-right), then their attention drops to the left sidebar to confirm they are in the Support section. From there, the eye moves into the central Support header (H1 and intro) and then directly to the view switcher, which presents the main choices (Channels vs Troubleshooting). Below the switcher, users either scan vertically through support-channel-list or troubleshooting-list. When an item is selected, the corresponding detail panel appears below the list, maintaining a natural downward reading flow. The right sidebar provides a secondary left-to-right flow when users look for quick navigation across major Support page sections (e.g., anchors into Channels, Troubleshooting, Playground links), updated by the support-deep-link-handler.

layout.overall_description: The Support page appears within a modern documentation shell: a fixed global header at the top, a structured left navigation column, a wide central reading column, and a slimmer right sidebar. The central column opens with a compact but clear Support header section: a small, colored category label, a large H1 title, a short subtitle, and a concise introductory paragraph, all left-aligned with comfortable spacing that invites reading. Directly beneath this, a horizontal pill-style view switcher lets users toggle between Channels and Troubleshooting modes, anchoring the functional core of the page. When Channels is active, a vertical list of support channels fills the central column, each item spanning the content width with card-like affordances; selecting a channel reveals a detailed panel below the list, keeping the user in a single continuous scroll context. When Troubleshooting is active, a prominent search/filter bar sits under the switcher, followed by a vertically stacked list of issues and, again, an inline detail panel that appears beneath the list when an item is chosen. Throughout, the left sidebar keeps users oriented in the broader documentation, while the right sidebar presents in-page anchors or quick links that relate to the current Support view. The entire layout feels airy, with consistent spacing and alignment, reinforcing a calm, developer-friendly environment for solving problems.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-page-loader appears only during data fetching states and overlays the central content column without affecting the permanent layout. It is positioned relative to the main content area: a semi-transparent light backdrop covers the full height and width of the central column (from just below the global header down to the top of the footer area), subtly dimming the underlying Support content. At the visual center of this column (both horizontally centered and vertically centered within the visible portion of the central column), a compact loader element (spinner or progress indicator with a short label like 'Loading support…') is displayed. The loader does not extend into the left or right sidebars; those remain visible but visually deprioritized by contrast. Vertical padding around the loader label maintains the page’s spacing scale (approximately 16–24px around the loader content), echoing the mockup’s comfort and clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the central content column using absolute or fixed positioning within the central column container; non-sticky and removed once data is loaded. Does not scroll independently; it moves with the central column if the user scrolls during loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-header occupies the topmost portion of the central content column, directly below the global header and aligned to the same left margin as the main article text. There is a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px between the bottom of the global header and the start of this section, so it feels distinct and uncluttered. At the top of support-header, a small, uppercase category label (e.g., 'Support' or 'Help & Support') appears in the brand accent color, left-aligned and constrained to a single line. Roughly 8–12px below this label sits the H1 title 'Support' in large, bold type that spans about 80% of the central column’s width, wrapping to multiple lines if needed but never reaching edge-to-edge. Another 8–12px below the H1, a subtler subtitle line (short description) appears in medium-weight text, slightly smaller and in a neutral color. Finally, after a 12–16px gap, a multi-line introductory paragraph extends across the same text width as the subtitle, with comfortable line-height for reading. The entire support-header section occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the central column’s vertical viewport on initial load, establishing context before any interactive elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the central column; scrolls away with the page content. No sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-view-switcher sits directly beneath the support-header, aligned to the same left text edge, and is separated from the last line of the introductory paragraph by a vertical gap of about 24px. It presents as a compact, horizontal pill-style toggle occupying roughly 40–60% of the central column’s width, depending on the number of options (e.g., 'Channels', 'Troubleshooting'). The pills are arranged left-to-right, with each pill having about 12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding, matching the mockup’s button/nav pill sizing. The switcher’s container remains left-aligned, not centered, preserving the documentation-like rhythm; the right side of the central column stays visually open, providing breathing room. When the active view is selected, that pill is highlighted with the brand accent color and possibly a subtle background, providing a clear focal point that guides users into the subsequent content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline control within the central column; scrolls with the page. May remain visually close to the top of the viewport on shorter pages but does not stick; switching views toggles which sets of components (support-channel-* vs troubleshooting-*) are shown below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-channel-list appears only when the 'Channels' view is active in the support-view-switcher. It is positioned directly beneath the switcher, with about 24–32px of vertical separation to visually indicate a new functional block. The list is left-aligned with the header and switcher, occupying around 80–90% of the central column’s width, leaving a modest right margin as whitespace. Vertically, it stacks channel entries in a column, each rendered as a card-like row with 16–20px internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, consistent with the mockup’s nav cards and documentation sections. Items are separated by 8–12px gaps, and logical groupings (e.g., 'Contact us', 'Community', 'Playground support') can be separated by slightly larger 16–24px gaps and subtle section labels to maintain scannability. The top of the list typically sits in the upper-middle viewport on load, and as users scroll, the list extends downward, occupying most of the middle portion of the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the central column; scrolls with the page. Visibility is controlled by the support-view-switcher state (shown only in Channels mode).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-channel-detail panel is visually tied to the support-channel-list and appears below it when a specific channel is selected. There is a clear 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the channel list and the top of this detail panel, signaling a transition from overview to detail while preserving a continuous top-to-bottom reading flow. The panel spans roughly the same width as the channel list (around 80–90% of the central column) and is left-aligned with it. It is styled as a contained card with a subtle border or shadow and 20–24px of internal padding on all sides, matching the design language of the mockup’s content blocks. Inside, headings, short paragraphs, and possibly inline links or CTAs (e.g., 'Open Playground', 'View related docs') are stacked vertically with 12–16px gaps. On initial selection, the page may scroll just enough to bring the start of this detail panel near the upper half of the viewport, but the panel itself remains within the central flow, not overlapping sidebars.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally as an inline block below support-channel-list. Scrolls with the page; not sticky and does not overlay other content. When no channel is selected, it can be hidden or replaced with a brief placeholder message occupying a minimal vertical height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The troubleshooting-search-filter is displayed only when the 'Troubleshooting' view is active in the support-view-switcher. It occupies a prominent position directly underneath the switcher, mirroring where the support-channel-list begins in Channels mode. A vertical gap of 24–32px separates it from the view switcher, preserving consistent spacing between sections. The search/filter form is left-aligned and spans about 60–70% of the central column’s width, wide enough to comfortably show a long query. The main search input sits horizontally, with 12–14px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding, echoing the mockup’s search bar in scale but adapted for this page. Optional filter controls (e.g., a dropdown for category or severity) sit to the right of or below the main input with an 8–12px gap, maintaining a compact, developer-friendly aesthetic. The area above and below this component remains relatively open, highlighting it as the primary entry point for troubleshooting.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form element within the central column; visible only in Troubleshooting mode. Scrolls with the page and does not stick, though it typically remains near the top of the viewport during initial interactions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The troubleshooting-list is placed immediately below the troubleshooting-search-filter, with about 24px of vertical space in between. It extends downwards in a single vertical column that aligns to the same left margin and spans roughly 80–90% of the central column’s width. Each troubleshooting item is a compact, clickable row or small card, with roughly 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding, slightly denser than the support-channel-list because users may scan more items here. Items may be grouped under section headings like 'Common Errors' or 'Playground Issues', with a 16–24px gap above each heading and slightly larger typography to maintain hierarchy. The entire list can grow to occupy much of the central column’s scrollable height; as the user scrolls down, the right sidebar remains visible, offering anchor links into different troubleshooting categories if implemented.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the central column; visible only when Troubleshooting is active. Scrolls naturally with the page. Selecting an item influences the display of troubleshooting-detail-panel below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The troubleshooting-detail-panel sits directly below the troubleshooting-list and appears when a specific troubleshooting item is selected. It is separated from the bottom of the list by approximately 24–32px, mirroring the space used between the channel list and the support-channel-detail panel to keep the layout consistent across views. The panel’s width matches that of the troubleshooting-list (around 80–90% of the central column), left-aligned, and visually styled as a bordered or lightly elevated card with 20–24px internal padding. Within the panel, the issue title is placed at the top as a clear heading, followed by step-by-step guidance, code examples, and links to related documentation or the playground, all arranged in a vertical stack with 12–16px gaps. The content is designed to be read like a mini documentation article. On selection, the page may auto-scroll so that the panel begins near the top or middle of the viewport, enabling focused reading while keeping the context of the list above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline detail block that appears conditionally below troubleshooting-list when an item is active. Scrolls with the page; no overlay or sticky behavior. Collapses or hides when no troubleshooting item is selected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-deep-link-handler has no direct visual representation; instead, it subtly coordinates the URL and scroll state with the visible components. Conceptually, it lives at the page level of the central column, 'behind' the Support content. When a deep link is loaded (e.g., a URL pointing to a specific channel or troubleshooting issue), the handler ensures that the correct view is active in support-view-switcher, the relevant list item is highlighted, and the corresponding detail panel (support-channel-detail or troubleshooting-detail-panel) is visible and scrolled into an appropriate position within the central column. It also updates the browser URL and may synchronize with the right sidebar’s in-page navigation so that the appropriate anchor appears active. This behavior maintains the linear visual flow while enabling direct navigation into deeper content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, occupies no layout space. Acts as a URL and scroll state manager tied to the central column; influences which components are visible and where the page scrolls, but does not render any UI itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-footer-links component anchors the bottom of the central content column, appearing after whichever detail panel is last in the flow (support-channel-detail or troubleshooting-detail-panel), or directly after the lists if no detail is visible. There is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px separating it from the content above, visually indicating the end of the main Support content. The footer links section spans the full width of the central column’s content area (roughly 80–90% of the column width) and is left-aligned with the rest of the content. A subtle horizontal divider line may appear above it with 16–24px of top and bottom padding to separate it from the main content. Within this area, a heading like 'More help' can appear at the top, followed by 2–4 horizontally arranged navigation links (e.g., 'View all docs', 'Contact support', 'Open playground') or a compact vertical list on narrow widths. Each link has 8–12px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal spacing between items, preserving the airy, minimalist style. This section visually signals completion and offers clear next steps without feeling cluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the central column. Scrolls with the page and defines the natural end of the Support content area, above any global site footer.
